solution:
We are asked to compute the carburizing (i.e., diffusion) time required for a specific nonsteady-state diffusion situation. It is first necessary to use equation (\frac{C_x-C_0}{C_s-C_0}=1-\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}\right)):

$$
\frac{C{x}-C{0}}{C{s}-C{0}}=1-\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}\right)
$$

wherein, $C{x}=0.30, C{0}=0.10, C{s}=0.90$, and $x=4 \mathrm{~mm}=4 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}$. Thus,

$$
\frac{C{x}-C{0}}{C{s}-C{0}}=\frac{0.30-0.10}{0.90-0.10}=0.2500=1-\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}\right)
$$

or

$$
\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}\right)=1-0.2500=0.7500
$$

By linear interpolation using data from  table 6.1 

| $z$ | $\operatorname{erf}(z)$ |
| :--: | :-- |
| 0.80 | 0.7421 |
| $z$ | 0.7500 |
| 0.85 | 0.7707 |

which leads to the following:

$$
\frac{z-0.800}{0.850-0.800}=\frac{0.7500-0.7421}{0.7707-0.7421}
$$

From which

$$
z=0.814=\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}
$$

Now, from  table 6.2 , data for the diffusion of carbon into $y$-iron are as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& D{0}=2.3 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s} \\
& Q{d}=148,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore at $1100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}(1373 \mathrm{~K})$

$$
\begin{gathered}
D=\left(2.3 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right) \exp \left[-\frac{148,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{(8.31 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}-\mathrm{K})(1373 \mathrm{~K})}\right] \\
=5.35 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}
\end{gathered}
$$

Thus,

$$
0.814=\frac{4 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}}{(2) \sqrt{\left(5.35 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right)(t)}}
$$

Solving for $t$ yields

$$
\begin{gathered}
t=\frac{\left(4 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)^{2}}{(2)^{2}(0.814)^{2}\left(5.35 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right)} \\
t=1.13 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~s}=31.3 \mathrm{~h}
\end{gathered}
$$

question:
An FCC iron-carbon alloy initially containing $0.55 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{C}$ is exposed to an oxygen-rich and virtually carbon-free atmosphere at $1325 \mathrm{~K}\left(1052^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$. Under these circumstances the carbon diffuses from the alloy and reacts at the surface with the oxygen in the atmosphere-that is, the carbon concentration at the surface position is maintained essentially at $0 \mathrm{wt} \%$ C. (This process of carbon depletion is termed decarburization.) At what position will the carbon concentration be $0.25 \mathrm{wt} \%$ after a 10-h treatment? The value of $D$ at 1325 K is $3.3 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$.

Reference:
"Table 6.1
Tabulation of Error Function Values
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline $z$ & erf(z) & $z$ & erf(z) & $z$ & $\operatorname{erf}(z)$ \\
\hline 0 & 0 & 0.55 & 0.5633 & 1.3 & 0.9340 \\
\hline 0.025 & 0.0282 & 0.60 & 0.6039 & 1.4 & 0.9523 \\
\hline 0.05 & 0.0564 & 0.65 & 0.6420 & 1.5 & 0.9661 \\
\hline 0.10 & 0.1125 & 0.70 & 0.6778 & 1.6 & 0.9763 \\
\hline 0.15 & 0.1680 & 0.75 & 0.7112 & 1.7 & 0.9838 \\
\hline 0.20 & 0.2227 & 0.80 & 0.7421 & 1.8 & 0.9891 \\
\hline 0.25 & 0.2763 & 0.85 & 0.7707 & 1.9 & 0.9928 \\
\hline 0.30 & 0.3286 & 0.90 & 0.7970 & 2.0 & 0.9953 \\
\hline 0.35 & 0.3794 & 0.95 & 0.8209 & 2.2 & 0.9981 \\
\hline 0.40 & 0.4284 & 1.0 & 0.8427 & 2.4 & 0.9993 \\
\hline 0.45 & 0.4755 & 1.1 & 0.8802 & 2.6 & 0.9998 \\
\hline 0.50 & 0.5205 & 1.2 & 0.9103 & 2.8 & 0.9999 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}"
solution:
This problem asks that we determine the position at which the carbon concentration is $0.25 \mathrm{wt} \%$ after a 10h heat treatment at 1325 K when $C{0}=0.55 \mathrm{wt} \%$ C. From equation (\frac{C_x-C_0}{C_s-C_0}=1-\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}\right))

$$
\frac{C{x}-C{0}}{C{s}-C{0}}=\frac{0.25-0.55}{0-0.55}=0.5455=1-\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}\right)
$$

Thus,

$$
\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{ } D t}\right)=0.4545
$$

Using data in  table 6.1  and linear interpolation

| $\boldsymbol{z}$ | $\operatorname{erf}(z)$ |
| :--: | :--: |
| 0.40 | 0.4284 |
| $z$ | 0.4545 |
| 0.45 | 0.4755 |

$$
\frac{z-0.40}{0.45-0.40}=\frac{0.4545-0.4284}{0.4755-0.4284}
$$

which leads to,

$$
$z=0.4277$
$$

This means that

$$
\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}=0.4277
$$

And, finally

$$
\begin{gathered}
x=2(0.4277) \sqrt{D t}=(0.8554) \sqrt{\left(3.3 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right)\left(3.6 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~s}\right)} \\
=9.32 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}=0.932 \mathrm{~mm}
\end{gathered}
$$

Note: This problem may also be solved using the "Diffusion" module in the VMSE software. Open the "Diffusion" module, click on the "Diffusion Design" submodule, and then do the following:

1. Enter the given data in left-hand window that appears. In the window below the label "D Value" enter the value of the diffusion coefficient—viz. "3.3e-11".
2. In the window just below the label "Initial, C0" enter the initial concentration—viz. " 0.55 ".
3. In the window the lies below "Surface, Cs" enter the surface concentration—viz. " 0 ".
4. Then in the "Diffusion Time t" window enter the time in seconds; in 10 h there are ( $60 \mathrm{~s} / \mathrm{min})(60$ $\mathrm{min} / \mathrm{h})(10 \mathrm{~h})=36,000 \mathrm{~s}$-so enter the value " 3.6 e 4 ".
5. Next, at the bottom of this window click on the button labeled "Add curve".
6. On the right portion of the screen will appear a concentration profile for this particular diffusion situation. A diamond-shaped cursor will appear at the upper left-hand corner of the resulting curve. Click and drag this cursor down the curve to the point at which the number below "Concentration:" reads " $0.25 \mathrm{wt} \%$ ". Then read the value under the "Distance:". For this problem, this value (the solution to the problem) is 0.91 mm .

question:
Nitrogen from a gaseous phase is to be diffused into pure iron at $675^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If the surface concentration is maintained at $0.2 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{~N}$, what will be the concentration 2 mm from the surface after 25 h ? The diffusion coefficient for nitrogen in iron at $675^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $2.8 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$.

Reference:
"Table 6.1
Tabulation of Error Function Values
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline $z$ & erf(z) & $z$ & erf(z) & $z$ & $\operatorname{erf}(z)$ \\
\hline 0 & 0 & 0.55 & 0.5633 & 1.3 & 0.9340 \\
\hline 0.025 & 0.0282 & 0.60 & 0.6039 & 1.4 & 0.9523 \\
\hline 0.05 & 0.0564 & 0.65 & 0.6420 & 1.5 & 0.9661 \\
\hline 0.10 & 0.1125 & 0.70 & 0.6778 & 1.6 & 0.9763 \\
\hline 0.15 & 0.1680 & 0.75 & 0.7112 & 1.7 & 0.9838 \\
\hline 0.20 & 0.2227 & 0.80 & 0.7421 & 1.8 & 0.9891 \\
\hline 0.25 & 0.2763 & 0.85 & 0.7707 & 1.9 & 0.9928 \\
\hline 0.30 & 0.3286 & 0.90 & 0.7970 & 2.0 & 0.9953 \\
\hline 0.35 & 0.3794 & 0.95 & 0.8209 & 2.2 & 0.9981 \\
\hline 0.40 & 0.4284 & 1.0 & 0.8427 & 2.4 & 0.9993 \\
\hline 0.45 & 0.4755 & 1.1 & 0.8802 & 2.6 & 0.9998 \\
\hline 0.50 & 0.5205 & 1.2 & 0.9103 & 2.8 & 0.9999 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}"
solution:
This problem asks us to compute the nitrogen concentration $\left(C{x}\right)$ at the 2 mm position after a $25-\mathrm{h}$ diffusion time, when diffusion is nonsteady-state. From equation (\frac{C_x-C_0}{C_s-C_0}=1-\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}\right))

$$
\begin{gathered}
\frac{C{x}-C{0}}{C{s}-C{0}}=\frac{C{x}-0}{0.2-0}=1-\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}\right) \\
=1-\operatorname{erf}\left[\frac{2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}}{(2) \sqrt{\left(2.8 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right)(25 \mathrm{~h})(3600 \mathrm{~s} / \mathrm{h})}}\right] \\
=1-\operatorname{erf}(0.630)
\end{gathered}
$$

Using data in  table 6.1  and linear interpolation

| $z$ | $\operatorname{erf}(z)$ |
| :--: | :--: |
| 0.600 | 0.6039 |
| 0.630 | $y$ |
| 0.650 | 0.6420 |

$$
\frac{0.630-0.600}{0.650-0.600}=\frac{y-0.6039}{0.6420-0.6039}
$$

from which

$$
y=\operatorname{erf}(0.630)=0.6268
$$

Thus,

$$
\frac{C{x}-0}{0.2-0}=1.0-0.6268
$$

And solving for $C{\mathrm{z}}$ gives

$$
C{\mathrm{z}}=0.075 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{~N}
$$

question:
Consider a diffusion couple composed of two semi-infinite solids of the same metal and that each side of the diffusion couple has a different concentration of the same elemental impurity; furthermore, assume each impurity level is constant throughout its side of the diffusion couple. For this situation, the solution to Fick's second law (assuming that the diffusion coefficient for the impurity is independent of concentration) is as follows:

$$
C{x}=C{2}+\left(\frac{C{1}-C{2}}{2}\right)\left[1-\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}\right)\right]
$$

The schematic diffusion profile in  figure 6.13  shows these concentration parameters as well as concentration profiles at times $t=0$ and $t>0$. Please note that at $t=0$, the $x=0$ position is taken as the initial diffusion couple interface, whereas $C{1}$ is the impurity concentration for $x<0$, and $C{2}$ is the impurity content for $x>0$.

Consider a diffusion couple composed of pure nickel and a $55 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Ni}-45 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Cu}$ alloy (similar to the couple shown in  figure 6.1 ). Determine the time this diffusion couple must be heated at $1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}(1273 \mathrm{~K})$ in order to achieve a composition of $56.5 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Ni}$ a distance of $15 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ into the $\mathrm{Ni}-\mathrm{Cu}$ alloy referenced to the original interface. Values for the preexponential and activation energy for this diffusion system are $2.3 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$ and $252,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}$, respectively.

Reference:
"Table 6.1
Tabulation of Error Function Values
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline $z$ & erf(z) & $z$ & erf(z) & $z$ & $\operatorname{erf}(z)$ \\
\hline 0 & 0 & 0.55 & 0.5633 & 1.3 & 0.9340 \\
\hline 0.025 & 0.0282 & 0.60 & 0.6039 & 1.4 & 0.9523 \\
\hline 0.05 & 0.0564 & 0.65 & 0.6420 & 1.5 & 0.9661 \\
\hline 0.10 & 0.1125 & 0.70 & 0.6778 & 1.6 & 0.9763 \\
\hline 0.15 & 0.1680 & 0.75 & 0.7112 & 1.7 & 0.9838 \\
\hline 0.20 & 0.2227 & 0.80 & 0.7421 & 1.8 & 0.9891 \\
\hline 0.25 & 0.2763 & 0.85 & 0.7707 & 1.9 & 0.9928 \\
\hline 0.30 & 0.3286 & 0.90 & 0.7970 & 2.0 & 0.9953 \\
\hline 0.35 & 0.3794 & 0.95 & 0.8209 & 2.2 & 0.9981 \\
\hline 0.40 & 0.4284 & 1.0 & 0.8427 & 2.4 & 0.9993 \\
\hline 0.45 & 0.4755 & 1.1 & 0.8802 & 2.6 & 0.9998 \\
\hline 0.50 & 0.5205 & 1.2 & 0.9103 & 2.8 & 0.9999 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}"
solution:
This problem calls for us to determine the value of time $t$ in equation (C_x=C_2+\left(\frac{C_1-C_2}{2}\right)\left[1-\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}\right)\right]) given the following values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& C{1}=100 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Ni} \\
& C{2}=55 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Ni} \\
& C{x}=56.5 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Ni} \\
& T=1273 \mathrm{~K} \\
& x=15 \mu \mathrm{~m}=15 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m} \\
& D{0}=2.3 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s} \\
& Q{d}=252,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

Let us first of all compute the value of the diffusion coefficient $D$ using equation (D=D_0 \exp \left(-\frac{Q_d}{R T}\right)):

$$
D=D{0} \exp \left(-\frac{Q{d}}{R T}\right)
$$

$$
\begin{gathered}
=\left(2.3 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right) \exp \left[-\frac{252,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{(8.31 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}+\mathrm{K})(1273 \mathrm{~K})}\right] \\
=1.04 \times 10^{-14} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}
\end{gathered}
$$

Now incorporating values for all of the parameters except $t$ into equation (C_x=C_2+\left(\frac{C_1-C_2}{2}\right)\left[1-\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}\right)\right]) leads to

$$
56.5 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Ni}=55 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Ni}+\left(\frac{100 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Ni}-55 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Cu}}{2}\right)\left[1-\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{15 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}}{2 \sqrt{\left(1.04 \times 10^{-14} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right)(\mathrm{t})}}\right)\right]
$$

This expression reduces to the following:

$$
0.9333=\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{73.54 \mathrm{~s}^{\frac{1}{2}}}{\sqrt{t}}\right)
$$

From  table 6.1  to determine the value of $z$ when $\operatorname{erf}(z)=0.9333$ an interpolation procedure is necessary as follows:

| $z$ | $\operatorname{erf}(z)$ |
| :-- | :-- |
| 1.2 | 0.9103 |
| $z$ | 0.9333 |
| 1.3 | 0.9340 |

$$
\frac{z-1.2}{1.3-1.2}=\frac{0.9333-0.9103}{0.9340-0.9103}
$$

And solving for $z$ we have

$$
$z=1.297$
$$

Which means that

$$
\frac{73.54 \mathrm{~s}^{\frac{1}{2}}}{\sqrt[3]{t}}=1.297
$$

Or that

$$
t=\left(\frac{73.54}{1.297} \mathrm{~s}^{\frac{1}{2}}\right)^{2}
$$

$=3215 \mathrm{~s}=53.6 \mathrm{~min}=0.89 \mathrm{~h}$

question:
Consider a diffusion couple composed of two cobalt-iron alloys; one has a composition of $75 \mathrm{wt} \%$ Co-25 wt $\%$ Fe; the other alloy composition is $50 \mathrm{wt} \%$ Co-50 wt $\%$ Fe. If this couple is heated to a temperature of $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ (1073 K) for $20,000 \mathrm{~s}$, determine how far from the original interface into the $50 \mathrm{wt} \%$ Co-50 wt $\%$ Fe alloy the composition has increased to $52 \mathrm{wt} \%$ Co-48 wt $\%$ Fe. For the diffusion coefficient, assume values of $6.6 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$ and $247,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}$, respectively, for the preexponential and activation energy.

Reference:
"Table 6.1
Tabulation of Error Function Values
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline $z$ & erf(z) & $z$ & erf(z) & $z$ & $\operatorname{erf}(z)$ \\
\hline 0 & 0 & 0.55 & 0.5633 & 1.3 & 0.9340 \\
\hline 0.025 & 0.0282 & 0.60 & 0.6039 & 1.4 & 0.9523 \\
\hline 0.05 & 0.0564 & 0.65 & 0.6420 & 1.5 & 0.9661 \\
\hline 0.10 & 0.1125 & 0.70 & 0.6778 & 1.6 & 0.9763 \\
\hline 0.15 & 0.1680 & 0.75 & 0.7112 & 1.7 & 0.9838 \\
\hline 0.20 & 0.2227 & 0.80 & 0.7421 & 1.8 & 0.9891 \\
\hline 0.25 & 0.2763 & 0.85 & 0.7707 & 1.9 & 0.9928 \\
\hline 0.30 & 0.3286 & 0.90 & 0.7970 & 2.0 & 0.9953 \\
\hline 0.35 & 0.3794 & 0.95 & 0.8209 & 2.2 & 0.9981 \\
\hline 0.40 & 0.4284 & 1.0 & 0.8427 & 2.4 & 0.9993 \\
\hline 0.45 & 0.4755 & 1.1 & 0.8802 & 2.6 & 0.9998 \\
\hline 0.50 & 0.5205 & 1.2 & 0.9103 & 2.8 & 0.9999 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}"
solution:
This problem calls for us to determine the value of the distance, $x$, in equation (C_x=C_2+\left(\frac{C_1-C_2}{2}\right)\left[1-\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}\right)\right]) given the following values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& C{1}=75 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Co} \\
& C{2}=50 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Co} \\
& C{x}=52 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Co} \\
& T=1073 \mathrm{~K} \\
& t=20,000 \mathrm{~s} \\
& D{0}=6.6 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s} \\
& Q{d}=247,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

Let us first of all compute the value of the diffusion coefficient $D$ using equation (D=D_0 \exp \left(-\frac{Q_d}{R T}\right)):

$$
\begin{gathered}
D=D{0} \exp \left(-\frac{Q{d}}{R T}\right) \\
=\left(6.6 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right) \exp \left[-\frac{247,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{(8.31 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol} \cdot \mathrm{~K})(1073 \mathrm{~K})}\right] \\
=6.15 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}
\end{gathered}
$$

Now incorporating values for all of the parameters except $x$ into equation (C_x=C_2+\left(\frac{C_1-C_2}{2}\right)\left[1-\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}\right)\right]) leads to

$$
52 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Co}=50 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Co}+\left(\frac{75 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Co}-50 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Co}}{2}\right)\left[1-\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{\left(6.15 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right)(20,000 \mathrm{~s})}}\right)\right]
$$

This expression reduces to the following:

$$
0.8400=\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{7.01 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}}\right)
$$

From  table 6.1  to determine the value of $z$ when $\operatorname{erf}(z)=0.8400$ an interpolation procedure is necessary as follows:

| $z$ | $\operatorname{erf}(z)$ |
| :-- | :-- |
| 0.95 | 0.8209 |
| $z$ | 0.8400 |
| 1.0 | 0.8427 |

$$
\frac{z-0.95}{1.0-0.95}=\frac{0.8400-0.8209}{0.8427-0.8209}
$$

And solving for $z$ we have

$$
$z=0.994$
$$

Which means that

$$
\frac{x}{7.01 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}}=0.994
$$

Or that

$$
\begin{aligned}
& x=\left(7.01 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}\right)(0.994) \\
& =6.97 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}=0.697 \mu \mathrm{~m}
\end{aligned}
$$

question:
Consider a diffusion couple between silver and a gold alloy that contains $10 \mathrm{wt} \%$ silver. This couple is heat treated at an elevated temperature and it was found that after 850 s the concentration of silver had increased to $12 \mathrm{wt} \%$ at $10 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ from the interface into the Ag-Au alloy. Assuming preexponential and activation energy values of $7.2 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$ and $168,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}$, compute the temperature of this heat treatment. (Note: you may find  figure 6.13  and equation (C_x=C_2+\left(\frac{C_1-C_2}{2}\right)\left[1-\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}\right)\right]) helpful.)

Reference:
"Table 6.1
Tabulation of Error Function Values
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline $z$ & erf(z) & $z$ & erf(z) & $z$ & $\operatorname{erf}(z)$ \\
\hline 0 & 0 & 0.55 & 0.5633 & 1.3 & 0.9340 \\
\hline 0.025 & 0.0282 & 0.60 & 0.6039 & 1.4 & 0.9523 \\
\hline 0.05 & 0.0564 & 0.65 & 0.6420 & 1.5 & 0.9661 \\
\hline 0.10 & 0.1125 & 0.70 & 0.6778 & 1.6 & 0.9763 \\
\hline 0.15 & 0.1680 & 0.75 & 0.7112 & 1.7 & 0.9838 \\
\hline 0.20 & 0.2227 & 0.80 & 0.7421 & 1.8 & 0.9891 \\
\hline 0.25 & 0.2763 & 0.85 & 0.7707 & 1.9 & 0.9928 \\
\hline 0.30 & 0.3286 & 0.90 & 0.7970 & 2.0 & 0.9953 \\
\hline 0.35 & 0.3794 & 0.95 & 0.8209 & 2.2 & 0.9981 \\
\hline 0.40 & 0.4284 & 1.0 & 0.8427 & 2.4 & 0.9993 \\
\hline 0.45 & 0.4755 & 1.1 & 0.8802 & 2.6 & 0.9998 \\
\hline 0.50 & 0.5205 & 1.2 & 0.9103 & 2.8 & 0.9999 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}"
solution:
This problem calls for us to determine the value of time $t$ in equation (C_x=C_2+\left(\frac{C_1-C_2}{2}\right)\left[1-\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}\right)\right]) given the following values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& C{1}=100 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Ag} \\
& C{2}=10 \mathrm{wt} \mathrm{Ag} \\
& C{x}=12 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Ag} \\
& t=850 \mathrm{~s} \\
& x=10 \mu \mathrm{~m}=10 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m} \\
& D{0}=7.2 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s} \\
& Q{d}=168,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

We first of all substitute values for all of the above parameters-except for $D{0}$ and $Q{d}$. This leads to the following expressions:

$$
\begin{gathered}
C{x}=C{2}+\left(\frac{C{1}-C{2}}{2}\right)\left[1-\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}\right)\right] \\
12 \mathrm{wt} \%=10 \mathrm{wt} \%+\left(\frac{100 \mathrm{wt} \%-10 \mathrm{wt} \%}{2}\right)\left[1-\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{10 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}}{2 \sqrt{D(850 \mathrm{~s})}}\right)\right]
\end{gathered}
$$

This equation reduces to the following:

$$
0.9556=\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{1.715 \times 10^{-7}}{\sqrt{D}}\right)
$$

Or

$$
0.9556=\operatorname{erf}(z)
$$

It is now necessary to determine using the data in  table 6.1  the value of $z$ for which the error function has a value of 0.9556 . This requires an interpolation using the following data:

| 1.4 | 0.9523 |
| :-- | :-- |
| $z$ | 0.9556 |
| 1.5 | 0.9661 |

The interpolation equation is

$$
\frac{1.5-z}{1.5-1.4}=\frac{0.9661-0.9556}{0.9661-0.9523}
$$

Solving for $z$ from this equation gives

$$
$z=1.424$
$$

Incorporating this value into the error function argument of equation (0.9556=\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{1.715 \times 10^{-7}}{\sqrt{D}}\right)) (above) leads to the following:

$$
1.424=\frac{1.715 \times 10^{-7}}{\sqrt{D}}
$$

from which $D$ is equal to

$$
D=1.45 \times 10^{-14} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}
$$

We may now compute the temperature at which the diffusion coefficient has this value using equation (D=D_0 \exp \left(-\frac{Q_d}{R T}\right)). Taking natural logarithms of both sides of equation (D=D_0 \exp \left(-\frac{Q_d}{R T}\right)) leads to

$$
\ln D-\ln D{0}-\frac{Q{d}}{R T}
$$

And, solving for $T$

$$
T=-\frac{Q{d}}{R\left(\ln D-\ln D{0}\right)}
$$

Into which we insert values of $D, D{0}$, and $Q{d}$ as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
T=-\frac{168,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{m}}{(8.31 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol} \cdot \mathrm{~K})\left[\ln \left(1.45 \times 10^{-14} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right)-\ln \left(7.2 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right)\right]} \\
=1010 \mathrm{~K}\left(737^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)
\end{gathered}
$$

question: For a steel alloy it has been determined that a carburizing heat treatment of 15 h duration will raise the carbon concentration to $0.35 \mathrm{wt} \%$ at a point 2.0 mm from the surface. Estimate the time necessary to achieve the same concentration at a $6.0-\mathrm{mm}$ position for an identical steel and at the same carburizing temperature.
solution:
This problem calls for an estimate of the time necessary to achieve a carbon concentration of $0.35 \mathrm{wt} \%$ at a point 6.0 mm from the surface. From equation (\frac{x^2}{D t}=\mathrm{constant}),

$$
\frac{x^{2}}{D t}=\text { constant }
$$

But since the temperature is constant, so also is $D$ constant, which means that

$$
\frac{x^{2}}{t}=\text { constant }
$$

or

$$
\frac{x{1}^{2}}{t{1}}=\frac{x{2}^{2}}{t{2}}
$$

Thus, if we assign $x{1}=0.2 \mathrm{~mm}, x{2}=0.6 \mathrm{~mm}$, and $t{1} 15 \mathrm{~h}$, then

$$
\frac{(2.0 \mathrm{~mm})^{2}}{15 \mathrm{~h}}=\frac{(6.0 \mathrm{~mm})^{2}}{t{2}}
$$

from which

$$
t{2}=135 \mathrm{~h}
$$

solution:
We are asked to calculate the temperature at which the diffusion coefficient for the diffusion of Zn in Cu has a value of $2.6 \times 10^{-16} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$. Solving for $T$ from equation (\ln D=\ln D_0-\frac{Q_d}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T}\right)) leads to

$$
T=-\frac{Q{d}}{R\left(\ln D-\ln D{0}\right)}
$$

and using the data from  table 6.2  for the diffusion of Zn in Cu (i.e., $D{0}=2.4 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$ and $Q{d}=189,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}$ ), we get

$$
\begin{gathered}
T=-\frac{189,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{(8.31 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}-\mathrm{K})\left[\ln \left(2.6 \times 10^{-16} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right)-\ln \left(2.4 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right)\right]} \\
=901 \mathrm{~K}=628^{\circ} \mathrm{C}
\end{gathered}
$$

Note: This problem may also be solved using the "Diffusion" module in the VMSE software. Open the "Diffusion" module, click on the "D vs \frac{1}{T} Plot" submodule, and then do the following:

Next just below the "Qd" the activation energy value (in $\mathrm{KJ} / \mathrm{mol}$ ), " 189 " is displayed. In the "Temperature Range" window minimum ("T Min") and maximum ("T Max") temperatures are displayed, in both ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and K . The default values for minimum and maximum temperatures are $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $1140^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, respectively.
3. Next, at the bottom of this window, click the "Add Curve" button.
4. A $\log \mathrm{D}$ versus $1 / \mathrm{T}$ plot then appears, with a line for the temperature dependence of the diffusion coefficient for Zn in Cu . At the top of this curve is a diamond-shaped cursor. We want to click-and-drag this cursor down the line (to the right) to the point at which the entry under the "Diff Coeff (D):" label reads $2.6 \times 10^{-16} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$. As we drag the cursor to the right, the value of the diffusion coefficient continues to decrease, but only to about 1.5 $\times 10^{-14} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$. This means that the temperature at which the diffusion coefficient is $2.6 \times 10^{-16} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$ is lower than

$800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Therefore, let us change the T Min value to $600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. After this change is made click again on the "Add Curve", and a new curve appears. Now click and drag the cursor to the right until " $2.6 \times 10^{-16} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$ " is displayed under "Diff Coeff (D)". At this time under the "Temperature (T)" label is displayed the temperature-in this case "902 K".

We are asked to calculate the temperature at which the diffusion coefficient for the diffusion of Cr in Ni has a value of $1.2 \times 10^{-14} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$. Solving for $T$ from equation (\ln D=\ln D_0-\frac{Q_d}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T}\right)) leads to

$$
T=-\frac{Q{d}}{R\left(\ln D-\ln D{0}\right)}
$$

and using the data the problem statement (i.e., $D{0}=1.1 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$ and $Q{d}=272,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}$ ), we get

$$
\begin{gathered}
T=-\frac{272,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{\left(8.31 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}-\mathrm{K}\right)\left[\ln \left(1.2 \times 10^{-14} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right)-\ln \left(1.1 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right)\right]} \\
=1427 \mathrm{~K}=1154^{\circ} \mathrm{C}

$$

question: The activation energy for the diffusion of copper in silver is 193,000 J/mol. Calculate the diffusion coefficient at $1200 \mathrm{~K}\left(927^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$, given that $D$ at $1000 \mathrm{~K}\left(727^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ is $1.0 \times 10^{-14} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$.
solution:
To solve this problem it first becomes necessary to solve for $D{0}$ from a rearranged form of equation (D=D_0 \exp \left(-\frac{Q_d}{R T}\right)) and using 1000 K diffusion data:

$$
\begin{gathered}
D{0}=D \exp \left(\frac{Q{d}}{R T}\right) \\
=\left(1.0 \times 10^{-14} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right) \exp \left[\frac{193,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{(8.31 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}-\mathrm{K})(1000 \mathrm{~K})}\right] \\
1.22 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}
\end{gathered}
$$

Now, solving for $D$ at 1200 K (using equation (D=D_0 \exp \left(-\frac{Q_d}{R T}\right))) gives

$$
D=\left(1.22 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right) \exp \left[-\frac{193,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{(8.31 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}-\mathrm{K})(1200 \mathrm{~K})}\right]
$$

Give your value as a tuple (activation energy, preexponential).

Carbon is allowed to diffuse through a steel plate 10 mm thick.  The concentrations of carbon at the two faces are 0.85 and 0.40 kg C/m³ Fe (maintained constant).  If the diffusion pre-exponential factor and activation energy are D₀ = 5.0×10⁻⁷ m²/s and Q = 77 000 J/mol, respectively, compute the temperature at which the diffusion flux is J = 6.3×10⁻¹⁰ kg/(m²·s).
solution:
This problem asks that we compute the temperature at which the diffusion flux is $6.3 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{2}-\mathrm{s}$. Combining equation ((J=-D \frac{d C}{d x}) and (D=D_0 \exp \left(-\frac{Q_d}{R T}\right))) yields

$$
\begin{gathered}
J=-D \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta x} \\
=-D{0} \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta x} \exp \left(-\frac{Q{d}}{R T}\right)
\end{gathered}
$$

Taking natural logarithms of both sides of this equation yields the following expression:

$$
\ln J=\ln \left(-D{0} \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta x}\right)-\frac{Q{d}}{R T}
$$

Or, upon rearrangement

$$
\frac{Q{d}}{R T}=\ln \left(-D{0} \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta x}\right)-\ln J=\ln \left(-\frac{D{0} \Delta C}{J \Delta x}\right)
$$

And solving for $T$ from this expression leads to

$$
T=\left(\frac{Q{d}}{R}\right) \frac{1}{\ln \left(-\frac{D{0} \Delta C}{J \Delta x}\right)}
$$

Now, incorporating values of the parameters in this equation provided in the problem statement leads to the following:

$$
\begin{gathered}
T=\left(\frac{77,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{8.31 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}-\mathrm{K}}\right) \frac{1}{\ln \left[-\frac{\left(5.0 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right)\left(0.40 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}-0.85 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}\right)}{\left(6.3 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{2}-\mathrm{s}\right)\left(10 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)}\right]} \\
=884 \mathrm{~K}=611^{\circ} \mathrm{C}
\end{gathered}
$$

question: The steady-state diffusion flux through a metal plate is $7.8 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{2} \cdot \mathrm{~s}$ at a temperature of $1200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}(1473$ K) and when the concentration gradient is $-500 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{4}$. Calculate the diffusion flux at $1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}(1273 \mathrm{~K})$ for the same concentration gradient and assuming an activation energy for diffusion of $145,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}$.
solution:
In order to solve this problem, we must first compute the value of $D{0}$ from the data given at $1200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}(1473$ K ); this requires the combining of both equation ((J=-D \frac{d C}{d x}) and (D=D_0 \exp \left(-\frac{Q_d}{R T}\right))) as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
J=-D \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta x} \\
=-D{0} \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta x} \exp \left(-\frac{Q{d}}{R T}\right)
\end{gathered}
$$

Solving for $D{0}$ from the above expression gives

$$
D{0}=-\frac{J}{\frac{\Delta C}{\Delta x}} \exp \left(\frac{Q{d}}{R T}\right)
$$

And incorporating values of the parameters in this expression provided in the problem statement yields the following:

$$
\begin{gathered}
D{0}=-\left(\frac{7.8 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{2}-\mathrm{s}}{-500 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{4}}\right) \exp \left[\frac{145,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{(8.31 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}-\mathrm{K})(1200+273 \mathrm{~K})}\right] \\
=2.18 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}
\end{gathered}
$$

The value of the diffusion flux at 1273 K may be computed using equation (\begin{aligned}
&J=-D \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta x}\\
&=-D_0 \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta x} \exp \left(-\frac{Q_d}{R T}\right)
\end{aligned}) (above) as follows:

$$
J=-D{0}\left(\frac{\Delta C}{\Delta x}\right) \exp \left(-\frac{Q{d}}{R T}\right)
$$

$$
\begin{gathered}
=-\left(2.18 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right)\left(-500 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{4}\right) \exp \left[-\frac{145,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{(8.31 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}-\mathrm{K})(1273 \mathrm{~K})}\right] \\
=1.21 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{2}-\mathrm{s}
\end{gathered}
$$

A copper-nickel diffusion couple similar to that shown in  figure 6.1a  is fashioned. After a 500-h heat treatment at $1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}(1273 \mathrm{~K})$ the concentration of Ni is $3.0 \mathrm{wt} \%$ at the $1.0-\mathrm{mm}$ position within the copper. At what temperature should the diffusion couple be heated to produce this same concentration (i.e., $3.0 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Ni}$ ) at a $2.0-\mathrm{mm}$ position after 500 h ? The preexponential and activation energy for the diffusion of Ni in Cu are $1.9 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$ and $230,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}$, respectively.

A diffusion couple similar to that shown in  figure 6.1a  is prepared using two hypothetical metals $A$ and $B$. After a 20-h heat treatment at $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ (and subsequently cooling to room temperature) the concentration of $B$ in $A$ is $2.5 \mathrm{wt} \%$ at the $5.0-\mathrm{mm}$ position within metal $A$. If another heat treatment is conducted on an identical diffusion couple, but at $1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for 20 h , at what position will the composition be $2.5 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{~B}$ ? Assume that the preexponential and activation energy for the diffusion coefficient are $1.5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$ and $125,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}$, respectively.

Consider the diffusion of some hypothetical metal $Y$ into another hypothetical metal $Z$ at $950^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$; after 10 h the concentration at the 0.5 mm position (in metal $Z$ ) is $2.0 \mathrm{wt} \% Y$. At what position will the concentration also be $2.0 \mathrm{wt} \% Y$ after a 17.5 h heat treatment again at $950^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ? Assume preexponential and activation energy values of 4.3 $\times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$ and $180,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}$, respectively, for this diffusion system.

A diffusion couple is prepared using two hypothetical metals $R$ and $S$. After a 2.5-h heat treatment at $750^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ the concentration of $R$ is $4 \mathrm{at} \%$ at the $4-\mathrm{mm}$ position within $S$. Another heat treatment is conducted on an identical diffusion couple at $900^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and the time required to produce this same diffusion result (viz., $4 \mathrm{at} \% R$ at the $4-\mathrm{mm}$ position within $S$ ) is 0.4 h . If it is known that the diffusion coefficient at $750^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $2.6 \times 10^{-17} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$ determine the activation energy for the diffusion of $R$ in $S$.
solution:
In order to determine the activation energy for this diffusion system it is first necessary to compute the diffusion coefficient at $900^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Inasmuch as $x$ is the same for both heat treatments, it is possible to use equation (D t=\text { constant }) to solve this problem-i.e.,

$$
D t=\text { constant }
$$

Or that

$$
$D{750} t{750}=D{900} t{900}$
$$

It is possible to solve for $D{900}$ inasmuch as values for the other parameters are provided in the problem statement; i.e.,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& t{750}=2.5 \mathrm{~h} \\
& D{750}=2.6 \times 10^{-17} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s} \\
& t{900}=0.4 \mathrm{~h}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $D{900}$ from the above equation leads to

$$
\begin{gathered}
D{900}=\frac{D{750} t{750}}{t{900}} \\
=\frac{\left(2.6 \times 10^{-17} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right)(2.5 \mathrm{~h})}{0.4 \mathrm{~h}} \\
1.6 \times 10^{-16} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}
\end{gathered}
$$

Inasmuch as we know the values of $D$ at both $750^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $900^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$-viz.,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& D{750}=2.6 \times 10^{-17} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s} \\
& D{900}=1.6 \times 10^{-16} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

it is possible to solve for the activation energy $Q{d}$ using the following equation:

$$
Q{d}=-2.3 R\left[\frac{\frac{\log D{1}-\log D{2}}{\frac{1}{T{1}}-\frac{1}{T{2}}}}{T{1}}\right]
$$

In this expression, if we assign

$$
\begin{aligned}
& T{1}=750^{\circ} \mathrm{C}+273 \mathrm{~K}=1023 \mathrm{~K} \\
& T{2}=900^{\circ} \mathrm{C}+273 \mathrm{~K}=1173 \mathrm{~K} \\
& D{750}=D{i}=2.6 \times 10^{-17} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s} \\
& D{900}=D{2} 1.6 \times 10^{-16} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

then, the activation energy is determined using the above equation and these values as follows:

$$
Q{d}=-(2.3)(8.31 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol} \cdot \mathrm{~K})\left[\frac{\frac{\log \left(2.6 \times 10^{-17} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right)-\log \left(1.6 \times 10^{-16} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right)}{1}}{\frac{1}{(1023 \mathrm{~K})}-\frac{1}{(1173 \mathrm{~K})}}\right] = 120,700 J/mol
$$

$$
D t=\text { constant }
$$

since $x$ is a constant $(0.5 \mathrm{~mm})$. This means that

$$
$D{600} t{600}=D{900} t{900}$
$$

In addition, it is necessary to compute values for both $D{600}$ and $D{900}$ using equation (D=D_0 \exp \left(-\frac{Q_d}{R T}\right)). From  table 6.2 , for the diffusion of C in $\alpha-\mathrm{Fe}, Q{d}=87,400 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}$ and $D{0}=1.1 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$. Therefore, we calculate values for $D{600}$ and $D{900}$ as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
D{600}=\left(1.1 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right) \exp \left[-\frac{87,400 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{(8.31 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}-\mathrm{K})(600+273 \mathrm{~K})}\right] \\
6.45 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s} \\
D{900}=\left(1.1 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right) \exp \left[-\frac{87,400 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{(8.31 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}-\mathrm{K})(900+273 \mathrm{~K})}\right] \\
1.40 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}
\end{gathered}

=\frac{\left(6.45 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right)(100 \mathrm{~min})}{1.40 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}} \\
=4.61 \mathrm{~min}
\end{gathered}
$$

solution:
This problem asks us to compute the temperature at which a nonsteady-state 48 h diffusion anneal was carried out in order to give a carbon concentration of $0.30 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{C}$ in FCC Fe at a position 3.5 mm below the surface. We begin by using equation (\frac{C_x-C_0}{C_s-C_0}=1-\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}\right)) as follows:

$$
\frac{C{x}-C{0}}{C{x}-C{0}}=\frac{0.30-0.10}{1.10-0.10}=0.2000=1-\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}\right)
$$

Or

$$
\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}\right)=0.8000
$$

Now it becomes necessary, using the data in  table 6.1  and linear interpolation, to determine the value of $\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}$. Thus

| $\boldsymbol{z}$ | $\operatorname{erf}(\boldsymbol{z})$ |
| :--: | :--: |
| 0.90 | 0.7970 |
| $y$ | 0.8000 |
| 0.95 | 0.8209 |

$$
\frac{y-0.90}{0.95-0.90}=\frac{0.8000-0.7970}{0.8209-0.7970}
$$

From which

$$
$y=0.9063$
$$

Thus,

$$
\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}=0.9063
$$

And since $t=48 \mathrm{~h}(172,800 \mathrm{~s})$ and $x=3.5 \mathrm{~mm}\left(3.5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)$, solving for $D$ from the above equation yields

$$
\begin{gathered}
D=\frac{x^{2}}{(4 t)(0.9063)^{2}} \\
=\frac{\left(3.5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)^{2}}{(4)(172,800 \mathrm{~s})(0.821)}=2.16 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}
\end{gathered}
$$

Now, in order to determine the temperature at which $D$ has the above value, we must employ equation (\ln D=\ln D_0-\frac{Q_d}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T}\right)); solving this equation for $T$ yields

$$
T=\frac{Q{d}}{R\left(\ln D{0}-\ln D\right)}
$$

From  table 6.2 , $D{0}$ and $Q{d}$ for the diffusion of C in FCC Fe are $2.3 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$ and $148,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}$, respectively. Therefore

$$
\begin{gathered}
T=\frac{148,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{(8.31 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}-\mathrm{K})\left[\ln \left(2.3 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right)-\ln \left(2.16 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right)\right]} \\
=1283 \mathrm{~K}=1010^{\circ} \mathrm{C}
\end{gathered}
$$

question:
For the predeposition heat treatment of a semiconducting device, gallium atoms are to be diffused into silicon at a temperature of $1150^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for 2.5 h . If the required concentration of Ga at a position $2 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ below the surface is $8 \times 10^{23}$ atoms $/ \mathrm{m}^{3}$, compute the required surface concentration of Ga. Assume the following:
(i) The surface concentration remains constant
(ii) The background concentration is $2 \times 10^{19} \mathrm{Ga}$ atoms $/ \mathrm{m}^{3}$
(iii) Preexponential and activation energy values are $3.74 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$ and $3.39 \mathrm{eV} /$ atom, respectively.

Reference:
"Table 6.1
Tabulation of Error Function Values
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline $z$ & erf(z) & $z$ & erf(z) & $z$ & $\operatorname{erf}(z)$ \\
\hline 0 & 0 & 0.55 & 0.5633 & 1.3 & 0.9340 \\
\hline 0.025 & 0.0282 & 0.60 & 0.6039 & 1.4 & 0.9523 \\
\hline 0.05 & 0.0564 & 0.65 & 0.6420 & 1.5 & 0.9661 \\
\hline 0.10 & 0.1125 & 0.70 & 0.6778 & 1.6 & 0.9763 \\
\hline 0.15 & 0.1680 & 0.75 & 0.7112 & 1.7 & 0.9838 \\
\hline 0.20 & 0.2227 & 0.80 & 0.7421 & 1.8 & 0.9891 \\
\hline 0.25 & 0.2763 & 0.85 & 0.7707 & 1.9 & 0.9928 \\
\hline 0.30 & 0.3286 & 0.90 & 0.7970 & 2.0 & 0.9953 \\
\hline 0.35 & 0.3794 & 0.95 & 0.8209 & 2.2 & 0.9981 \\
\hline 0.40 & 0.4284 & 1.0 & 0.8427 & 2.4 & 0.9993 \\
\hline 0.45 & 0.4755 & 1.1 & 0.8802 & 2.6 & 0.9998 \\
\hline 0.50 & 0.5205 & 1.2 & 0.9103 & 2.8 & 0.9999 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}"
solution:
This problem requires that we solve for the surface concentration, $C{s}$ of equation (\frac{C_x-C_0}{C_s-C_0}=1-\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}\right)) given the following:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& C{x}=8 \times 10^{23} \text { atoms } / \mathrm{m}^{3} \\
& C{0}=2 \times 10^{19} \text { atoms } / \mathrm{m}^{3} \\
& x=2 \mu \mathrm{~m}=2 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m} \\
& T=1150^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \\
& t=2.5 \mathrm{~h}=9000 \mathrm{~s} \\
& D{0}=3.74 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s} \\
& Q{d}=3.39 \mathrm{eV} / \text { atom }
\end{aligned}
$$

equation (\frac{C_x-C_0}{C_s-C_0}=1-\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}\right)) calls for a value of the diffusion coefficient $D$, which is determined at $1150^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ using equation (D=D_0 \exp \left(-\frac{Q_d}{R T}\right)) as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
D=D{0} \exp \left(-\frac{Q{d}}{R T}\right) \\
=\left(3.74 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right) \exp \left(-\frac{3.39 \mathrm{eV} / \text { atom }}{\left(8.62 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{eV} / \text { atom } \cdot \mathrm{K}\right)\left(1150^{\circ} \mathrm{C}+273 \mathrm{~K}\right)}\right) \\
=3.72 \times 10^{-17} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}
\end{gathered}
$$

Now it is required that we solve for $C{s}$ in equation (\frac{C_x-C_0}{C_s-C_0}=1-\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}\right)):

$$
\frac{C{x}-C{0}}{C{s}-C{0}}=1-\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}\right)
$$

And solving for $C{s}$ from this expression and incorporation of values for all of the other parameters leads to

$$
\begin{gathered}
C{s}=C{0}+\frac{C{x}-C{0}}{1-\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}\right)} \\
=\left(2 \times 10^{19} \text { atoms } / \mathrm{m}^{3}\right)+\frac{\left(8 \times 10^{23} \text { atoms } / \mathrm{m}^{3}\right)-\left(2 \times 10^{19} \text { atoms } / \mathrm{m}^{3}\right)}{1-\operatorname{erf}\left[\frac{2 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}}{2 \sqrt{\left(3.72 \times 10^{-17} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right)(9000 \mathrm{~s})}}\right]} \\
=\left(2 \times 10^{19} \text { atoms } / \mathrm{m}^{3}\right)+\frac{\left(8 \times 10^{23} \text { atoms } / \mathrm{m}^{3}\right)}{1-\operatorname{erf}(1.7283)}
\end{gathered}
$$

At this point it becomes necessary to conduct an interpolation-we know the $z$ and need to determine the value of $\operatorname{erf}(z)$. This interpolation is conducted using data found in  table 6.1  as follows:

| $\operatorname{erf}(z)$ | $z$ |
| :--: | :--: |
| 0.9838 | 1.7 |
| $\operatorname{erf}(z)$ | 1.7283 |
| 0.9891 | 1.8 |

$$
\frac{1.8000-1.7283}{1.8000-1.7000}=\frac{0.9891-\operatorname{erf}(z)}{0.9891-0.9838}
$$

And solving this expression for $\operatorname{erf}(z)$ leads to

$$
\operatorname{erf}(z)=0.9853
$$

Inserting this value into the above equation in which we are solving for $C{s}$ yields

$$
C{s}=\left(2 \times 10^{19} \text { atoms } / \mathrm{m}^{3}\right)+\frac{\left(8 \times 10^{23} \text { atoms } / \mathrm{m}^{3}\right)}{1-\operatorname{erf}(1.7283)}
$$

$$
\begin{gathered}
=\left(2 \times 10^{19} \text { atoms } / \mathrm{m}^{3}\right)+\frac{\left(8 \times 10^{23} \text { atoms } / \mathrm{m}^{3}\right)}{1-0.9853} \\
=5.44 \times 10^{25} \mathrm{Ga} \text { atoms } / \mathrm{m}^{3}
\end{gathered}
$$

question:
Antimony atoms are to be diffused into a silicon wafer using both predeposition and drive-in heat treatments; the background concentration of Sb in this silicon material is known to be $2 \times 10^{20}$ atoms $/ \mathrm{m}^{3}$. The predeposition treatment is to be conducted at $900^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for 1 h ; the surface concentration of Sb is to be maintained at a constant level of $8.0 \times 10^{25}$ atoms $/ \mathrm{m}^{3}$. Drive-in diffusion will be carried out at $1200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for a period of 1.75 h . For the diffusion of Sb in Si , values of $Q{d}$ and $D{0}$ are $3.65 \mathrm{eV} /$ atom and $2.14 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$, respectively.
(a) Calculate the value of $Q{0}$.
(b) Determine the value of $x_{i}$ for the drive-in diffusion treatment.
(c) Also, for the drive-in treatment, compute the position $x$ at which the concentration of $S b$ atoms is $5 \times$ $10^{23} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$.

Give your answer as a tuple ($Q{0}$ in (a), $x{I}$ in (b), $x$ in (c)).

Indium atoms are to be diffused into a silicon wafer using both predeposition and drive-in heat treatments; the background concentration of In in this silicon material is known to be $2 \times 10^{20}$ atoms $/ \mathrm{m}^{3}$. The drive-in diffusion treatment is to be carried out at $1175^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for a period of 2.0 h , which gives a junction depth $x{j}$ of $2.35 \mu \mathrm{~m}$. Compute the predeposition diffusion time at $925^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ if the surface concentration is maintained at a constant level of $2.5 \times 10^{26}$ atoms $/ \mathrm{m}^{3}$. For the diffusion of In in Si, values of $Q{d}$ and $D{0}$ are $3.63 \mathrm{eV} /$ atom and $7.85 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$, respectively.

Assuming conditions of steady state, what is the diffusion flux [in $\left[\left(\mathrm{cm}^{3} \mathrm{STP}\right) / \mathrm{cm}^{2}-\mathrm{s}\right]$ at 298 K ?

Carbon dioxide diffuses through a high-density polyethylene (HDPE) sheet 50 mm thick at a rate of $2.2 \times$ $10^{-8}\left(\mathrm{~cm}^{3} \mathrm{STP}\right) / \mathrm{cm}^{2}-\mathrm{s}$ at 325 K . The pressures of carbon dioxide at the two faces are 4000 kPa and 2500 kPa , which are maintained constant. Assuming conditions of steady state, what is the permeability coefficient at 325 K ?

The permeability coefficient of a type of small gas molecule in a polymer is dependent on absolute temperature according to the following equation:

$$
P_{M}=P_{M_{0}} \exp \left(-\frac{Q_{p}}{R T}\right)
$$

where $P_{M_{0}}$ and $Q_{p}$ are constants for a given gas-polymer pair. Consider the diffusion of water through a polystyrene sheet 30 mm thick. The water vapor pressures at the two faces are 20 kPa and 1 kPa , which are maintained constant. Compute the diffusion flux $\left[\right.$ in $\left.\left(\mathrm{cm}^{3} \mathrm{STP}\right) / \mathrm{cm}^{2}-\mathrm{s}\right]$ at 350 K ? For this diffusion system,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& P_{M_{0}}=9.0 \times 10^{-5}\left(\mathrm{~cm}^{3} \mathrm{STP}\right)(\mathrm{cm}) / \mathrm{cm}^{2}-\mathrm{s}-\mathrm{Pa} \\
& Q_{p}=42,300 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

Assume a condition of steady-state diffusion.

A gas mixture is found to contain two diatomic $A$ and $B$ species ( $A_{2}$ and $B_{2}$ ), the partial pressures of both of which are 0.1013 MPa ( 1 atm ). This mixture is to be enriched in the partial pressure of the $A$ species by passing both gases through a thin sheet of some metal at an elevated temperature. The resulting enriched mixture is to have a partial pressure of 0.051 MPa ( 0.5 atm ) for gas $A$ and 0.0203 MPa ( 0.2 atm ) for gas $B$. The concentrations of $A$ and $B\left(C_{A}\right.$ and $C_{B}$, in $\left.mol / m^{3}\right)$ are functions of gas partial pressures $\left(p_{A_{2}}\right.$ and $p_{B_{2}}$, in MPa) and absolute temperature according to the following expressions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& C_{\mathrm{A}}=1.5 \times 10^{3} \sqrt{p_{\mathrm{A}_{2}}} \exp \left(-\frac{20,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{R T}\right) \\
& C_{\mathrm{B}}=2.0 \times 10^{3} \sqrt{p_{\mathrm{B}_{2}}} \exp \left(-\frac{27,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{R T}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Furthermore, the diffusion coefficients for the diffusion of these gases in the metal are functions of the absolute temperature as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& D_{\mathrm{A}}\left(\mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right)=5.0 \times 10^{-7} \exp \left(-\frac{13,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{R T}\right) \\
& D_{\mathrm{B}}\left(\mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\right)=3.0 \times 10^{-6} \exp \left(-\frac{21,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{R T}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

In addition that the permeate must be produced at a flux of 1.0×10⁻⁵ mol m⁻² s⁻¹ (total of A₂ + B₂) under a counter‐current sweep on the permeate side, determine the temperature at which the ratio of partial pressures 0.051 MPa∶0.0203 MPa can be maintained in steady state.
solution:
Since this situation involves steady-state diffusion, we employ Fick's first law, equation (J=-D \frac{d C}{d x}). Inasmuch as the partial pressures on the high-pressure side of the sheet are the same, and the pressure of $\mathrm{A}_{2}$ on the low pressure side is 2.5 times that of $\mathrm{B}_{2}$, and concentrations are proportional to the square root of the partial pressure, the diffusion flux of $\mathrm{A}, J_{\mathrm{A}}$, is the square root of 2.5 times the diffusion flux of nitrogen $J_{\mathrm{B}}$-i.e.

$$
J_{\mathrm{A}}=\sqrt{2.5} J_{\mathrm{B}}
$$

Let us begin by producing expressions for the diffusion flux of both A and B , and then use them to satisfy the above equation. Fick's first law describing the flux of $\mathrm{A}, J_{\mathrm{A}}$, is as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
J_{\mathrm{A}}=-D_{\mathrm{A}} \frac{\Delta C_{\mathrm{A}}}{\Delta x} \\
=-\left(\frac{1}{\Delta x}\right)\left(5.0 \times 10^{-7}\right)\left[\exp \left(-\frac{13,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{R T}\right)\right]\left[C_{\mathrm{A}}(\mathrm{Lo})-C_{\mathrm{A}}(\mathrm{Hi})\right]
\end{gathered}
$$

where $C_{\mathrm{A}}(\mathrm{Lo})$ and $C_{\mathrm{A}}(\mathrm{Hi})$ designate values of the concentrations of species A on low and high pressure sides of the plate, respectively. Expressions for these two parameters, as determined using equation (C_{\mathrm{A}}=1.5 \times 10^3 \sqrt{p_{\mathrm{A}_2}} \exp \left(-\frac{20,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{R T}\right)), are as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& C_{\mathrm{A}}(\mathrm{Lo})=\left(1.5 \times 10^{3}\right)(\sqrt{0.051 \mathrm{MPa}}[\exp \left(-\frac{20,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{R T}\right)] \\
& C_{\mathrm{A}}(\mathrm{Hi})=\left(1.5 \times 10^{3}\right)(\sqrt{0.1013 \mathrm{MPa}}[\exp \left(-\frac{20,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{R T}\right)]
\end{aligned}
$$

Now, the expression for $C_{\mathrm{A}}(\mathrm{Lo})-C_{\mathrm{A}}(\mathrm{Hi})$ may be written as follows:

$$
C_{\mathrm{A}}(\mathrm{Lo})-C_{\mathrm{A}}(\mathrm{Hi})=\left(1.5 \times 10^{3}\right)(\sqrt{0.051 \mathrm{MPa}}-\sqrt{0.1013 \mathrm{MPa}}[\exp \left(-\frac{20,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{R T}\right)]
$$

Hence, the expression for $J_{\mathrm{A}}$ reads as follows

$$
J_{\mathrm{A}}=-\left(\frac{1}{\Delta x}\right)\left(5.0 \times 10^{-7}\right)\left[\exp \left(-\frac{13,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{R T}\right)\right]\left(1.5 \times 10^{3}\right)(\sqrt{0.051 \mathrm{MPa}}-\sqrt{0.1013 \mathrm{MPa}})\left[\exp \left(-\frac{20,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{R T}\right)\right]
$$

An expression for the diffusion flux for species B may be formulated in a similar manner, which reads as follows:

$$
J_{\mathrm{B}}=-D_{\mathrm{B}} \frac{\Delta C_{\mathrm{B}}}{\Delta x}
$$

$$
=-\left(\frac{1}{\Delta x}\right)\left(3.0 \times 10^{-6}\right)\left[\exp \left(-\frac{21,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{R T}\right)\right]\left(2.0 \times 10^{3}\right)(\sqrt{0.0203 \mathrm{MPa}}-\sqrt{0.1013 \mathrm{MPa}})\left[\exp \left(-\frac{27,000 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}}{R T}\right)\right]
$$

And, finally, as noted above, we want to equate these two expressions such that they satisfy the following relationship:

$$
J_{\mathrm{A}}=\sqrt{2.5} J_{\mathrm{B}}
$$

and then solve this relationship for the temperature $T$ (taking the value of $R$ to be $8.31 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}-\mathrm{K}$ ). Following through this procedure results in a temperature of

$$
T=568 \mathrm{~K}\left(295^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)
$$

Thus, this purification process is possible, which may be carried out at 568 K ; furthermore, it is independent of sheet thickness.

A cylindrical specimen of a nickel alloy having an elastic modulus of $207 \mathrm{GPa}\left(30 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{psi}\right)$ and an original diameter of 10.2 mm ( 0.40 in.) experiences only elastic deformation when a tensile load of $8900 \mathrm{~N}\left(2000 \mathrm{lb}\right)$ is applied. Compute the maximum length of the specimen before deformation if the maximum allowable elongation is 0.25 mm ( 0.010 in.).

An aluminum bar 125 mm (5.0 in.) long and having a square cross section 16.5 mm ( 0.65 in.) on an edge is pulled in tension with a load of $66,700 \mathrm{~N}\left(15,000 \mathrm{lb}\right)$ and experiences an elongation of $0.43 \mathrm{~mm}\left(1.7 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{in}\right.$.). Assuming that the deformation is entirely elastic, calculate the modulus of elasticity of the aluminum.
solution:
This problem asks us to compute the elastic modulus of aluminum. For a square cross-section, $A{0}=b{0}^{2}$, where $b{0}$ is the edge length. Combining equations ((\sigma=\frac{F}{A_0}), (\varepsilon=\frac{l_i-l_0}{l_0}=\frac{\Delta l}{l_0}) and (\sigma=E \varepsilon)) and solving for the modulus of elasticity, $E$, leads to

$$
E=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}=\frac{\frac{F}{A{0}}}{\frac{\Delta l}{l{0}}}=\frac{F l{0}}{b{0}^{2} \Delta l}
$$

Substitution into this expression, values of $l{0}, b{0} \Delta l$, and $F$ given in the problem statement yields the following

$$
\begin{gathered}
E=\frac{(67,700 \mathrm{~N})\left(125 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)}{\left(16.5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)^{2}\left(0.43 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)} \\
=71.2 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}=71.2 \mathrm{GPa}\left(10.4 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{psi}\right)
\end{gathered}
$$

question:

solution:
In order to compute the elongation of the Ni wire when the 300 N load is applied we must employ equation ((\sigma=\frac{F}{A_0}), (\varepsilon=\frac{l_i-l_0}{l_0}=\frac{\Delta l}{l_0}) and (\sigma=E \varepsilon)). Combining these equations and solving for $\Delta I$ leads to the following

$$
\Delta I=l{0} \varepsilon=l{0} \frac{\sigma}{E}=\frac{l{0} F}{E A{0}}=\frac{l{0} F}{E \pi\left(\frac{d{0}}{2}\right)^{2}}=\frac{4 l{0} F}{E \pi d{0}^{2}}
$$

since the cross-sectional area $A{0}$ of a cylinder of diameter $d{0}$ is equal to

$$
A{0}=\pi\left(\frac{d{0}}{2}\right)^{2}
$$

Incorporating into this expression values for $l{0}, F$, and $d{0}$ in the problem statement, and realizing that for $\mathrm{Ni}, E=207$ GPa ( $30 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{psi}$ ) ( table 7.1 ), and that $3 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~mm}=30 \mathrm{~m}$, the wire elongation is

$$
\Delta I=\frac{(4)(30 \mathrm{~m})(300 \mathrm{~N})}{\left(207 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}\right)(\pi)\left(2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)^{2}}=0.0138 \mathrm{~m}=13.8 \mathrm{~mm}(0.53 \mathrm{in} .)
$$

question:
For a brass alloy, the stress at which plastic deformation begins is 345 MPa (50,000 psi), and the modulus of elasticity is $103 \mathrm{GPa}\left(15.0 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{psi}\right)$.
(a) What is the maximum load that can be applied to a specimen with a cross-sectional area of $130 \mathrm{~mm}^{2}$ (0.2 in. ${ }^{2}$ ) without plastic deformation?
(b) If the original specimen length is 76 mm (3.0 in.), what is the maximum length to which it can be stretched without causing plastic deformation?

Give your answer as a tuple: (answer to (a), answer to (b)).
solution:
(a) This portion of the problem calls for a determination of the maximum load that can be applied without plastic deformation $\left(F{y}\right)$. Taking the yield strength to be 345 MPa , and employment of equation (\sigma=\frac{F}{A_0}) leads to

$$
\begin{gathered}
F{y}=\sigma{y} A{0}=\left(345 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}\right)\left(133 \mathrm{~mm}^{2}\right)\left(\frac{1 \mathrm{~m}}{10^{3} \mathrm{~mm}}\right)^{2} \\
=44,850 \mathrm{~N} \quad\left(10,000 \mathrm{lb}{\mathrm{f}}\right)
\end{gathered}
$$

(b) The maximum length to which the sample may be deformed without plastic deformation is determined by combining equation ((\varepsilon=\frac{l_i-l_0}{l_0}=\frac{\Delta l}{l_0}) and (\sigma=E \varepsilon)) as

$$
\begin{gathered}
l{i}=\varepsilon l{0}+l{0}=l{0}(\varepsilon+1) \\
l{i}=l{0}\left(\frac{\sigma}{E}+1\right)
\end{gathered}
$$

Incorporating values of $l{0}, \sigma$, and $E$ provided in the problem statement leads to the computation of $l{i}$ as follows:

$$
l{i}=(76 \mathrm{~mm})\left[\frac{345 \mathrm{MPa}}{103 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{MPa}}+1\right]=76.25 \mathrm{~mm} \text { (3.01 in.) }
$$

question: A cylindrical rod of steel ( $E=207 \mathrm{GPa}, 30 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{psi}$ ) having a yield strength of 310 MPa (45,000 psi) is to be subjected to a load of $11,100 \mathrm{~N}$ (2500 lb). If the length of the rod is 500 mm (20.0 in.), what must be the diameter to allow an elongation of $0.38 \mathrm{~mm}(0.015 \mathrm{in}$.)?
solution:
This problem asks us to compute the diameter of a cylindrical specimen of steel in order to allow an elongation of 0.38 mm . Employing equation ((\sigma=\frac{F}{A_0}) and (\varepsilon=\frac{l_i-l_0}{l_0}=\frac{\Delta l}{l_0})), and 7.5, assuming that deformation is entirely elastic, we may write the following expression:

$$
\begin{gathered}
\sigma=\frac{F}{A{0}}=\frac{F}{\pi\left(\frac{d{0}^{2}}{4}\right)} \\
=E \varepsilon=E\left(\frac{\Delta l}{l{0}}\right)
\end{gathered}
$$

And upon simplification

$$
\frac{F}{\pi\left(\frac{d{0}^{2}}{4}\right)}=E\left(\frac{\Delta l}{l{0}}\right)
$$

Solving for the original cross-sectional area, $d{0}$, leads to

$$
d{0}=\sqrt{\frac{4 l{0} F}{\pi E \Delta l}}
$$

Incorporation of values for $l{0}, F, E$, and $\Delta l$ provided in the problem statement yields the following:

$$
\begin{gathered}
d{0}=\sqrt{\frac{(4)\left(500 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)(11,100 \mathrm{~N})}{(\pi)\left(207 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}\right)\left(0.38 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)}} \\
=9.5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}=9.5 \mathrm{~mm}(0.376 \mathrm{in} .)
\end{gathered}
$$

solution:
The elastic modulus is the slope in the linear elastic region (equation (E=\text { slope }=\frac{\Delta \sigma}{\Delta \varepsilon}=\frac{\sigma_2-\sigma_1}{\varepsilon_2-\varepsilon_1})) as

$$
E=\frac{\Delta \sigma}{\Delta \varepsilon}=\frac{\sigma{2}-\sigma{1}}{\varepsilon{2}-\varepsilon{1}}
$$

Since stress-strain curves for all of the \frac{metals}{alloys} pass through the origin, we make take $\sigma{1}=0$ and $\varepsilon{1}=$ 0 . Determinations of $\sigma{2}$ and $\varepsilon{2}$ are possible by moving the cursor to some arbitrary point in the linear region of the curve and then reading corresponding values in the "Stress" and "Strain" windows that are located below the plot.

$$
E=\frac{\sigma{2}-\sigma{1}}{\varepsilon{2}-\varepsilon{1}}=\frac{492.4 \mathrm{MPa}-0 \mathrm{MPa}}{0.0049-0}=100,500 \mathrm{MPa}=100.5 \mathrm{GPa}
$$

The elastic modulus for titanium given in  table 7.1  is 107 GPa , which is in reasonably good agreement with this value.

Here the cursor point resides in the elastic region at a stress of 916.7 MPa (which is the value of $\sigma{2}$ ) at a strain of 0.0045 (which is the value of $\varepsilon{2}$ ). Thus, the elastic modulus is equal to

$$

$$

The elastic modulus for steel given in  table 7.1  is 207 GPa , which is in good agreement with this value.

(c) A screenshot for the aluminum alloy in the elastic region is shown below.


Here the cursor point resides in the elastic region at a stress of 193.6 MPa (which is the value of $\sigma{2}$ ) at a strain of 0.0028 (which is the value of $\varepsilon{2}$ ). Thus, the elastic modulus is equal to

$$
E=\frac{\sigma{2}-\sigma{1}}{\varepsilon{2}-\varepsilon{1}}=\frac{193.6 \mathrm{MPa}-0 \mathrm{MPa}}{0.0028-0}=69,100 \mathrm{MPa}=69.1 \mathrm{GPa}
$$

The elastic modulus for aluminum given in  table 7.1  is 69 GPa , which is in excellent agreement with this value.
(d) A screenshot for the carbon steel alloy in the elastic region is shown below.



Here the cursor point resides in the elastic region at a stress of 160.6 MPa (which is the value of $\sigma{2}$ ) at a strain of 0.0008 (which is the value of $\varepsilon{2}$ ). Thus, the elastic modulus is equal to

$$
E=\frac{\sigma{2}-\sigma{1}}{\varepsilon{2}-\varepsilon{1}}=\frac{160.6 \mathrm{MPa}-0 \mathrm{MPa}}{0.0008-0}=200,800 \mathrm{MPa}=200.8 \mathrm{GPa}
$$

The elastic modulus for steel given in  table 7.1  is 207 GPa , which is in reasonable agreement with this value.

question: Consider a cylindrical specimen of a steel alloy ( figure 7.34 ) 8.5 mm (0.33 in.) in diameter and 80 mm (3.15 in.) long that is pulled in tension. Determine its elongation when a load of $65,250 \mathrm{~N}(14,500 \mathrm{lb})$ is applied.
solution:
This problem asks that we calculate the elongation $\Delta l$ of a specimen of steel the stress-strain behavior of which is shown in  figure 7.34 . First it becomes necessary to compute the stress when a load of $65,250 \mathrm{~N}$ is applied using equation (\sigma=\frac{F}{A_0}) as

$$
\sigma=\frac{F}{A{0}}=\frac{F}{\pi\left(\frac{d{0}}{2}\right)^{2}}=\frac{65,250 \mathrm{~N}}{\pi\left(\frac{8.5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}}{2}\right)^{2}}=1.15 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}=1150 \mathrm{MPa}(170,000 \mathrm{psi})
$$

Referring to  figure 7.34 , at this stress level we are in the elastic region (shown in the inset) on the stress-strain curve, which corresponds to a strain of 0.0054 . Now, utilization of equation (\varepsilon=\frac{l_i-l_0}{l_0}=\frac{\Delta l}{l_0}) to compute the value of $\Delta l$ :

$$
\Delta l=\varepsilon l{0}=(0.0054)(80 \mathrm{~mm})=0.43 \mathrm{~mm}(0.017 \mathrm{in} .)
$$

question:
The net bonding energy $E{N}$ between two isolated positive and negative ions is a function of interionic distance $r$ as follows:

$$
E{N}=-\frac{A}{r}+\frac{B}{r^{n}}
$$

where $A, B$, and $n$ are constants for the particular ion pair. equation (E_N=-\frac{A}{r}+\frac{B}{r^n}) is also valid for the bonding energy between adjacent ions in solid materials. The modulus of elasticity $E$ is proportional to the slope of the interionic force-separation curve at the equilibrium interionic separation; that is,

$$
E \propto\left(\frac{d F}{d r}\right){r{0}}
$$

Derive an expression for the dependence of the modulus of elasticity on these $A, B$, and $n$ parameters (for the twoion system), using the following procedure:

1. Establish a relationship for the force $F$ as a function of $r$, realizing that

$$
F=\frac{d E{N}}{d r}
$$

2. Now take the derivative $d F / d r$.
3. Develop an expression for $r{0}$, the equilibrium separation. Because $r{0}$ corresponds to the value of $r$ at the minimum of the $E{N}$-versus-r curve ( figure 2.10b ), take the derivative $d E{N} / d r$, set it equal to zero, and solve for $r$, which corresponds to $r{0}$.
4. Finally, substitute this expression for $r{0}$ into the relationship obtained by taking $d F / d r$.

solution:
(a) We are asked, in this portion of the problem, to determine the elongation of a cylindrical specimen of steel. To solve this part of the problem requires that we use equation ((\sigma=\frac{F}{A_0}), (\varepsilon=\frac{l_i-l_0}{l_0}=\frac{\Delta l}{l_0}) and (\sigma=E \varepsilon)). equation (\sigma=E \varepsilon) reads as follows:

$$
\sigma=E \varepsilon
$$

Substitution the expression for $\sigma$ from equation (\sigma=\frac{F}{A_0}) and the expression for $\varepsilon$ from equation (\varepsilon=\frac{l_i-l_0}{l_0}=\frac{\Delta l}{l_0}) leads to

$$
\frac{F}{\pi\left(\frac{d{0}^{2}}{4}\right)}=E\left(\frac{\Delta l}{l{0}}\right)
$$

In this equation $d{0}$ is the original cross-sectional diameter. Now, solving for $\Delta l$ yields

$$
\Delta l=\frac{4 F l{0}}{\pi d{0}^{2} E}
$$

And incorporating values of $F, l{0}$, and $d{0}$, and realizing that $E=207 \mathrm{GPa}$ ( table 7.1 ), leads to

$$
\Delta l=\frac{(4)\left(48,900 \mathrm{~N}\right)\left(250 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)}{(\pi)\left(15.2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)^{2}\left(207 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}\right)}=3.25 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}=0.325 \mathrm{~mm}(0.013 \mathrm{in} .)
$$

(b) We are now called upon to determine the change in diameter, $\Delta d$. Using equation (\nu=-\frac{\varepsilon_x}{\varepsilon_z}=-\frac{\varepsilon_y}{\varepsilon_z}) (the definition of Poisson's ratio)

$$
v=-\frac{\varepsilon{x}}{\varepsilon{z}}=-\frac{\Delta \frac{d}{d}{0}}{\Delta \frac{l}{l}{0}}
$$

From  table 7.1 , for steel, the value of Poisson's ratio, $v$, is 0.30 . Now, solving the above expression for $\Delta d$ yields

$$
\begin{gathered}
\Delta d=-\frac{v \Delta l d{0}}{l{0}}=-\frac{(0.30)(0.325 \mathrm{~mm})(15.2 \mathrm{~mm})}{250 \mathrm{~mm}} \\
=-5.9 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mm}\left(-2.3 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{in} .\right)
\end{gathered}
$$

The diameter will decrease since $\Delta d$ is negative.

solution:
This problem asks that we calculate the force necessary to produce a reduction in diameter of $2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ mm for a cylindrical bar of aluminum. For a cylindrical specimen, the cross-sectional area is equal to

$$
A{0}=\frac{\pi d{0}^{2}}{4}
$$

Now, combining equation ((\sigma=\frac{F}{A_0}) and (\sigma=E \varepsilon)) leads to

$$
\sigma=\frac{F}{A{0}}=\frac{F}{\frac{\pi d{0}^{2}}{4}}=E \varepsilon{z}
$$

And, since from equation (\nu=-\frac{\varepsilon_x}{\varepsilon_z}=-\frac{\varepsilon_y}{\varepsilon_z})

$$
\varepsilon{z}=-\frac{\varepsilon{x}}{v}=-\frac{\frac{\Delta d}{d{0}}}{v}=-\frac{\Delta d}{v d{0}}
$$

Substitution of this equation into the previous one gives

$$
\frac{F}{\frac{\pi d{0}^{2}}{4}}=E\left(-\frac{\Delta d}{v d{0}}\right)
$$

And, solving for $F$ leads to

$$
F=-\frac{d{0} \Delta d \pi E}{4 v}
$$

Incorporating into this equation values of $d{0}$ and $\Delta d$ from the problem statement and realizing that, for aluminum, $v$ $=0.33$ and $E=69 \mathrm{GPa}$ ( table 7.1 ) allows us to determine the value of the force $F$ as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
F=-\frac{\left(19 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)\left(-2.5 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}\right)(\pi)\left(69 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}\right)}{(4)(0.33)} \\
=7,800 \mathrm{~N}\left(1,785 \mathrm{lb}{\mathrm{f}}\right)
\end{gathered}
$$

question: A cylindrical specimen of a metal alloy 10 mm ( 0.4 in.) in diameter is stressed elastically in tension. A force of $15,000 \mathrm{~N}$ (3,370 lbs) produces a reduction in specimen diameter of $7 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mm}\left(2.8 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{in}\right.$.). Compute Poisson's ratio for this material if its elastic modulus is 100 GPa ( $14.5 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{psi}$ ).
solution:
This problem asks that we compute Poisson's ratio for the metal alloy. From equation ((\sigma=\frac{F}{A_0}) and (\sigma=E \varepsilon))

$$
\varepsilon{z}=\frac{\sigma}{E}=\frac{F}{A{0}}\left(\frac{1}{E}\right)=\frac{F}{A{0} E}=\frac{F}{\pi\left(\frac{d{0}}{2}\right)^{2} E}=\frac{4 F}{\pi d{0}^{2} E}
$$

Since the transverse strain $\varepsilon{\mathrm{x}}$ is equal to

$$
\varepsilon{x}=\frac{\Delta d}{d{0}}
$$

and Poisson's ratio is defined by equation (\nu=-\frac{\varepsilon_x}{\varepsilon_z}=-\frac{\varepsilon_y}{\varepsilon_z}), then

$$
v=-\frac{\varepsilon{x}}{\varepsilon{z}}=-\frac{\Delta \frac{d}{d}{0}}{\left(\frac{4 F}{\pi d{0}^{2} E}\right)}=-\frac{d{0} \Delta d \pi E}{4 F}
$$

Now, incorporating values of $d{0}, \Delta d, E$ and $F$ from the problem statement yields the following value for Poisson's ratio

$$
v=-\frac{\left(10 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)\left(-7 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}\right)(\pi)\left(100 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}\right)}{(4)(15,000 \mathrm{~N})}=0.367
$$

question: A cylindrical specimen of a hypothetical metal alloy is stressed in compression. If its original and final diameters are 30.00 and 30.04 mm , respectively, and its final length is 105.20 mm , compute its original length if the deformation is totally elastic. The elastic and shear moduli for this alloy are 65.5 and 25.4 GPa, respectively.
solution:
This problem asks that we compute the original length of a cylindrical specimen that is stressed in compression. It is first convenient to compute the lateral strain $\varepsilon{x}$ using the Poisson's ratio expression for this strain:

$$
\varepsilon{x}=\frac{\Delta d}{d{0}}=\frac{30.04 \mathrm{~mm}-30.00 \mathrm{~mm}}{30.00 \mathrm{~mm}}=1.33 \times 10^{-3}
$$

In order to determine the longitudinal strain $\varepsilon{z}$ we need Poisson's ratio, which may be computed using equation (E=2 G(1+\nu)); using values for $E$ and $G$ in the problem statement and solving this equation for $v$ yields

$$
v=\frac{E}{2 G}-1=\frac{65.5 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{MPa}}{(2)\left(25.4 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{MPa}\right)}-1=0.289
$$

Now $\varepsilon{z}$ may be computed from equation (\nu=-\frac{\varepsilon_x}{\varepsilon_z}=-\frac{\varepsilon_y}{\varepsilon_z}) as

$$
\varepsilon{z}=-\frac{\varepsilon{x}}{v}=-\frac{1.33 \times 10^{-3}}{0.289}=-4.60 \times 10^{-3}
$$

Using this value of $\varepsilon{z}$ we solve for $l{0}$ using equation (\varepsilon=\frac{l_i-l_0}{l_0}=\frac{\Delta l}{l_0}) and the value of $l{i}$ given in the problem statement:

$$
\begin{gathered}
l{0}=\frac{l{i}}{1+\varepsilon{z}} \\
=\frac{105.20 \mathrm{~mm}}{1+\left(-4.60 \times 10^{-3}\right)}=105.69 \mathrm{~mm}
\end{gathered}
$$

question: Consider a cylindrical specimen of some hypothetical metal alloy that has a diameter of 10.0 mm (0.39 in.). A tensile force of $1500 \mathrm{~N}(340 \mathrm{lb})$ produces an elastic reduction in diameter of $6.7 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mm}(2.64 \times$ $10^{-5}$ in.). Compute the elastic modulus of this alloy, given that Poisson's ratio is 0.35 .
solution:
This problem asks that we calculate the modulus of elasticity of a metal that is stressed in tension. Combining equation ((\sigma=\frac{F}{A_0}) and (\sigma=E \varepsilon)) leads to

$$
E=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon{z}}=\frac{\frac{F}{A{0}}}{\varepsilon{z}}=\frac{F}{A{0} \varepsilon{z}}=\frac{F}{\varepsilon{z} \pi\left(\frac{d{0}}{2}\right)^{2}}=\frac{4 F}{\varepsilon{z} \pi d{0}^{2}}
$$

From the definition of Poisson's ratio, (equation (\nu=-\frac{\varepsilon_x}{\varepsilon_z}=-\frac{\varepsilon_y}{\varepsilon_z})) and realizing that for the transverse strain, $\varepsilon{x}=\frac{\Delta d}{d{0}}$ leads to

$$
\varepsilon{z}=-\frac{\varepsilon{x}}{v}=-\frac{\Delta \frac{d}{d}{0}}{v}=-\frac{\Delta d}{d{0} v}
$$

Therefore, substitution of this expression for $\varepsilon{z}$ into the above equation for $E$ yields

$$
E=\frac{4 F}{\varepsilon{z} \pi d{0}^{2}}=\frac{4 F}{\left(-\frac{\Delta d}{d{0} v}\right) \pi d{0}^{2}}=-\frac{4 F v}{\pi d{0} \Delta d}
$$

Incorporation of values for $F, v, d{0}$, and $\Delta d$ given in the problem statement (and realizing that $\Delta d$ is negative) allows us to calculate the modulus of elasticity as follows:

$$
E=-\frac{(4)(1500 \mathrm{~N})(0.35)}{\pi\left(10 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)\left(-6.7 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}\right)}=10^{11} \mathrm{~Pa}=100 \mathrm{GPa}\left(14.7 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{psi}\right)
$$

question:
Consider the brass alloy for which the stress-strain behavior is shown in  figure 7.12 . A cylindrical specimen of this material 10.0 mm ( 0.39 in.) in diameter and 101.6 mm ( 4.0 in.) long is pulled in tension with a force of $10,000 \mathrm{~N}\left(2250 \mathrm{lb}\right)$. If it is known that this alloy has a value for Poisson's ratio of 0.35 , compute (a) the specimen elongation and (b) the reduction in specimen diameter.

Give your answer as a tuple (the specimen elongation, the reduction in specimen diameter(positive))
solution:
(a) This portion of the problem asks that we compute the elongation of the brass specimen. The first calculation necessary is that of the applied stress using equation (\sigma=\frac{F}{A_0}), as

$$
\sigma=\frac{F}{A{0}}=\frac{F}{\pi\left(\frac{d{0}}{2}\right)^{2}}=\frac{10,000 \mathrm{~N}}{\pi\left(\frac{10 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}}{2}\right)^{2}}=127 \mathrm{MPa} \quad(17,900 \mathrm{psi})
$$

From the stress-strain plot in  figure 7.12 , this stress corresponds to a strain of about $1.5 \times 10^{-3}$. From the definition of strain, equation (\varepsilon=\frac{l_i-l_0}{l_0}=\frac{\Delta l}{l_0})

$$
\Delta l=\varepsilon l{0}=\left(1.5 \times 10^{-3}\right)(101.6 \mathrm{~mm})=0.15 \mathrm{~mm} \quad\left(6.0 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{in}\right)
$$

(b) In order to determine the reduction in diameter $\Delta d$, it is necessary to use equation (\nu=-\frac{\varepsilon_x}{\varepsilon_z}=-\frac{\varepsilon_y}{\varepsilon_z}) and the definition of lateral strain (i.e., $\varepsilon{x}=\Delta \frac{d}{d}{0}$ ) as follows

$$
\begin{gathered}
\Delta d=d{0} \varepsilon{x}=-d{0} v \varepsilon{z}=-(10 \mathrm{~mm})(0.35)\left(1.5 \times 10^{-3}\right) \\
=-5.25 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mm} \quad\left(-2.05 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{in}.\right)
\end{gathered}
$$

question:
A cylindrical rod 120 mm long and having a diameter of 15.0 mm is to be deformed using a tensile load of $35,000 \mathrm{~N}$. It must not experience either plastic deformation or a diameter reduction of more than $1.2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mm}$. Calculate the change in diameter of the candidate materials.

| Material | Modulus of Elasticity (GPa) | Yield Strength (MPa) | Poisson's Ratio |
| :-- | :--: | :--: | :--: |
| Aluminum alloy | 70 | 250 | 0.33 |
| Titanium alloy | 105 | 850 | 0.36 |
| Steel alloy | 205 | 550 | 0.27 |

Give your answer as a tuple (Aluminum alloy,  Steel alloy, Titanium alloy).
solution:
This problem asks that we assess the four alloys relative to the two criteria presented. The first criterion is that the material not experience plastic deformation when the tensile load of $35,000 \mathrm{~N}$ is applied; this means that the stress corresponding to this load not exceed the yield strength of the material. Upon computing the stress

$$
\sigma=\frac{F}{A{0}}=\frac{F}{\pi\left(\frac{d{0}}{2}\right)^{2}}=\frac{35,000 \mathrm{~N}}{\pi\left(\frac{15 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}}{2}\right)^{2}}=200 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}=200 \mathrm{MPa}
$$

Of the alloys listed, the $\mathrm{Al}, \mathrm{Ti}$ and steel alloys have yield strengths greater than 200 MPa .
Relative to the second criterion (i.e., that $\Delta d$ be less than $1.2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mm}$ ), it is necessary to calculate the change in diameter $\Delta d$ for these three alloys. It is first necessary to generate an expression for Poisson's ratio from equation (\nu=-\frac{\varepsilon_x}{\varepsilon_z}=-\frac{\varepsilon_y}{\varepsilon_z}), where $\varepsilon{x}=\Delta \frac{d}{d}{0}$, and, from equation (\sigma=E \varepsilon), $\varepsilon{y}=\\frac{sigma}{E}$. Thus

$$
v=-\frac{\varepsilon{x}}{\varepsilon{z}}=-\frac{\frac{\Delta d}{d{0}}}{\frac{\sigma}{E}}=-\frac{E \Delta d}{\sigma d{0}}
$$

Now, solving for $\Delta d$ from this expression,

$$
\Delta d=-\frac{v \sigma d{0}}{E}
$$

From the data presented in the table in the problem statement, we now compute the value of $\Delta d$ for each of the four alloys.

For the aluminum alloy ( $E=70 \mathrm{GPa}, v=0.33$, and taking the stress $\sigma$ to be 200 MPa )

$$
\Delta d=-\frac{(0.33)(200 \mathrm{MPa})(15 \mathrm{~mm})}{70 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{MPa}}=-1.41 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mm}
$$

Therefore, the Al alloy is not a candidate because its $\Delta d$ (in a negative sense) is greater than $1.2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mm}$.
For the steel alloy $(E=205 \mathrm{GPa}, v=0.27)$

$$
\Delta d=-\frac{(0.27)(200 \mathrm{MPa})(15 \mathrm{~mm})}{205 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{MPa}}=-0.40 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mm}
$$

Therefore, the steel is a candidate.
For the Ti alloy $(E=105 \mathrm{GPa}, v=0.36)$

$$
\Delta d=-\frac{(0.36)(200 \mathrm{MPa})(15 \mathrm{~mm})}{105 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{MPa}}=-1.0 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mm}
$$

Hence, the titanium alloy is also a candidate.

question:
A cylindrical rod 500 mm (20.0 in.) long and having a diameter of 12.7 mm ( 0.50 in.) is to be subjected to a tensile load. If the rod is to experience neither plastic deformation nor an elongation of more than 1.3 mm ( 0.05 in.) when the applied load is $29,000 \mathrm{~N}\left(6500 \mathrm{lb}{2}\right)$, compute $\Delta l$ of the candidate materials.

| Material | Modulus of Elasticity (GPa) | Yield Strength (MPa) | Tensile Strength (MPa) |
| :-- | :--: | :--: | :--: |
| Aluminum alloy | 70 | 255 | 420 |
| Brass alloy | 100 | 345 | 420 |
| Steel alloy | 207 | 450 | 550 |

Give your answer as a tuple (Aluminum alloy, Brass alloy, Steel alloy)
solution:
This problem asks that we ascertain which of four metal alloys will not (1) experience plastic deformation, and (2) elongate more than 1.3 mm when a tensile load of $29,000 \mathrm{~N}$ is applied. It is first necessary to compute the stress using equation (\sigma=\frac{F}{A_0}); a material to be used for this application must necessarily have a yield strength greater than this value. Thus,

$$
\sigma=\frac{F}{A{0}}=\frac{F}{\pi\left(\frac{d{0}}{2}\right)^{2}}=\frac{29,000 \mathrm{~N}}{\pi\left(\frac{12.7 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}}{2}\right)^{2}}=230 \mathrm{MPa}
$$

Of the metal alloys listed, aluminum, brass and steel have yield strengths greater than this stress.
Next, we must compute the elongation produced in each of aluminum, brass, and steel by combining equation ((\varepsilon=\frac{l_i-l_0}{l_0}=\frac{\Delta l}{l_0}) and (\sigma=E \varepsilon)) in order to determine whether or not this elongation is less than 1.3 mm . For aluminum ( $E=$ $70 \mathrm{GPa}=70 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{MPa}$ )

$$
\Delta l=\varepsilon l{0}=\frac{\sigma l{0}}{E}=\frac{(230 \mathrm{MPa})(500 \mathrm{~mm})}{70 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{MPa}}=1.64 \mathrm{~mm}
$$

Thus, aluminum is not a candidate because its $\Delta l$ is greater than 1.3 mm .

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { For brass }\left(E=100 \mathrm{GPa}=100 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{MPa}\right) \\
& \qquad \Delta l=\frac{\sigma l{0}}{E}=\frac{(230 \mathrm{MPa})(500 \mathrm{~mm})}{100 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{MPa}}=1.15 \mathrm{~mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, brass is a candidate. And, for steel $\left(E=207 \mathrm{GPa}=207 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{MPa}\right)$

$$
\Delta I=\frac{\sigma I{0}}{E}=\frac{(230 \mathrm{MPa})(500 \mathrm{~mm})}{207 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{MPa}}=0.56 \mathrm{~mm}
$$

Therefore, of these four alloys, only brass and steel satisfy the stipulated criteria.

solution:

E=\frac{\sigma{2}-\sigma{1}}{\varepsilon{2}-\varepsilon{1}}
