question: A cylindrical specimen of a brass alloy having a length of 100 mm (4 in.) must elongate only 5 mm ( 0.2 in.) when a tensile load of $100,000 \mathrm{~N}(22,500 \mathrm{lb})$ is applied. Under these circumstances, what must be the radius of the specimen? Consider this brass alloy to have the stress-strain behavior shown in  figure 7.12 .
solution:
We are asked to calculate the radius of a cylindrical brass specimen in order to produce an elongation of 5 mm when a load of $100,000 \mathrm{~N}$ is applied. It first becomes necessary to compute the strain corresponding to this elongation using equation (\varepsilon=\frac{l_i-l_0}{l_0}=\frac{\Delta l}{l_0}) as

$$
\varepsilon=\frac{\Delta l}{l{0}}=\frac{5 \mathrm{~mm}}{100 \mathrm{~mm}}=5 \times 10^{-2}
$$

From  figure 7.12 , a stress of 335 MPa (49,000 psi) corresponds to this strain. Since for a cylindrical specimen, stress, force, and initial radius $r{0}$ are related (equation (\sigma=\frac{F}{A_0})) as

$$
\sigma=\frac{F}{\Delta{0}}=\frac{F}{\pi r{0}^{2}}
$$

then taking $F=10,000 \mathrm{~N}$ and $\sigma=335 \mathrm{MPa}=335 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}, r{0}$ is calculated as follows

$$
r{0}=\sqrt{\frac{F}{\pi \sigma}}=\sqrt{\frac{100,000 \mathrm{~N}}{\pi\left(335 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}\right)}}=0.0097 \mathrm{~m}=9.7 \mathrm{~mm} \quad(0.38 \mathrm{in} .)
$$

question: A load of $140,000 \mathrm{~N}(31,500 \mathrm{lb})$ is applied to a cylindrical specimen of a steel alloy (displaying the stressstrain behavior shown in  figure 7.34 ) that has a cross-sectional diameter of 10 mm ( 0.40 in.). If the original specimen length is 500 mm (20 in.), how much will it increase in length when this load is applied?
solution:
This problem asks us to determine the deformation characteristics of a steel specimen, the stress-strain behavior for which is shown in  figure 7.34 .
(a) In order to ascertain whether the deformation is elastic or plastic, we must first compute the stress, then locate it on the stress-strain curve, and, finally, note whether this point is on the elastic or elastic + plastic region. Thus, from equation (\sigma=\frac{F}{A_0})

$$
\sigma=\frac{F}{A{0}}=\frac{F}{\pi\left(\frac{d{0}}{2}\right)^{2}}=\frac{140,000 \mathrm{~N}}{\pi\left(\frac{10 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}}{2}\right)^{2}}=1,782 \mathrm{MPa} \quad(250,000 \mathrm{psi})
$$

The 1782 MPa point is beyond the linear portion of the curve, and, therefore, the deformation will be both elastic and plastic.
(b) This portion of the problem asks us to compute the increase in specimen length. From the stress-strain curve, the strain at 1782 MPa is approximately 0.017 . Thus, from equation (\varepsilon=\frac{l_i-l_0}{l_0}=\frac{\Delta l}{l_0})

$$
\Delta l=\varepsilon l{0}=(0.017)(500 \mathrm{~mm})=8.5 \mathrm{~mm} \quad(0.34 \mathrm{in} .)
$$

question:
A bar of a steel alloy that exhibits the stress-strain behavior shown in  figure 7.34  is subjected to a tensile load; the specimen is 375 mm (14.8 in.) long and has a square cross section 5.5 mm ( 0.22 in.) on a side.
Compute the magnitude of the load necessary to produce an elongation of 2.25 mm ( 0.088 in.).
solution:
We are asked to compute the magnitude of the load necessary to produce an elongation of 2.25 mm for the steel displaying the stress-strain behavior shown in  figure 7.34 . First, calculate the strain, and then the corresponding stress from the plot. Using equation (\varepsilon=\frac{l_i-l_0}{l_0}=\frac{\Delta l}{l_0}), the strain $\varepsilon$ is equal to

$$
\varepsilon=\frac{\Delta l}{l{0}}=\frac{2.25 \mathrm{~mm}}{375 \mathrm{~mm}}=0.006
$$

This is within the elastic region, which, from the inset of  figure 7.34 , corresponds to a stress of about 1250 MPa (180,000 psi). Now, from equation (\sigma=\frac{F}{A_0})

$$
F=\sigma A{0}=\sigma b^{2}
$$

in which $b$ is the cross-section side length. Thus,

$$
F=\left(1250 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}\right)\left(5.5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)^{2}=37,800 \mathrm{~N} \quad\left(8500 \mathrm{lb}{\mathrm{f}}\right)
$$

This problem calls for us to make a stress-strain plot for stainless steel, given its tensile load-length data, and then to determine some of its mechanical characteristics.
(a) The data are plotted below on two plots: the first corresponds to the entire stress-strain curve, while for the second, the curve extends to just beyond the elastic region of deformation.


(b) The elastic modulus is the slope in the linear elastic region (equation (E=\text { slope }=\frac{\Delta \sigma}{\Delta \varepsilon}=\frac{\sigma_2-\sigma_1}{\varepsilon_2-\varepsilon_1}))—i.e.,

$$
E=\frac{\sigma{2}-\sigma{1}}{\varepsilon{2}-\varepsilon{1}}
$$

Because the stress-strain curve passes through the origin, to simplify the computation let us take both $\sigma{1}$ and $\varepsilon{1}$ to be zero. If we select $\sigma{2}=400 \mathrm{MPa}$, its corresponding strain on the plot $\varepsilon{2}$ is about 0.002 . Thus the elastic modulus is equal to

$$
E=\frac{400 \mathrm{MPa}-0 \mathrm{MPa}}{0.002-0}=200 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{MPa}=200 \mathrm{GPa} \quad\left(29 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{psi}\right)
$$

(c) For the yield strength, the 0.002 strain offset line is drawn dashed in the lower plot. It intersects the stress-strain curve at approximately $750 \mathrm{MPa}(112,000 \mathrm{psi})$.
(d) The tensile strength is approximately $1250 \mathrm{MPa}(180,000 \mathrm{psi})$, corresponding to the maximum stress on the complete stress-strain plot.
(e) Ductility, in percent elongation, is just the plastic strain at fracture, multiplied by one-hundred. The total fracture strain at fracture is 0.115 ; subtracting out the elastic strain (which is about 0.003 ) leaves a plastic strain of 0.112 . Thus, the ductility is about $11.2 \% \mathrm{EL}$.
(f) From equation (U_r=\frac{1}{2} \sigma_y \varepsilon_y=\frac{1}{2} \sigma_y\left(\frac{\sigma_y}{E}\right)=\frac{\sigma_y^2}{2 E}), the modulus of resilience is just

$$
U{r}=\frac{\sigma{c}^{2}}{2 E}
$$

Using values of $\sigma{c}$ and $E$ computed above, $\left(750 \mathrm{MPa}=750 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}\right.$ and $200 \mathrm{GPa}=200 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$, respectively) the modulus of resilience is computed as follows:

$$
U{r}=\frac{\left(750 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}\right)^{2}}{(2)\left(200 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}\right)}=1.40 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}=1.40 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{m}^{3} \quad\left(210 \mathrm{in} .-\mathrm{lb}{\mathrm{f}} / \mathrm{in} .^{3}\right)
$$

This problem calls for us to make a stress-strain plot for a magnesium, given its tensile load-length data, and then to determine some of its mechanical characteristics.
(a) The data are plotted below on two plots: the first corresponds to the entire stress-strain curve, while for the second, the curve extends just beyond the elastic region of deformation.


(b) The elastic modulus is the slope in the linear elastic region (equation (E=\text { slope }=\frac{\Delta \sigma}{\Delta \varepsilon}=\frac{\sigma_2-\sigma_1}{\varepsilon_2-\varepsilon_1}))-i.e.,

$$
E=\frac{\sigma{2}-\sigma{1}}{\varepsilon{2}-\varepsilon{1}}
$$

Because the stress-strain curve passes through the origin, to simplify the computation let us take both $\sigma{1}$ and $\varepsilon{1}$ to be zero. If we select $\sigma{2}=50 \mathrm{MPa}$, its corresponding strain on the plot $\varepsilon{2}$ is about 0.001 . Thus the elastic modulus is equal to

$$
E=\frac{\sigma{2}-\sigma{1}}{\varepsilon{2}-\varepsilon{1}}=\frac{50 \mathrm{MPa}-0 \mathrm{MPa}}{0.001-0}=50 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{MPa}=50 \mathrm{GPa}\left(7.3 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{psi}\right)
$$

(c) For the yield strength, the 0.002 strain offset line is drawn dashed. It intersects the stress-strain curve at approximately $140 \mathrm{MPa}(20,300 \mathrm{psi})$.
(d) The tensile strength is approximately $230 \mathrm{MPa}(33,350 \mathrm{psi})$, corresponding to the maximum stress on the complete stress-strain plot.
(e) From equation (U_r=\frac{1}{2} \sigma_y \varepsilon_y=\frac{1}{2} \sigma_y\left(\frac{\sigma_y}{E}\right)=\frac{\sigma_y^2}{2 E}), the modulus of resilience is just

$$
U{r}=\frac{\sigma{y}^{2}}{2 E}
$$

which, using data computed above, yields a value of

$$
U{r}=\frac{\left(140 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}\right)^{2}}{(2)\left(50 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}\right)}=1.96 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}=1.96 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{m}^{3} \quad\left(28.4 \mathrm{in} .-\mathrm{lb}{\mathrm{f}} / \mathrm{in} .^{3}\right)
$$

(f) The ductility, in percent elongation, is just the plastic strain at fracture, multiplied by one-hundred. The total fracture strain at fracture is 0.110 ; subtracting out the elastic strain (which is about 0.003 ) leaves a plastic strain of 0.107 . Thus, the ductility is about $10.7 \% \mathrm{EL}$.

question: A cylindrical metal specimen having an original diameter of 12.8 mm ( 0.505 in.) and gauge length of 50.80 mm (2.000 in.) is pulled in tension until fracture occurs. The diameter at the point of fracture is 8.13 mm ( 0.320 in.), and the fractured gauge length is 74.17 mm (2.920 in.). Calculate the ductility in terms of percent reduction in area and percent elongation. Give your answer as a tuple: (percent reduction, percent elongation).
solution:
This problem calls for the computation of ductility in both percent reduction in area and percent elongation. Percent reduction in area, for a cylindrical specimen, is computed using equation (\% \mathrm{RA}=\left(\frac{A_0-A_f}{A_0}\right) \times 100) as

$$
\% \mathrm{RA}=\left(\frac{A{0}-A{f}}{A{0}}\right) \times 100=\frac{\pi\left(\frac{d{0}}{2}\right)^{2}-\pi\left(\frac{d{f}}{2}\right)^{2}}{\pi\left(\frac{d{0}}{2}\right)^{2}} \times 100
$$

in which $d{0}$ and $d{f}$ are, respectively, the original and fracture cross-sectional areas. Thus,

$$
\% \mathrm{RA}=\frac{\pi\left(\frac{12.8 \mathrm{~mm}}{2}\right)^{2}-\pi\left(\frac{8.13 \mathrm{~mm}}{2}\right)^{2}}{\pi\left(\frac{12.8 \mathrm{~mm}}{2}\right)^{2}} \times 100=60 \%
$$

While, for percent elongation, we use equation (\% \mathrm{EL}=\left(\frac{l_f-l_0}{l_0}\right) \times 100) as

$$
\begin{gathered}
\% \mathrm{EL}=\left(\frac{l{f}-l{0}}{l{0}}\right) \times 100 \\
=\frac{74.17 \mathrm{~mm}-50.80 \mathrm{~mm}}{50.80 \mathrm{~mm}} \times 100=46 \%
\end{gathered}
$$

solution:

$$
U{r}=\frac{\sigma{y}^{2}}{2 E}

Yield strength values are provided in this table, whereas the elastic moduli are tabulated in  table 7.1 .

$$
\begin{gathered}
U{r}=\frac{(830 \mathrm{MPa})^{2}}{(2)(207 \mathrm{GPa})}=\frac{\left(830 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}\right)^{2}}{(2)\left(207 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}\right)} \\

$$
\begin{gathered}
U{r}=\frac{(380 \mathrm{MPa})}{(2)(97 \mathrm{GPa})}=\frac{\left(380 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}\right)^{2}}{(2)\left(97 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}\right)} \\

\end{gathered}

For the aluminum alloy $(E=69 \mathrm{GPa})$

$$
\begin{gathered}
U{r}=\frac{(275 \mathrm{MPa})^{2}}{(2)(69 \mathrm{GPa})}=\frac{\left(275 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}\right)^{2}}{(2)\left(69 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}\right)} \\
=5.48 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}=5.48 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{m}^{3} \quad\left(80.0 \mathrm{in} .-\mathrm{lb}{\Gamma} / \mathrm{in} .^{3}\right)
\end{gathered}
$$

And, for the titanium alloy ( $E=107 \mathrm{GPa}$ )

$$
\begin{gathered}
U{r}=\frac{(690 \mathrm{MPa})^{2}}{(2)(107 \mathrm{GPa})}=\frac{\left(690 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}\right)^{2}}{(2)\left(107 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}\right)} \\
=2.22 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}=2.22 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{m}^{3} \quad\left(323 \mathrm{in} .-\mathrm{lb}{\Gamma} / \mathrm{in} .^{3}\right)

$$

question: A steel alloy to be used for a spring application must have a modulus of resilience of at least 2.07 MPa (300 psi).  If the Young’s modulus of the steel is 207 GPa, what minimum yield strength must it have?
solution:
The modulus of resilience, yield strength, and elastic modulus of elasticity are related to one another through

$$
U{r}=\frac{\sigma{y}^{2}}{2 E}
$$

Solving for $\sigma{y}$ from this expression yields

$$
\sigma{y}=\sqrt{2 U{r} E}
$$

The value of $E$ for steel is 207 GPa . Using this elastic modulus value leads to a yield strength of

$$
\begin{gathered}
\sigma{y}=\sqrt{2 U{r} E}=\sqrt{(2)(2.07 \mathrm{MPa})\left(207 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{MPa}\right)} \\
=926 \mathrm{MPa}(134,000 \mathrm{psi})
\end{gathered}
$$

question: Using data(The modulus of elasticity for 17-4PH stainless steel is 196 GPa ( $196 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{MPa}$ ). Furthermore, the yield strength for this annealed stainless steel is 760 MPa.), estimate the modulus of restlience (in MPa) of annealed 17-4PH stainless steel.
solution:
To solve this problem it is necessary to use equation (U_r=\frac{1}{2} \sigma_y \varepsilon_y=\frac{1}{2} \sigma_y\left(\frac{\sigma_y}{E}\right)=\frac{\sigma_y^2}{2 E})—viz.

$$
U{r}=\frac{\sigma{y}^{2}}{2 E}
$$

The modulus of elasticity for 17-4PH stainless steel is 196 GPa ( $196 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{MPa}$ ). Furthermore, the yield strength for this annealed stainless steel is 760 MPa. Insertion of these two values into equation (U_r=\frac{1}{2} \sigma_y \varepsilon_y=\frac{1}{2} \sigma_y\left(\frac{\sigma_y}{E}\right)=\frac{\sigma_y^2}{2 E}) leads to the following:

$$
U{r}=\frac{(760 \mathrm{MPa})^{2}}{(2)\left(196 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{MPa}\right)}=1.47 \mathrm{MPa}
$$

question: A tensile test is performed on a metal specimen, and it is found that a true plastic strain of 0.16 is produced when a true stress of 500 MPa (72,500 psi) is applied; for the same metal, the value of $K$ is 825 MPa (120,000 psi). Calculate the true strain that results from the application of a true stress of 600 MPa (87,000 psi).
solution:
We are asked to compute the true strain that results from the application of a true stress of 600 MPa (87,000 psi ); other true stress-strain data are also given. It first becomes necessary to solve for $n$ in equation (\sigma_T=K \varepsilon_T^n). Taking logarithms of both sides of this expression leads to

$$
\log \sigma{T}=\log K+n \log \varepsilon{T}
$$

Next we rearrange this equation such that $n$ is the dependent variable:

$$
n=\frac{\log \sigma{T}-\log K}{\log \varepsilon{T}}
$$

We now solve for $n$ using the following data given in the problem statement:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \sigma{T}=500 \mathrm{MPa} \\
& \varepsilon{T}=0.16 \\
& K=825 \mathrm{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus

$$
n=\frac{\log (500 \mathrm{MPa})-\log (825 \mathrm{MPa})}{\log (0.16)}=0.273
$$

We now rearrange equation (\sigma_T=K \varepsilon_T^n) such that $\varepsilon{T}$ is the dependent variable; we first divide both sides of the equation (\sigma_T=K \varepsilon_T^n) by $K$, which leads to the following expression:

$$
\varepsilon{T}^{\alpha}=\frac{\sigma{T}}{K}
$$

$\varepsilon{T}$ becomes the dependent variable by taking the $\frac{1}{n}$ root of both sides of this expression, as

$$
\varepsilon{T}=\left(\frac{\sigma{T}}{K}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}}
$$

Finally, using values of $K$ and $n$, we solve for the true strain at a true stress of 600 MPa :

$$
\varepsilon{T}=\left(\frac{\sigma{T}}{K}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}}=\left(\frac{600 \mathrm{MPa}}{825 \mathrm{MPa}}\right)^{\frac{1}{0}.273}=0.311
$$

question: For some metal alloy, a true stress of 345 MPa (50,000 psi) produces a plastic true strain of 0.02 . How much does a specimen of this material elongate when a true stress of 415 MPa (60,000 psi) is applied if the original length is 500 mm (20 in.)? Assume a value of 0.22 for the strain-hardening exponent, $n$.
solution:
We are asked to compute how much elongation a metal specimen will experience when a true stress of 415 MPa is applied, given the value of $n$ and that a given true stress produces a specific true strain. Solution of this problem requires that we utilize equation (\sigma_T=K \varepsilon_T^n). It is first necessary to solve for $K$ from the given true stress and strain. Rearrangement of this equation (\sigma_T=K \varepsilon_T^n) so that $K$ is the dependent parameter yields

$$
K=\frac{\sigma_{T}}{\left(\varepsilon_{T}\right)^{n}}
$$

The value of $K$ is determined by substitution of the first set of data in the problem statement, as follows:

$$
K=\frac{345 \mathrm{MPa}}{(0.02)^{0.22}}=816 \mathrm{MPa}(118,000 \mathrm{psi})
$$

Next we must solve for the true strain produced when a true stress of 415 MPa is applied, also using equation (\sigma_T=K \varepsilon_T^n). Algebraic manipulation of equation (\sigma_T=K \varepsilon_T^n) such that true strain is the dependent variable leads to

$$
\varepsilon_{T}=\left(\frac{\sigma_{T}}{K}\right)^{1 / n}
$$

Incorporation into this expression the value of $K$ and the applied true stress ( 415 MPa ) results in the following value for true strain:

$$
\varepsilon_{T}=\left(\frac{415 \mathrm{MPa}}{816 \mathrm{MPa}}\right)^{1 / 0.22}=0.0463
$$

And from the definition of true strain, equation (\varepsilon_T=\ln \frac{l_i}{l_0}), we have

$$
\varepsilon_{T}=\ln \left(\frac{l_{i}}{l_{0}}\right)=0.0463
$$

Another way to write this expression is as follows:

$$
e^{0.0463}=\frac{l_{i}}{l_{0}}
$$

Now, solving for $l_{i}$ gives

$$
l_{i}=l_{0} e^{0.0463}=(500 \mathrm{~mm}) e^{0.0463}=523.7 \mathrm{~mm} \text { (20.948 in.) }
$$

And, since $l_{0}=500 \mathrm{~mm}$, we solve for $\Delta l$ as follows:

$$
\Delta l=l_{i}-l_{0}=523.7 \mathrm{~mm}-500 \mathrm{~mm}=23.7 \mathrm{~mm} \text { (0.948 in.) }
$$

question:
The following true stresses produce the corresponding true plastic strains for a brass alloy:

| True Stress (psi) | True Strain |
| :--: | :--: |
| 60,000 | 0.15 |
| 70,000 | 0.25 |

What true stress is necessary to produce a true plastic strain of 0.21 ?
solution:
For this problem, we are given two values of $\varepsilon_{T}$ and $\sigma_{T}$, from which we are asked to calculate the true stress that produces a true plastic strain of 0.21 . From equation (\sigma_T=K \varepsilon_T^n), we want to set up two simultaneous equations with two unknowns (the unknowns being $K$ and $n$ ). Taking logarithms of both sides of equation (\sigma_T=K \varepsilon_T^n) leads to

$$
\log \sigma_{T}=\log K+n \log \varepsilon_{T}
$$

The two simultaneous equations using data provided in the problem statement are as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \log (60,000 \mathrm{psi})=\log K+n \log (0.15) \\
& \log (70,000 \mathrm{psi})=\log K+n \log (0.25)
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $n$ from these two expressions yields

$$
n=\frac{\log (60,000)-\log (70,000)}{\log (0.15)-\log (0.25)}=0.302
$$

We solve for $K$ by substitution of this value of $n$ into the first simultaneous equation as

$$
\begin{gathered}
\log K=\log \sigma_{T}-n \log \varepsilon_{T} \\
=\log (60,000)-(0.302)[\log (0.15)]=5.027
\end{gathered}
$$

Thus, the value of $K$ is equal to

$$
K=10^{5.027}=106,400 \mathrm{psi}
$$

The true stress required to produce a true strain of 0.21 is determined using equation (\sigma_T=K \varepsilon_T^n) as follows:

$$
\sigma_{T}=K\left(\varepsilon_{T}\right)^{n}=(106,400 \mathrm{psi})(0.21)^{0.302}=66,400 \mathrm{psi}(460 \mathrm{MPa})
$$

question:
For a brass alloy, the following engineering stresses produce the corresponding plastic engineering strains prior to necking:

| Engineering Stress (MPa) | Engineering Strain |
| :--: | :--: |
| 315 | 0.105 |
| 340 | 0.220 |

On the basis of this information, compute the engineering stress necessary to produce an engineering strain of 0.28 .
solution:
This problem is solved using the following steps:
Step 1
Convert the two engineering stress values to true stress values using equation (\sigma_T=\sigma(1+\varepsilon)).
Convert the two engineering strain values to true strain values using equation (\varepsilon_T=\ln (1+\varepsilon)).
Step 2
Next determine values of $n$ and $K$ in equation (\sigma_T=K \varepsilon_T^n). This is accomplished by taking logarithms of both sides of equation (\sigma_T=K \varepsilon_T^n), and then by setting up two simultaneous equations of this form using true stress and true strain values determined in Step 1.

Step 3
Convert the 0.28 engineering strain to true strain.
Step 4
Determine the true stress required to produce the true strain value determined in Step 3 using equation (\sigma_T=K \varepsilon_T^n) and the values the $n$ and $K$ values determined in Step 2 as well as the true strain value computed in Step 3.

Step 5
Convert the true stress determined in Step 4 to engineering stress using equation (\sigma_T=\sigma(1+\varepsilon)).

These steps are carried out as follows:
Step 1
Since $\sigma_{T}=\sigma(1+\varepsilon)$ (equation (\sigma_T=\sigma(1+\varepsilon))), we convert the two values of engineering stress into true stresses as

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \sigma_{T 1}=(315 \mathrm{MPa})(1+0.105)=348 \mathrm{MPa} \\
& \sigma_{T 2}=(340 \mathrm{MPa})(1+0.220)=415 \mathrm{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly for strains-we convert engineering strains to true strains using equation (\varepsilon_T=\ln (1+\varepsilon)) [i.e., $\varepsilon_{T}=\ln (1+\varepsilon)]$ as

$$
\begin{gathered}
\varepsilon_{T 1}=\ln (1+0.105)=0.09985 \\
\varepsilon_{T 2}=\ln (1+0.220)=0.19885
\end{gathered}
$$

# Step 2 

Taking logarithms of both sides of equation (\sigma_T=K \varepsilon_T^n) leads to

$$
\log \sigma_{T}=\log K+n \log \varepsilon_{T}
$$

which allows us to set up two simultaneous equations for the above pairs of true stresses and true strains, with $K$ and $n$ as unknowns. Thus

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \log (348)=\log K+n \log (0.09985) \\
& \log (415)=\log K+n \log (0.19885)
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $n$ from these two expressions yields

$$
n=\frac{\log (348)-\log (415)}{\log (0.09985)-\log (0.19885)}=0.256
$$

We solve for $K$ by substitution of this value of $n$ into the first simultaneous equation as

$$
\begin{gathered}
\log K=\log \sigma_{T}-n \log \varepsilon_{T} \\
=\log (348)-(0.256)[\log (0.09985)]=2.7977
\end{gathered}
$$

Thus, the value of $K$ is equal to

$$
K=10^{2.7977}=628 \mathrm{MPa}
$$

## Step 3

We now, convert $\varepsilon=0.28$ to true strain using equation (\varepsilon_T=\ln (1+\varepsilon)) as follows:

$$
\varepsilon_{T}=\ln (1+0.28)=0.247
$$

# Step 4 

Using values of $n$ and $K$ from Step 2, the $\sigma_{T}$ required to produce the $\varepsilon_{T}$ from Step 3 (using equation (\sigma_T=K \varepsilon_T^n)) is just

$$
\sigma_{T}=K \varepsilon_{T}^{n}=(628 \mathrm{MPa})(0.247)^{0.256}=439 \mathrm{MPa}
$$

## Step 5

Finally, this value of $\sigma_{T}$ is converted to engineering stress using a rearranged form of equation (\sigma_T=\sigma(1+\varepsilon)) as

$$
\sigma=\frac{\sigma_{T}}{1+\varepsilon}=\frac{439 \mathrm{MPa}}{1+0.28}=343 \mathrm{MPa}
$$

question: Find the toughness (or energy to cause fracture) for a metal that experiences both elastic and plastic deformation. Assume equation (\sigma=E \varepsilon) for elastic deformation, that the modulus of elasticity is 103 GPa ( $15 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{psi}$ ), and that elastic deformation terminates at a strain of 0.007 . For plastic deformation, assume that the relationship between stress and strain is described by equation (\sigma_T=K \varepsilon_T^n), in which the values for $K$ and $n$ are 1520 MPa (221,000 psi) and 0.15 , respectively. Furthermore, plastic deformation occurs between strain values of 0.007 and 0.60 , at which point fracture occurs.
solution:
This problem calls for us to compute the toughness (or energy to cause fracture). This toughness is equal to the area beneath the entire stress-strain curve. The easiest way to make this computation is to perform integrations in both elastic and plastic regions using data given in the problem statement, and then add these values together. Thus, we may define toughness using the following equation:

$$
\text { Toughness }=\int \sigma d \varepsilon
$$

Toughness in the elastic region is determined by integration of equation (\sigma=E \varepsilon), integrating between strains of 0 and 0.007 (per the problem statement). Thus

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Toughness (elastic) }=\int{0}^{0.007} E \varepsilon d \varepsilon \\
& =\frac{E \varepsilon^{2}}{2} \left\lvert\, \begin{array}{c}
0.007 \\
0
\end{array}\right. \\
& =\frac{103 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}}{2}\left[(0.007)^{2}-(0)^{2}\right] \\
& =2.52 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}=2.52 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now for the plastic region of the stress-strain curve, we integrate equation (\sigma_T=K \varepsilon_T^n) between the strain limits of 0.007 and 0.60 , as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Toughness }(\text { plastic })=\int{c{0}^{\text {min }}}^{c{\text {max }}} K \varepsilon^{n} d \varepsilon \\
& =\frac{K}{(n+1)} \varepsilon^{(n+1)}\left\lvert\, \begin{array}{l}
0.60 \\
0.007
\end{array}\right. \\
& =\frac{1520 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}}{(1.0+0.15)}\left[(0.60)^{1.15}-(0.007)^{1.15}\right] \\
& =730 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}=7.30 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

And, finally, the total toughness is the sum of elastic and plastic values, which is equal to

$$
\begin{gathered}
\text { Toughness }=\text { Toughness }(\text { elastic })+\text { Toughness }(\text { plastic }) \\
=2.52 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{m}^{3}+730 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{m}^{3} \\
=733 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{m}^{3}=7.33 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{m}^{3} \quad\left(1.07 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{in} .-\mathrm{lb}{\mathrm{f}} / \mathrm{in} .^{3}\right)
\end{gathered}
$$

For a tensile test, it can be demonstrated that necking begins when

$$
\frac{d \sigma{T}}{d \varepsilon{T}}=\sigma{T}
$$

Using equation (\sigma_T=K \varepsilon_T^n), determine an expression for the value of the true strain at this onset of necking.

question:
We have the following engineering stress–strain data for a tensile test on a ductile metal:

| Point | σ (MPa) | ε (in-drawn) |
|–|–:|–:|
| 1 | 100 | 0.000 |
| 2 | 250 | 0.005 |
| 3 | 400 | 0.010 |
| … | … | … |
| 8 |  797 | 0.0075 |
| 9 |  838 | 0.0100 |
| 10|  928 | 0.0150 |
| 11|  997 | 0.0200 |
| 12| 1163 | 0.0400 |
| 13| 1236 | 0.0600 |

For true strain, determine n and K in σₜ = K εₜⁿ.

Give your answer as a tuple (n, k).
solution:
This problem calls for us to utilize the appropriate data from Problem 7.30 in order to determine the values of $n$ and $K$ for this material. From equation (\log \sigma_T=\log K+n \log \varepsilon_T) the slope of a $\log \sigma{T}$ versus $\log \epsilon{T}$ plot will yield $n$. However, equation (\sigma_T=K \varepsilon_T^n) is only valid in the region of plastic deformation to the point of necking; thus, only the 8th, 9th, 10th, 11th, 12th, and 13th data points may be utilized. These data are tabulated below:

| $\sigma(\mathrm{MPa})$ | $\sigma{T}(\mathrm{MPa})$ | $\log \sigma{T}$ | $\varepsilon$ | $\epsilon{T}$ | $\log \epsilon{T}$ |
| :--: | :--: | :--: | :--: | :--: | :--: |
| 797 | 803 | 2.905 | 0.0075 | $7.47 \times 10^{-3}$ | -2.127 |
| 838 | 846 | 2.927 | 0.010 | $9.95 \times 10^{-3}$ | -2.002 |
| 928 | 942 | 2.974 | 0.015 | $1.488 \times 10^{-2}$ | -1.827 |
| 997 | 1017 | 3.007 | 0.020 | $1.980 \times 10^{-2}$ | -1.703 |
| 1163 | 1210 | 3.083 | 0.040 | $3.92 \times 10^{-2}$ | -1.407 |
| 1236 | 1310 | 3.117 | 0.060 | $5.83 \times 10^{-2}$ | -1.234 |

The $\log -\log$ plot with these data points is given below.



From equation (\log \sigma_T=\log K+n \log \varepsilon_T), the value of $n$ is equal to the slope of the line that has been drawn through the data points; that is

$$
n=\frac{\Delta \log \sigma{T}}{\Delta \log \varepsilon{T}}
$$

From this line, values of $\log \sigma{T}$ corresponding to $\log \varepsilon{T}(1)=-2.20$ and $\log \varepsilon{T}(2)=-1.20$ are, respectively, $\log \sigma{T}$
$(1)=2.886$ and $\log \sigma{T}(2)=3.131$. Therefore, we determine the value of $n$ as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
n=\frac{\Delta \log \sigma{T}}{\Delta \log \varepsilon{T}}=\frac{\log \sigma{T}(1)-\log \sigma{T}(2)}{\log \varepsilon{T}(1)-\log \varepsilon{T}(2)} \\
=\frac{2.886-3.131}{-2.20-(-1.20)}=0.245
\end{gathered}
$$

In order to determine the value of $K$ we rearrange equation (\log \sigma_T=\log K+n \log \varepsilon_T) to read as follows:

$$
\log K=\log \sigma{T}-n \log \varepsilon{T}
$$

It is possible to compute $\log K$ by inserting into this equation the value for a specific $\log \sigma{T}$ and its corresponding $\log \varepsilon{T}$. (Of course we have already calculated the value of $n$.) If we use $\log \sigma{T}(1)$ and $\log \varepsilon{T}(1)$ values from above, then $K$ is determined as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
\log K=\log \sigma{T}(1)-n \log \varepsilon{T}(1) \\
$=2.886-(0.245)(-2.20)=3.425$
\end{gathered}
$$

Which means that

$$
K=10^{3.425}=2660 \mathrm{MPa}
$$

question:
A cylindrical specimen of a brass alloy 10.0 mm (0.39 in.) in diameter and 120.0 mm (4.72 in.) long is pulled in tension with a force of $11,750 \mathrm{~N}(2640 \mathrm{lb})$; the force is subsequently released.
(a) Compute the final length of the specimen at this time. The tensile stress-strain behavior for this alloy is shown in  figure 7.12 .
(b) Compute the final specimen length when the load is increased to 23,500 N (5280 lb) and then released.

Give your answer as a tuple (answer to (a), answer to (b)).
solution:
(a) In order to determine the final length of the brass specimen when the load is released, it first becomes necessary to compute the applied stress using equation (\sigma=\frac{F}{A_0}); thus

$$
\sigma=\frac{F}{4{0}}=\frac{F}{\pi\left(\frac{d{0}}{2}\right)^{2}}=\frac{11,750 \mathrm{~N}}{\pi\left(\frac{10 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}}{2}\right)^{2}}=150 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}=150 \mathrm{MPa}(22,000 \mathrm{psi})
$$

Upon locating this point on the stress-strain curve ( figure 7.12 ), we note that it is in the linear, elastic region; therefore, when the load is released the specimen will return to its original length of 120 mm ( 4.72 in .).
(b) In this portion of the problem we are asked to calculate the final length, after load release, when the load is increased to $23,500 \mathrm{~N}\left(5280 \mathrm{lb}{\mathrm{f}}\right)$. Again, computing the stress

$$
\sigma=\frac{23,500 \mathrm{~N}}{\pi\left(\frac{10 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}}{2}\right)^{2}}=300 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}=300 \mathrm{MPa}(44,200 \mathrm{psi})
$$

The point on the stress-strain curve corresponding to this stress is in the plastic region. We are able to estimate the amount of permanent strain by drawing a straight line parallel to the linear elastic region; this line intersects the strain axis at a strain of about 0.012 , which is the amount of plastic strain. The final specimen length $l{f}$ may be determined from a rearranged form of equation (\varepsilon=\frac{l_i-l_0}{l_0}=\frac{\Delta l}{l_0}) as

$$
l{f}=l{0}(1+\varepsilon)=(120 \mathrm{~mm})(1+0.012)=121.44 \mathrm{~mm} \text { (4.78 in.) }
$$

This specimen is subjected to a tensile force of 110,000 N $\left(25,000 \mathrm{lb}{\mathrm{f}}\right)$.
(a) Determine the elastic and plastic strain values.
(b) If its original length is 610 mm (24.0 in.), what will be its final length after the load in part (a) is applied and then released?

Give your answer as a tuple (elastic strains, plastic strains, final length)

solution:
(a) We are asked to determine both the elastic and plastic strain values when a tensile force of $110,000 \mathrm{~N}$ $\left(25,000 \mathrm{lb}{\mathrm{f}}\right)$ is applied to the steel specimen and then released. First it becomes necessary to determine the applied stress using equation (\sigma=\frac{F}{A_0}); thus

$$
\sigma=\frac{F}{A{0}}=\frac{F}{b{0} d{0}}
$$

where $b{0}$ and $d{0}$ are cross-sectional width and depth $\left[19 \mathrm{~mm}\left(19 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)\right.$ and $3.2 \mathrm{~mm}\left(3.2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)$, respectively). Thus

$$
\sigma=\frac{110,000 \mathrm{~N}}{\left(19 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)\left(3.2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)}=1.810 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}=1810 \mathrm{MPa}(265,000 \mathrm{psi})
$$

From  figure 7.34 , this point is in the plastic region so the specimen will be both elastic and plastic strains. The total strain at this point, $\varepsilon{\mathrm{r}}$, is about 0.020 . From this point on the stress-strain curve we are able to estimate the amount of strain recovery $\varepsilon{e}$ from Hooke's law, equation (\sigma=E \varepsilon) as

$$
\varepsilon{e}=\frac{\sigma}{E}
$$

And, since $E=207$ GPa for steel ( table 7.1 )

$$
\varepsilon{e}=\frac{1810 \mathrm{MPa}}{207 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{MPa}}=0.0087
$$

The value of the plastic strain, $\varepsilon{p}$ is just the difference between the total and elastic strains; that is

$$
\varepsilon{p}=\varepsilon{t}-\varepsilon{e}=0.020-0.0087=0.0113
$$

(b) If the initial length is 610 mm ( 24.0 in.) then the final specimen length $l{i}$ may be determined from a rearranged form of equation (\varepsilon=\frac{l_i-l_0}{l_0}=\frac{\Delta l}{l_0}) using the plastic strain value as

$$
l{i}=l{0}\left(1+\varepsilon{p}\right)=(610 \mathrm{~mm})(1+0.0113)=616.9 \mathrm{~mm}(24.26 \mathrm{in} .)
$$

A three-point bending test is performed on a spinel $\left(\mathrm{MgAl}{2} \mathrm{O}{4}\right)$ specimen having a rectangular cross section of height $d=3.8 \mathrm{~mm}$ ( 0.15 in.) and width $b=9 \mathrm{~mm}$ ( 0.35 in.); the distance between support points is 25 mm (1.0 in.).
(a) Compute the flexural strength if the load at fracture is $350 \mathrm{~N}(80 \mathrm{lb})$.
(b) The point of maximum deflection $\Delta y$ occurs at the center of the specimen and is described by

$$
\Delta y=\frac{F L^{3}}{48 E I}
$$

where $E$ is the modulus of elasticity and $I$ is the cross-sectional moment of inertia. Compute $\Delta y$ at a load of 310 N $\left(70 \mathrm{lb}{f}\right)$.

Give your answer as a tuple (the flexural strength in (a), $\Delta y$ in (b)).
