solution:
(a) For this portion of the problem we are asked to compute the flexural strength for a spinel specimen that is subjected to a three-point bending test. The flexural strength for a rectangular cross-section (equation (\sigma_{f s}=\frac{3 F_f L}{2 b d^2})) is just

$$
\sigma{f s}=\frac{3 F{f} L}{2 b d^{2}}
$$

Incorporating values for the parameters in this equation that are provided in the problem statement, gives the following flexural strength:

$$
\sigma{f s}=\frac{(3)(350 \mathrm{~N})\left(25 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)}{(2)\left(9.0 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)\left(3.8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)^{2}}=101 \mathrm{MPa} \quad(15,200 \mathrm{psi})
$$

(b) We are now asked to compute the maximum deflection using equation (\Delta y=\frac{F L^3}{48 E I}). From  table 7.1 , the elastic modulus $(E)$ for spinel is $260 \mathrm{GPa}\left(38 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{psi}\right)$. Also, the moment of inertia for a rectangular cross section is just

$$
I=\frac{b d^{3}}{12}
$$

Incorporating this expression for $I$ into equation (\Delta y=\frac{F L^3}{48 E I}) and also the value for $E$, and values of $F, L, b$, and $d$ given in the problem statement $\left[310 \mathrm{~N}, 25 \mathrm{~mm}\left(25 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right), 9 \mathrm{~mm}\left(9 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)\right.$, and $\left.3.8 \mathrm{~mm}\left(3.8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)\right.$, respectively] the value of $\Delta y$ is computed as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
\Delta y=\frac{F L^{3}}{48 E\left(\frac{b d^{3}}{12}\right)}=\frac{F L^{3}}{4 E b d^{3}} \\
=\frac{(310 \mathrm{~N})\left(25 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)^{3}}{(4)\left(260 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}\right)\left(9.0 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)\left(3.8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)^{3}} \\
=9.4 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}=9.4 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mm} \quad\left(3.9 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{in}\right)
\end{gathered}
$$

question: A circular specimen of MgO is loaded using a three-point bending mode. Compute the minimum possible radius of the specimen without fracture, given that the applied load is $5560 \mathrm{~N}\left(1250 \mathrm{lb}{\mathrm{f}}\right)$, the flexural strength is 105 MPa (15,000 psi), and the separation between load points is 45 mm (1.75 in.).
solution:
We are asked to calculate the maximum radius of a circular specimen of MgO that is loaded using threepoint bending. Solving for the specimen radius, $R$, from equation (\sigma_{f s}=\frac{F_f L}{\pi R^3}) leads to the following:

$$
R=\left[\frac{F{f} L}{\sigma{f s} \pi}\right]^{\frac{1}{3}}
$$

which, when substituting the parameters stipulated in the problem statement, yields

$$
\begin{gathered}
R=\left[\frac{(5560 N)\left(45 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)}{\left(105 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}\right)(\pi)}\right]^{\frac{1}{3}} \\
=9.1 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}=9.1 \mathrm{~mm} \quad(0.36 \mathrm{in} .)
\end{gathered}
$$

Thus, the minimum allowable specimen radius is $9.1 \mathrm{~mm}(0.36 \mathrm{in}$.)

question: A three-point bending test was performed on an aluminum oxide specimen having a circular cross section of radius 5.0 mm ( 0.20 in.); the specimen fractured at a load of $3000 \mathrm{~N}\left(675 \mathrm{lb}\right.$ ) when the distance between the support points was 40 mm (1.6 in.). Another test is to be performed on a specimen of this same material, but one that has a square cross section of 15 mm ( 0.6 in.) length on each edge. At what load would you expect this specimen to fracture if the support point separation is maintained at 40 mm (1.6 in.)?
solution:
For this problem, the load is given at which a circular specimen of aluminum oxide fractures when subjected to a three-point bending test; we are then are asked to determine the load at which a specimen of the same material having a square cross-section fractures. It is first necessary to compute the flexural strength of the cylindrical specimen from equation (\sigma_{f s}=\frac{F_f L}{\pi R^3}); then, using this value, calculate the value of $F{f}$ in equation (\sigma_{f s}=\frac{3 F_f L}{2 b d^2}) (for the specimen having a square cross-section). From equation (\sigma_{f s}=\frac{F_f L}{\pi R^3}), and using the first set of data provided in the problem statement, we compute the flexural strength as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
\sigma{f s}=\frac{F{f} L}{\pi R^{3}} \\
=\frac{(3000 \mathrm{~N})\left(40 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)}{(\pi)\left(5.0 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)^{3}}=306 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}=306 \mathrm{MPa} \quad(42,970 \mathrm{psi})
\end{gathered}
$$

Now, solving for $F{f}$ for the second specimen using equation (\sigma_{f s}=\frac{3 F_f L}{2 b d^2}), realizing that $b=d=15 \mathrm{~mm}\left(15 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)$, yields

$$
\begin{gathered}
F{f}=\frac{2 \sigma{f s} d^{3}}{3 L} \\
=\frac{(2)\left(306 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}\right)\left(15 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)^{3}}{(3)\left(40 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)}=17,200 \mathrm{~N} \quad\left(3870 \mathrm{lb}{\mathrm{f}}\right)
\end{gathered}
$$

question: A three-point transverse bending test is conducted on a cylindrical specimen of aluminum oxide having a reported flexural strength of 300 MPa (43,500 psi). If the specimen radius is 5.0 mm ( 0.20 in .) and the support point separation distance is 15.0 mm ( 0.61 in .), would you expect the specimen to fracture when a load of 7500 N (1690 lb) is applied? Justify your answer.
solution:
(a) This portion of the problem asks that we determine whether or not a cylindrical specimen of aluminum oxide having a flexural strength of 300 MPa and a radius of 5.0 mm will fracture when subjected to a load of 7500 N in a three-point bending test; the support point separation is given as 15.0 mm . Using equation (\sigma_{f s}=\frac{F_f L}{\pi R^3}) we will calculate the value of $\sigma$, in this case the flexural stress; if this value is greater than the flexural strength, $\sigma{f s}$ (300 MPa ), then fracture is expected to occur. Employment of equation (\sigma_{f s}=\frac{F_f L}{\pi R^3}) to compute the flexural stress yields

$$
\begin{gathered}
\sigma=\frac{F L}{\pi R^{3}}=\frac{(7500 \mathrm{~N})\left(15 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)}{(\pi)\left(5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)^{3}} \\
=286.5 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}=286.5 \mathrm{MPa} \quad(40,300 \mathrm{psi})
\end{gathered}
$$

Since this value ( 286.5 MPa ) is less than the given value of $\sigma{f s}(300 \mathrm{MPa})$, then fracture is not predicted.

question:
Use the empirical relation  
 E = E₀·(1 – 1.9 P + 0.9 P²)  
to relate the Young’s modulus E of a ceramic with porosity P (vol fraction) to the modulus E₀ of the fully dense (nonporous) material. For spinel (MgAl₂O₄) with 5 vol % porosity (P=0.05) the measured modulus is 240 GPa.  
(a) Compute the nonporous modulus E₀.  
(b) Compute the modulus E when P=0.15 (15 vol %). 

Give your answer as a tuple (answer to (a), answer to (b)).
solution:
(a) This portion of the problem requests that we compute the modulus of elasticity for nonporous spinel given that $E=240 \mathrm{GPa}$ for a material having $5 \mathrm{vol} \%$ porosity. Thus, we solve equation (E=E_0\left(1-1.9 P+0.9 P^2\right)) for $E{0}$ (the modulus of elasticity of the nonporous material) using $P=0.05$, as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
E{0}=\frac{E}{1-1.9 P+0.9 P^{2}} \\
=\frac{240 \mathrm{GPa}}{1-(1.9)(0.05)+(0.9)(0.05)^{2}}=265 \mathrm{GPa}\left(38.6 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{psi}\right)
\end{gathered}
$$

(b) Now we are asked to determine the value of $E$ at $P=15 \mathrm{vol} \%$ (i.e., 0.15 ). Using equation (E=E_0\left(1-1.9 P+0.9 P^2\right)) the following results:

$$
\begin{gathered}
E=E{0}\left(1-1.9 P+0.9 P^{2}\right) \\
=(265 \mathrm{GPa})\left[1-(1.9)(0.15)+(0.09)(0.15)^{2}\right]=195 \mathrm{GPa}\left(28.4 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{psi}\right)
\end{gathered}
$$

question:
The modulus of elasticity for titanium carbide (TiC) having 5 vol\% porosity is 310 GPa ( $45 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{psi}$ ).
(a) Compute the modulus of elasticity for the nonporous material.
(b) At what volume percent porosity will the modulus of elasticity be $240 \mathrm{GPa}\left(35 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{psi}\right)$ ?

Give your answer a s tuple: (answer to (a), answer to (b)).
solution:
(a) This portion of the problem requests that we compute the modulus of elasticity for nonporous TiC given that $E=310 \mathrm{GPa}\left(45 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{psi}\right)$ for a material having $5 \mathrm{vol} \%$ porosity. Thus, we solve equation (E=E_0\left(1-1.9 P+0.9 P^2\right)) for $E_{0}$ (the modulus of elasticity of the nonporous material) using $P=0.05$, as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
E_{0}=\frac{E}{1-1.9 P+0.9 P^{2}} \\
=\frac{310 \mathrm{GPa}}{1-(1.9)(0.05)+(0.9)(0.05)^{2}}=342 \mathrm{GPa}\left(49.6 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{psi}\right)
\end{gathered}
$$

(b) Now we are asked to compute the volume percent porosity at which the elastic modulus of TiC is 240 MPa ( $35 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{psi}$ ). Since from part (a), $E_{0}=342 \mathrm{GPa}$, and using equation (E=E_0\left(1-1.9 P+0.9 P^2\right)) , we may write the following:

$$
\frac{E}{E_{0}}=\frac{240 \mathrm{GPa}}{342 \mathrm{GPa}}=0.702=1-1.9 P+0.9 P^{2}
$$

Or, after rearrangement the following expression results:

$$
0.9 P^{2}-1.9 P+0.298=0
$$

Now, solving for the value of $P$ using the quadratic equation solution yields

$$
P=\frac{1.9 \pm \sqrt{(-1.9)^{2}-(4)(0.9)(0.298)}}{(2)(0.9)}
$$

The positive and negative roots are as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& P^{+}=1.94 \\
& P^{-}=0.171
\end{aligned}
$$

Obviously, only the negative root is physically meaningful, and therefore the value of the porosity to give the desired modulus of elasticity is 17.1 vol $\%$.

question:
The flexural strength and associated volume fraction porosity for two specimens of the same ceramic material are as follows:

| $\sigma{f s}(\mathrm{MPa})$ | $\boldsymbol{P}$ |
| :-- | :-- |
| 70 | 0.10 |
| 60 | 0.15 |

(a) Compute the flexural strength for a completely nonporous specimen of this material.
(b) Compute the flexural strength for a 0.20 volume fraction porosity.

Give your answer as a tuple (answer to (a), answer to (b)).
solution:
(a) Given the flexural strengths at two different volume fraction porosities, we are asked to determine the flexural strength for a nonporous material. Taking the natural logarithm of both sides of equation (\sigma_{f s}=\sigma_0 \exp (-n P)) yields the following expression:

$$
\ln \sigma{f s}=\ln \sigma{0}-n P
$$

Using the data provided in the problem statement, two simultaneous equations may be written in the above form as

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \ln (70 \mathrm{MPa})=\ln \sigma{0}-(0.10) n \\
& \ln (60 \mathrm{MPa})=\ln \sigma{0}-(0.15) n
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $n$ and $\sigma{0}$ leads to the following values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& n=3.08 \\
& \sigma{0}=95.3 \mathrm{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the nonporous material, $P=0$, and, from equation (\sigma_{f s}=\sigma_0 \exp (-n P)), $\sigma{0}=\sigma{f s}$. Thus, $\sigma{f s}$ for $P=0$ is 95.3 MPa .
(b) Now we are asked for $\sigma{f s}$ at $P=0.20$ for this same material. From equation (\sigma_{f s}=\sigma_0 \exp (-n P))

$$
\sigma{f s}=\sigma{0} \exp (-n P)
$$

$$
\begin{gathered}
=(95.3 \mathrm{MPa}) \exp [-(3.08)(0.20)] \\
=51.5 \mathrm{MPa}
\end{gathered}
$$

question: From the stress-strain data for poly(methyl methacrylate) shown in  figure 7.24 , determine the modulus of elasticity at room temperature $\left[20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right]$.
solution:
From  figure 7.24 , the elastic modulus is the slope in the elastic linear, or

$$
E=\frac{\Delta(\text { stress })}{\Delta(\text { strain })}=\frac{\sigma{1}-\sigma{2}}{\varepsilon{1}-\varepsilon{2}}
$$

From the plot in  figure 7.24 \left(20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right.$ curve) let us assign

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \sigma{1}=30 \mathrm{MPa} \\
& \sigma{2}=0 \mathrm{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

And their corresponding strains are

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \varepsilon{1}=9 \times 10^{-3} \\
& \varepsilon{2}=0
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, the value of the elastic modulus is determined as follows:

$$
E=\frac{\sigma{1}-\sigma{2}}{\varepsilon{1}-\varepsilon{2}}=\frac{30 \mathrm{MPa}-0 \mathrm{MPa}}{9 \times 10^{-3}-0}=3.3 \mathrm{GPa} \quad(483,000 \mathrm{psi})
$$

question:
For some viscoelastic polymers that are subjected to stress relaxation tests, the stress decays with time according to

$$
\sigma(t)=\sigma(0) \exp \left(-\frac{t}{\tau}\right)
$$

where $\sigma(t)$ and $\sigma(0)$ represent the time-dependent and initial (i.e., time $=0$ ) stresses, respectively, and $t$ and $\tau$ denote elapsed time and the relaxation time, respectively; $\tau$ is a time-independent constant characteristic of the material. A specimen of a viscoelastic polymer whose stress relaxation obeys equation (\sigma(t)=\sigma(0) \exp \left(-\frac{t}{\tau}\right)) was suddenly pulled in tension to a measured strain of 0.5 ; the stress necessary to maintain this constant strain was measured as a function of time. Determine $E_{r}(10)$ for this material if the initial stress level was 3.5 MPa ( 500 psi ), which dropped to 0.5 MPa ( 70 psi) after 30 s.
solution:
In order to determine $\sigma(10)$, it is first necessary to compute $\tau$ from the data provided in the problem statement. In order to solve for $\tau$ from equation (\sigma(t)=\sigma(0) \exp \left(-\frac{t}{\tau}\right)) it is first necessary to take natural logarithms of both sides of the equation as follows:

$$
\ln \sigma(t)=\ln \sigma(0)-\frac{t}{\tau}
$$

And solving this expression for $\tau$ leads to the following:

$$
\tau=\frac{-t}{\ln \sigma(t)-\ln \sigma(0)}=\frac{-t}{\ln \left[\frac{\sigma(t)}{\sigma(0)}\right]}
$$

The problem statement provides the following values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \sigma(0)=3.5 \mathrm{MPa} \\
& \sigma(t)=\sigma(30)=0.5 \mathrm{MPa} \\
& t=30 \mathrm{~s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the value of $\tau$ is equal to

$$
\tau=\frac{-t}{\ln \left[\frac{\sigma(t)}{\sigma(0)}\right]}
$$$$
=\frac{-30 \mathrm{~s}}{\ln \left[\frac{0.5 \mathrm{MPa}}{3.5 \mathrm{MPa}}\right]}=15.4 \mathrm{~s}
$$

Using equation (\sigma(t)=\sigma(0) \exp \left(-\frac{t}{\tau}\right)) we compute the value of $\sigma(10)$ (that is, the value of $\sigma$ when $t=10 \mathrm{~s}$ ), as follows:
$$
=\frac{-30 \mathrm{~s}}{\ln \left[\frac{0.5 \mathrm{MPa}}{3.5 \mathrm{MPa}}\right]}=15.4 \mathrm{~s}
$$

Using equation (\sigma(t)=\sigma(0) \exp \left(-\frac{t}{\tau}\right)) we compute the value of $\sigma(10)$ (that is, the value of $\sigma$ when $t=10 \mathrm{~s}$ ), as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
\sigma(10)=\sigma(0) \exp \left(-\frac{10 \mathrm{~s}}{r}\right) \\
=(3.5 \mathrm{MPa}) \exp \left(-\frac{10 \mathrm{~s}}{15.4 \mathrm{~s}}\right)=1.83 \mathrm{MPa}
\end{gathered}
$$

We now compute the value of $E_{r}(10)$ using equation (E_r(t)=\frac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon_0}) as follows:

$$
E_{r}(10)=\frac{\sigma(10)}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{1.83 \mathrm{MPa}}{0.5}=3.66 \mathrm{MPa}(522 \mathrm{psi})
$$
$$
\begin{gathered}
\sigma(10)=\sigma(0) \exp \left(-\frac{10 \mathrm{~s}}{r}\right) \\
=(3.5 \mathrm{MPa}) \exp \left(-\frac{10 \mathrm{~s}}{15.4 \mathrm{~s}}\right)=1.83 \mathrm{MPa}
\end{gathered}
$$

We now compute the value of $E_{r}(10)$ using equation (E_r(t)=\frac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon_0}) as follows:

$$
E_{r}(10)=\frac{\sigma(10)}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{1.83 \mathrm{MPa}}{0.5}=3.66 \mathrm{MPa}(522 \mathrm{psi})
$$

question:
(a) A 10-mm-diameter Brinell hardness indenter produced an indentation 2.50 mm in diameter in a steel alloy when a load of 1000 kg was used. Compute the HB of this material.
(b) What will be the diameter of an indentation to yield a hardness of 300 HB when a $500-\mathrm{kg}$ load is used?

Give your answer as a tuple (HB of this material in (a), the diameter in (b).

(a) Calculate the Knoop hardness when a 500-g load yields an indentation diagonal length of $100 \mu \mathrm{~m}$.
(b) The measured HK of some material is 200. Compute the applied load if the indentation diagonal length is 0.25 mm .

Give your answer as a tuple (the Knoop hardness in (a), the applied load in (b)).
solution:
(a) Knoop microhardness is related to the applied load ( $P$ in kg ) and indentation length ( $l$ in mm ) according to the equation given in  table 7.5  - viz.

$$
\mathrm{HK}=\frac{14.2 P}{l^{2}}
$$

The applied load is $500 \mathrm{~g}=0.500 \mathrm{~kg}$, whereas the indentation length is $100 \mu \mathrm{~m}=0.100 \mathrm{~mm}$. Incorporating these values into the equation above leads to

$$
\mathrm{HK}=\frac{(14.2)(0.500 \mathrm{~kg})}{(0.100 \mathrm{~mm})^{2}}=710
$$

(b) This portion of the problem asks for the applied load for $\mathrm{HK}=200$ and $l=0.25 \mathrm{~mm}$. Assigning $P$ in the above equation to be the dependent variable yields the following expression:

$$
P=\frac{(\mathrm{HK}) l^{2}}{14.2}
$$

When we insert the above values for HK and $l$ into this expression, the value of the $P$ is computed as follows:

$$
P=\frac{(200)(0.25 \mathrm{~mm})^{2}}{14.2}=0.880 \mathrm{~kg}
$$

question:
(a) What is the indentation diagonal length when a load of 0.60 kg produces a Vickers HV of 400?
(b) Calculate the Vickers hardness when a 700-g load yields an indentation diagonal length of 0.050 mm .

Give your answer as a tuple (the indentation diagonal length in (a), the Vickers hardness in (b)).
solution:
(a) The equation given in  table 7.5  for Vickers microhardness is as follows:

$$
\mathrm{HV}=\frac{1.854 P}{d{1}^{2}}
$$

Here $P$ is the applied load (in kg ) and $d{1}$ is the diagonal length (in mm ). This portion of the problem asks that we compute the indentation diagonal length. Rearranging the above equation to make $d{1}$ the dependent variable gives

$$
d{1}=\sqrt{\frac{1.854 P}{\mathrm{HV}}}
$$

Using values for $P$ and HV given in the problem statement (viz. 0.60 kg and 400 , respectively), yields the following value for $d{1}$ :

$$
d{1}=\sqrt{\frac{(1.854)(0.60 \mathrm{~kg})}{400}}=0.0527 \mathrm{~mm}
$$

(b) For $P=700 \mathrm{~g}(0.700 \mathrm{~kg})$ and $d{1}=0.050 \mathrm{~mm}$, the Vickers hardness value is

$$
\begin{gathered}
\mathrm{HV}=\frac{1.854 P}{d{1}^{2}} \\
=\frac{(1.854)(0.700 \mathrm{~kg})}{(0.050 \mathrm{~mm})^{2}}=519
\end{gathered}
$$

question:
The following table gives a number of Rockwell G hardness values that were measured on a single steel specimen. Compute average and standard deviation hardness values. Give your answer a tuple: (average, standard deviation).

| 47.3 | 48.7 | 47.1 |
| :-- | :-- | :-- |
| 52.1 | 50.0 | 50.4 |
| 45.6 | 46.2 | 45.9 |
| 49.9 | 48.3 | 46.4 |
| 47.6 | 51.1 | 48.5 |
| 50.4 | 46.7 | 49.7 |
solution:
The average of the given hardness values is calculated using equation (\bar{x}=\frac{\sum_{i=1}^n x_i}{n}) as

$$
\begin{gathered}
\overline{\mathrm{HRG}}=\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{18} \mathrm{HRG}_{i}}{18} \\
=\frac{47.3+52.1+45.6 \ldots+49.7}{18}=48.4
\end{gathered}
$$

And we compute the standard deviation using equation (s=\left[\frac{\sum_{i=1}^n\left(x_i-\bar{x}\right)^2}{n-1}\right]^{1 / 2}) as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
s=\sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{18}\left(\mathrm{HRG}_{i}-\overline{\mathrm{HRG}}\right)^{2}}{18-1}} \\
=\left[\frac{(47.3-48.4)^{2}+(52.1-48.4)^{2}+\ldots+(49.7-48.4)^{2}}{17}\right]^{1 / 2} \\
=\sqrt{\frac{64.95}{17}}=1.95
\end{gathered}
$$

question:
The following table gives a number of yield strength values (in MPa) that were measured on the same aluminum alloy. Compute average and standard deviation yield strength values.

| 274.3 | 277.1 | 263.8 |
| :-- | :-- | :-- |
| 267.5 | 258.6 | 271.2 |
| 255.4 | 266.9 | 257.6 |
| 270.8 | 260.1 | 264.3 |
| 261.7 | 279.4 | 260.5 |

 Give your name as a tuple (mean, average).

A large tower is to be supported by a series of steel wires; it is estimated that the load on each wire will be $13,300 \mathrm{~N}(3000 \mathrm{lb})$. Determine the minimum required wire diameter, assuming a factor of safety of 2.0 and a yield strength of $860 \mathrm{MPa}(125,000 \mathrm{psi})$ for the steel.
solution:
For this problem the working stress is computed using equation (\sigma_w=\frac{\sigma_y}{N}) with $N=2$, as

$$
\sigma_{w}=\frac{\sigma_{y}}{2}=\frac{860 \mathrm{MPa}}{2}=430 \mathrm{MPa}(62,500 \mathrm{psi})
$$

Force, original cross-sectional area, and stress (in this case working stress) are related to one another according to equation (\sigma=\frac{F}{A_0}). Rearrangement of equation (\sigma=\frac{F}{A_0}) such that cross-sectional area $\left(A_{0}\right)$ is the dependent variable leads to

$$
A_{0}=\frac{F}{\sigma_{w}}
$$

For a cylindrical wire it is the case that

$$
A_{0}=\pi\left(\frac{d_{0}}{2}\right)^{2}
$$

in which $d_{0}$ is the original cross-sectional diameter. Substitution of this expression for $A_{0}$ into equation (A_0=\frac{F}{\sigma_w}) (above) leads to the following expression:

$$
\pi\left(\frac{d_{0}}{2}\right)^{2}=\frac{F}{\sigma_{w}}
$$

And solving for $d_{0}$ gives

$$
d_{0}=\sqrt{\frac{4 F}{\pi \sigma_{w}}}
$$

Inasmuch as

$$
F=13,300 \mathrm{~N}
$$
$$
\sigma_{w}=430 \mathrm{MPa}=430 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}
$$

then

$$
\begin{gathered}
d_{0}=\sqrt{\frac{(4)(13,300 \mathrm{~N})}{\pi\left(430 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}\right)}} \\
=6.3 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}=6.3 \mathrm{~mm} \quad(0.25 \mathrm{in} .)
\end{gathered}
$$

question: Sometimes $\cos \phi \cos \lambda$ in equation (\tau_R=\sigma \cos \phi \cos \lambda) is termed the Schmid factor. Determine the magnitude of the Schmid factor for an FCC single crystal oriented with its [120] direction parallel to the loading axis.
solution:
We are asked to compute the Schmid factor for an FCC crystal oriented with its [120] direction parallel to the loading axis. With this scheme, slip may occur on the (111) plane and in the $[01 \overline{1}]$ direction as noted in the figure below.


The angle between the [120] and $[01 \overline{1}]$ directions, $\lambda$, may be determined using equation (\theta=\cos ^{-1}\left[\frac{u_1 u_2+v_1 v_2+w_1 w_2}{\sqrt{\left(u_1^2+v_1^2+w_1^2\right)\left(u_2^2+v_2^2+w_2^2\right)}}\right])

$$
\lambda=\cos ^{-1}\left[\frac{\left.\cup{1} U{2}+v{1} v{2}+w{1} w{2}\right)}{\sqrt{\left(u{1}^{2}+v{1}^{2}+w{1}^{2}\right)\left(u{2}^{2}+v{2}^{2}+w{2}^{2}\right)}}\right]
$$

where (for [120]) $u{1}=1, v{1}=2, w{1}=0$, and (for [01 $\left.\overline{1}\right]$ ) $u{2}=0, v{2}=1, w{2}=-1$. Therefore, $\lambda$ is equal to

$$
\begin{gathered}
\lambda=\cos ^{-1}\left[\frac{(1)(0)+(2)(1)+(0)(-1)}{\sqrt{\left[(1)^{2}+(2)^{2}+(0)^{2}\right]\left[(0)^{2}+(1)^{2}+(-1)^{2}\right]}}\right] \\
=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{10}}\right)=50.8^{\circ}
\end{gathered}
$$

Now, the angle $\phi$ is equal to the angle between the normal to the (111) plane (which is the [111] direction), and the [120] direction. Again from equation (\theta=\cos ^{-1}\left[\frac{u_1 u_2+v_1 v_2+w_1 w_2}{\sqrt{\left(u_1^2+v_1^2+w_1^2\right)\left(u_2^2+v_2^2+w_2^2\right)}}\right]), and for $u{1}=1, v{1}=1, w{1}=1$, and $u{2}=1, v{2}=2$, and $w{2}=0$, we have

$$
\begin{gathered}
\phi=\cos ^{-1}\left[\frac{(1)(1)+(1)(2)+(1)(0)}{\sqrt{\left[(1)^{2}+(1)^{2}+(1)^{2}\right]\left[(1)^{2}+(2)^{2}+(0)^{2}\right]}}\right] \\
=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{\sqrt{15}}\right)=39.2^{\circ}
\end{gathered}
$$

Therefore, the Schmid factor is equal to

$$
\cos \phi \cos \lambda=\cos \left(39.2^{\circ}\right) \cos \left(50.8^{\circ}\right)=\left(\frac{3}{\sqrt{15}}\right)\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{10}}\right)=0.490
$$

question: Consider a metal single crystal oriented such that the normal to the slip plane and the slip direction are at angles of $60^{\circ}$ and $35^{\circ}$, respectively, with the tensile axis. If the critical resolved shear stress is 6.2 MPa ( 900 psi ), will an applied stress of 12 MPa ( 1750 psi ) cause the single crystal to yield? If not, what stress will be necessary?
solution:
This problem calls for us to determine whether or not a metal single crystal having a specific orientation and of given critical resolved shear stress will yield. We are given that $\phi=60^{\circ}, \lambda=35^{\circ}$, and that the values of the critical resolved shear stress and applied tensile stress are 6.2 MPa ( 900 psi ) and 12 MPa ( 1750 psi ), respectively. From equation (\tau_R=\sigma \cos \phi \cos \lambda)

$$
\tau{R}=\sigma \cos \phi \cos \lambda=(12 \mathrm{MPa})\left(\cos 60^{\circ}\right)\left(\cos 35^{\circ}\right)=4.91 \mathrm{MPa} \quad(717 \mathrm{psi})
$$

Since the resolved shear stress ( 4.91 MPa ) is less that the critical resolved shear stress ( 6.2 MPa ), the single crystal will not yield.

However, from equation (\sigma_y=\frac{\tau_{\mathrm{crss}}}{(\cos \phi \cos \lambda)_{\max }}), the stress at which yielding occurs is

$$
\sigma{y}=\frac{\tau{\text {cros }}}{\cos \phi \cos \lambda}=\frac{6.2 \mathrm{MPa}}{\left(\cos 60^{\circ}\right)\left(\cos 35^{\circ}\right)}=15.1 \mathrm{MPa} \quad(2200 \mathrm{psi})
$$

question: A single crystal of zinc is oriented for a tensile test such that its slip plane normal makes an angle of $65^{\circ}$ with the tensile axis. Three possible slip directions make angles of $30^{\circ}, 48^{\circ}$, and $78^{\circ}$ with the same tensile axis. If plastic deformation begins at a tensile stress of 2.5 MPa ( 355 psi ), determine the critical resolved shear stress for zinc.
solution:
We are asked to compute the critical resolved shear stress for Zn . As stipulated in the problem, $\phi=65^{\circ}$, while possible values for $\lambda$ are $30^{\circ}, 48^{\circ}$, and $78^{\circ}$.
(a) Slip will occur along that direction for which $(\cos \phi \cos \lambda)$ is a maximum, or, in this case, for the largest $\cos \lambda$. Cosines for the possible $\lambda$ values are given below.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \cos \left(30^{\circ}\right)=0.87 \\
& \cos \left(48^{\circ}\right)=0.67 \\
& \cos \left(78^{\circ}\right)=0.21
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, the slip direction is at an angle of $30^{\circ}$ with the tensile axis.
(b) From equation (\sigma_y=\frac{\tau_{\mathrm{crss}}}{(\cos \phi \cos \lambda)_{\max }}), the critical resolved shear stress is just

$$
\begin{gathered}
\tau{\text {cros }}=\sigma{y}(\cos \phi \cos \lambda){\max } \\
=(2.5 \mathrm{MPa})\left[\cos \left(65^{\circ}\right) \cos \left(30^{\circ}\right)\right]=0.91 \mathrm{MPa}(130 \mathrm{psi})
\end{gathered}
$$

question: Consider a single crystal of nickel oriented such that a tensile stress is applied along a [001] direction. If slip occurs on a (111) plane and in a [ $\overline{1} 01]$ direction and is initiated at an applied tensile stress of 13.9 MPa (2020 psi), compute the critical resolved shear stress.
solution:
This problem asks that we compute the critical resolved shear stress for nickel. In order to do this, we must employ equation (\sigma_y=\frac{\tau_{\mathrm{crss}}}{(\cos \phi \cos \lambda)_{\max }}), but first it is necessary to solve for the angles $\lambda$ and $\phi$ which are shown in the sketch below.

The angle $\lambda$ is the angle between the tensile axis-i.e., along the [001] direction-and the slip direction-i.e., [ $\overline{1} 01$ ], and may be determined using equation (\theta=\cos ^{-1}\left[\frac{u_1 u_2+v_1 v_2+w_1 w_2}{\sqrt{\left(u_1^2+v_1^2+w_1^2\right)\left(u_2^2+v_2^2+w_2^2\right)}}\right]) as

$$
\lambda=\cos ^{-1}\left[\frac{u{1} u{2}+v{1} v{2}+w{1} w{2}}{\sqrt{\left(u{1}^{2}+v{1}^{2}+w{1}^{2}\right)\left(u{2}^{2}+v{2}^{2}+w{2}^{2}\right)}}\right]
$$

where (for [001]) $u{1}=0, v{1}=0, w{1}=1$, and (for [ $\overline{1} 01$ ]) $u{2}=-1, v{2}=0, w{2}=1$. Therefore, $\lambda$ is equal to

$$
\lambda=\cos ^{-1}\left[\frac{(0)(-1)+(0)(0)+(1)(1)}{\sqrt{\left[(0)^{2}+(0)^{2}+(1)^{2}\right]\left[(-1)^{2}+(0)^{2}+(1)^{2}\right]}}\right]
$$

$$
=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)=45^{\circ}
$$

Furthermore, $\phi$ is the angle between the tensile axis-the [001] direction-and the normal to the slip plane-i.e., the (111) plane; for this case this normal is along a [111] direction. Therefore, again using equation (\theta=\cos ^{-1}\left[\frac{u_1 u_2+v_1 v_2+w_1 w_2}{\sqrt{\left(u_1^2+v_1^2+w_1^2\right)\left(u_2^2+v_2^2+w_2^2\right)}}\right])

$$
\begin{gathered}
\phi=\cos ^{-1}\left[\frac{(0)(1)+(0)(1)+(1)(1)}{\sqrt{\left[(0)^{2}+(0)^{2}+(1)^{2}\right]\left[(1)^{2}+(1)^{2}+(1)^{2}\right]}}\right] \\
=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)=54.7^{\circ}
\end{gathered}
$$

And, finally, using equation (\sigma_y=\frac{\tau_{\mathrm{crss}}}{(\cos \phi \cos \lambda)_{\max }}), the critical resolved shear stress is equal to

$$
\begin{gathered}
\tau{\text {crss }}=\sigma{y}(\cos \phi \cos \lambda) \\
=(13.9 \mathrm{MPa})\left[\cos \left(54.7^{\circ}\right) \cos \left(45^{\circ}\right)\right] \\
=(13.9 \mathrm{MPa})\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)=5.68 \mathrm{MPa} \quad(825 \mathrm{psi})
\end{gathered}
$$

question:
A single crystal of a metal that has the FCC crystal structure is oriented such that a tensile stress is applied parallel to the [100] direction. If the critical resolved shear stress for this material is 0.5 MPa , calculate the magnitude(s) of applied stress(es) necessary to cause slip to occur on the (111) plane in each of the [1 $\overline{1} 0$ ]. [10 $\overline{1}]$, and $[0 \overline{1} 1]$ directions.

Give your answer as a tuple (magnitude in in [1 $\overline{1} 0$ ], in [10 $\overline{1}]$, in $[0 \overline{1} 1]$).

A single crystal of a metal that has the BCC crystal structure is oriented such that a tensile stress is applied in the [100] direction. If the magnitude of this stress is 4.0 MPa , compute the resolved shear stress in the [1 $\overline{1} 1]$ direction on each of the (110), (011), and (101) planes.

Give your answer as a tuple (resolved shear stress on (110), on (011), on (101))
solution:
In order to solve this problem it is necessary to employ equation (\tau_R=\sigma \cos \phi \cos \lambda), which means that we first need to solve for the angles $\lambda$ and $\phi$ for the three slip systems.

For each of these three slip systems, the $\lambda$ will be the same-i.e., the angle between the direction of the applied stress, [100] and the slip direction, [1 $\overline{1} 1]$. This angle $\lambda$ may be determined using equation (\theta=\cos ^{-1}\left[\frac{u_1 u_2+v_1 v_2+w_1 w_2}{\sqrt{\left(u_1^2+v_1^2+w_1^2\right)\left(u_2^2+v_2^2+w_2^2\right)}}\right])

$$
\lambda=\cos ^{-1}\left[\frac{u{1} u{2}+v{1} v{2}+w{1} w{2}}{\sqrt{\left(u{1}^{2}+v{1}^{2}+w{1}^{2}\right)\left(u{2}^{2}+v{2}^{2}+w{2}^{2}\right)}}\right]
$$

where (for [100]) $u{1}=1, v{1}=0, w{1}=0$, and (for [1 $\overline{1} 1]$ ) $u{2}=1, v{2}=-1, w{2}=1$. Therefore, $\lambda$ is determined as

$$
\begin{gathered}
\lambda=\cos ^{-1}\left[\frac{(1)(1)+(0)(-1)+(0)(1)}{\sqrt{\left[(1)^{2}+(0)^{2}+(0)^{2}\right]\left[(1)^{2}+(-1)^{2}+(1)^{2}\right]}}\right] \\
=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)=54.7^{\circ}
\end{gathered}
$$

Let us now determine $\phi$ for the angle between the direction of the applied tensile stress-i.e., the [100] directionand the normal to the (110) slip plane-i.e., the [110] direction. Again, using equation (\theta=\cos ^{-1}\left[\frac{u_1 u_2+v_1 v_2+w_1 w_2}{\sqrt{\left(u_1^2+v_1^2+w_1^2\right)\left(u_2^2+v_2^2+w_2^2\right)}}\right]) where $u{1}=1, v{1}=0, w{1}$ $=0$ (for [100]), and $u{2}=1, v{2}=1, w{2}=0$ (for [110]), $\phi$ is equal to

$$
\phi[100]-[110]=\cos ^{-1}\left[\frac{(1)(1)+(0)(1)+(0)(0)}{\sqrt{\left[(1)^{2}+(0)^{2}+(0)^{2}\right]\left[(1)^{2}+(1)^{2}+(0)^{2}\right]}}\right]
$$

$$
=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)=45^{\circ}
$$

Now, using equation (\tau_R=\sigma \cos \phi \cos \lambda)

$$
\tau{R}=\sigma \cos \phi \cos \lambda
$$

we solve for the resolved shear stress for this slip system as

$$
\tau{R}(110)-[1 \overline{1} 1]=(4.0 \mathrm{MPa})\left[\cos \left(45^{\circ}\right) \cos \left(54.7^{\circ}\right)\right]=(4.0 \mathrm{MPa})(0.707)(0.578)=1.63 \mathrm{MPa}
$$

Now, we must determine the value of $\phi$ for the (011)-[1 $\overline{1} 1]$ slip system-that is, the angle between the direction of the applied stress, [100], and the normal to the (011) plane-i.e., the [011] direction. Again using equation (\theta=\cos ^{-1}\left[\frac{u_1 u_2+v_1 v_2+w_1 w_2}{\sqrt{\left(u_1^2+v_1^2+w_1^2\right)\left(u_2^2+v_2^2+w_2^2\right)}}\right])

$$
\begin{gathered}
\lambda{[100]-[011]}=\cos ^{-1}\left[\frac{(1)(0)+(0)(1)+(0)(1)}{\sqrt{\left[(1)^{2}+(0)^{2}+(0)^{2}\right]\left[(0)^{2}+(1)^{2}+(1)^{2}\right]}}\right] \\
=\cos ^{-1}(0)=90^{\circ}
\end{gathered}
$$

Thus, the resolved shear stress for this (011)-[1 $\overline{1} 1]$ slip system is

$$
\tau{R(011)-[1 \overline{1} 1]}==(4.0 \mathrm{MPa})\left[\cos \left(90^{\circ}\right) \cos \left(54.7^{\circ}\right)\right]=(4.0 \mathrm{MPa})(0)(0.578)=0 \mathrm{MPa}
$$

And, finally, it is necessary to determine the value of $\phi$ for the $(10 \overline{1})-[1 \overline{1} 1]$ slip system-that is, the angle between the direction of the applied stress, [100], and the normal to the $(10 \overline{1})$ plane-i.e., the $[10 \overline{1}]$ direction. Again using equation (\theta=\cos ^{-1}\left[\frac{u_1 u_2+v_1 v_2+w_1 w_2}{\sqrt{\left(u_1^2+v_1^2+w_1^2\right)\left(u_2^2+v_2^2+w_2^2\right)}}\right])

$$
\lambda{[100]-[10 \overline{1}]}=\cos ^{-1}\left[\frac{(1)(1)+(0)(0)+(0)(-1)}{\sqrt{\left[(1)^{2}+(0)^{2}+(0)^{2}\right]\left[(1)^{2}+(0)^{2}+(-1)^{2}\right]}}\right]
$$

$$
=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)=45^{\circ}
$$

Here, as with the (110) $-[1 \overline{1} 1]$ slip system above, the value of $\phi$ is $45^{\circ}$, which again leads to

$$
\tau{R(10 \overline{1})-[1 \overline{1} 1]}=(4.0 \mathrm{MPa})\left[\cos \left(45^{\circ}\right) \cos \left(54.7^{\circ}\right)\right]=(4.0 \mathrm{MPa})(0.707)(0.578)=1.63 \mathrm{MPa}
$$

question: Consider a single crystal of some hypothetical metal that has the BCC crystal structure and is oriented such that a tensile stress is applied along a [121] direction. If slip occurs on a (101) plane and in a [ $\overline{1} 11]$ direction, compute the stress at which the crystal yields if its critical resolved shear stress is 2.4 MPa .

Consider a single crystal of some hypothetical metal that has the FCC crystal structure and is oriented such that a tensile stress is applied along a [112] direction. If slip occurs on a (111) plane and in a [011] direction, and the crystal yields at a stress of 5.12 MPa compute the critical resolved shear stress.
The critical resolved shear stress for copper $(\mathrm{Cu})$ is 0.48 MPa ( 70 psi ). Determine the maximum possible yield strength for a single crystal of Cu pulled in tension.
solution:
In order to determine the maximum possible yield strength for a single crystal of Cu pulled in tension, we simply employ equation (\sigma_y=2 \tau_{\mathrm{crss}}) as

$$
\sigma{y}=2 \tau{\text {crs }}=\left.(2)(0.48 \mathrm{MPa})=0.96 \mathrm{MPa} \quad(140 \mathrm{psi})\right.
$$

question: The lower yield point for an iron that has an average grain diameter of $1 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mm}$ is 230 MPa (33,000 psi). At a grain diameter of $6 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mm}$, the yield point increases to 275 MPa (40,000 psi). At what grain diameter will the lower yield point be 310 MPa (45,000 psi)?
solution:
The best way to solve this problem is to first establish two simultaneous expressions of equation (\sigma_y=\sigma_0+k_y d^{-1 / 2}), solve for $\sigma{0}$ and $k{y}$, and finally determine the value of $d$ when $\sigma{y}=310 \mathrm{MPa}$. The data pertaining to this problem may be tabulated as follows:

| $\sigma{y}$ | $d(\mathrm{~mm})$ | $d^{-\frac{1}{2}}(\mathrm{~mm})^{-\frac{1}{2}}$ |
| :-- | :-- | :--: |
| 230 MPa | $1 \times 10^{-2}$ | 10.0 |
| 275 MPa | $6 \times 10^{-3}$ | 12.91 |

The two equations thus become

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 230 \mathrm{MPa}=\sigma{0}+(10.0) k{y} \\
& 275 \mathrm{MPa}=\sigma{0}+(12.91) k{y}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving these two simultaneous equations leads to $\sigma{0}=75.4 \mathrm{MPa}$ and $k{y}=15.46 \mathrm{MPa}(\mathrm{mm})^{\frac{1}{2}}$. At a yield strength of 310 MPa

$$
310 \mathrm{MPa}=75.4 \mathrm{MPa}+\left[15.46 \mathrm{MPa}(\mathrm{~mm})^{\frac{1}{2}}\right] d^{-\frac{1}{2}}
$$

Or

$$
d^{-\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{310 \mathrm{MPa}-75.4 \mathrm{MPa}}{15.46 \mathrm{MPa}(\mathrm{~mm})^{\frac{1}{2}}}=15.17 \mathrm{~mm}^{-\frac{1}{2}}
$$

Thus,

$$
d=\left[\frac{15.46 \mathrm{MPa}(\mathrm{~mm})^{\frac{1}{2}}}{310 \mathrm{MPa}-75.4 \mathrm{MPa}}\right]^{2}=4.34 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mm}
$$

question: If it is assumed that the plot in  figure 8.15  is for non-cold-worked brass, determine the grain size of the alloy in  figure 8.19 ; assume its composition is the same as the alloy in  figure 8.15 .
solution:
From  figure 8.19a , the yield strength of brass at $0 \% \mathrm{CW}$ is approximately 175 MPa (26,000 psi). This yield strength from  figure 8.15  corresponds to a $d^{-\frac{1}{2}}$ value of approximately $12.0(\mathrm{~mm})^{-\frac{1}{2}}$. Thus,

$$
d=\frac{1}{\left[12.0(\mathrm{~mm})^{-\frac{1}{2}}\right]^{2}}=6.94 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mm}
$$

question:
(a) Show, for a tensile test, that

$$
\% \mathrm{CW}=\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{\varepsilon+1}\right) \times 100
$$

if there is no change in specimen volume during the deformation process (i.e., $A{0} I{0}=A{d} I{d}$ ).
(b) Using the result of part (a), compute the percent cold work experienced by naval brass (for which the stress-strain behavior is shown in  figure 7.12 ) when a stress of 415 MPa (60,000 psi) is applied.
solution:
(a) We are asked to show, for a tensile test, that

$$
\% \mathrm{CW}=\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{\varepsilon+1}\right) \times 100
$$

From equation (\% \mathrm{CW}=\left(\frac{A_0-A_d}{A_0}\right) \times 100)

$$
\% \mathrm{CW}=\left(\frac{A{0}-A{d}}{A{0}}\right) \times 100=\left(1-\frac{A{d}}{A{0}}\right) \times 100
$$

The following relationship

$$
$A{0} I{0}=A{d} I{d}$
$$

may be rearranged to read as follows:

$$
\frac{A{d}}{A{0}}=\frac{I{0}}{I{d}}
$$

Substitution of the right-hand-side of this expression into the above equation for $\% \mathrm{CW}$ leads to

$$
\% \mathrm{CW}=\left(1-\frac{I{0}}{I{d}}\right) \times 100
$$

Now, from the definition of engineering strain (equation (\varepsilon=\frac{l_i-l_0}{l_0}=\frac{\Delta l}{l_0}))

$$
\varepsilon=\frac{l{d}-l{0}}{l{0}}=\frac{l{d}}{l{0}}-1
$$

Or, upon rearrangement

$$
\frac{l{0}}{l{d}}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon+1}
$$

Substitution of this expression for $l{0} / l{d}$ into the equation (\% \mathrm{CW}=\left(1-\frac{l_0}{l_d}\right) \times 100) above gives

$$
\% \mathrm{CW}=\left(1-\frac{l{0}}{l{d}}\right) \times 100=\left(1-\frac{1}{\varepsilon+1}\right) \times 100=\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{\varepsilon+1}\right) \times 100
$$

the desired equation
(b) From  figure 7.12 , a stress of $415 \mathrm{MPa}(60,000 \mathrm{psi})$ corresponds to a strain of 0.15 . Using the above expression

$$
\% \mathrm{CW}=\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{\varepsilon+1}\right) \times 100=\left(\frac{0.15}{0.15+1.00}\right) \times 100=13.0 \% \mathrm{CW}
$$

question: Two previously undeformed cylindrical specimens of an alloy are to be strain hardened by reducing their cross-sectional areas (while maintaining their circular cross sections). For one specimen, the initial and deformed radii are 15 and 12 mm , respectively. The second specimen, with an initial radius of 11 mm , must have the same deformed hardness as the first specimen; compute the second specimen's radius after deformation.
solution:
In order for these two cylindrical specimens to have the same deformed hardness, they must be deformed to the same percent cold work. For the first specimen

$$
\% \mathrm{CW}=\left(\frac{A{0}-A{d}}{A{0}}\right) \times 100=\left(\frac{\pi r{0}^{2}-\pi r{d}^{2}}{\pi r{0}^{2}}\right) \times 100
$$

in which $r{0}$ and $r{d}$ denote the original and deformed specimen radii, respectively. Substitution of the values for these two parameters provided in the problem statement leads to the following:

$$
\% \mathrm{CW}=\left(\frac{\pi r{0}^{2}-\pi r{d}^{2}}{\pi r{0}^{2}}\right) \times 100==\frac{\pi(15 \mathrm{~mm})^{2}-\pi(12 \mathrm{~mm})^{2}}{\pi(15 \mathrm{~mm})^{2}} \times 100=36 \% \mathrm{CW}
$$

For the second specimen, the deformed radius is computed using the above equation (\% \mathrm{CW}=\left(\frac{A_0-A_d}{A_0}\right) \times 100=\left(\frac{\pi r_0^2-\pi r_d^2}{\pi r_0^2}\right) \times 100) and solving for $r{d}$ as follows:

$$
r{d}=r{0} \sqrt{1-\frac{\% \mathrm{CW}}{100}}
$$

And insertion into this equation of the value of $\% \mathrm{CW}$ determined above (i.e., $36 \% \mathrm{CW}$ ) and the $r{0}(11 \mathrm{~mm})$ for the second specimen leads to

$$
r{d}=(11 \mathrm{~mm}) \sqrt{1-\frac{36 \% \mathrm{CW}}{100}}=8.80 \mathrm{~mm}
$$

question: A cylindrical specimen of cold-worked copper has a ductility (\%EL) of $15 \%$. If its cold-worked radius is 6.4 $\mathrm{mm}(0.25 \mathrm{in}$.) , what was its radius before deformation?
solution:
This problem calls for us to calculate the precold-worked radius of a cylindrical specimen of copper that has a cold-worked ductility of $15 \%$ EL. From  figure 8.19c , copper that has a ductility of $15 \%$ EL will have experienced a deformation of about $20 \%$ CW. For a cylindrical specimen, equation (\% \mathrm{CW}=\left(\frac{A_0-A_d}{A_0}\right) \times 100) becomes

$$
\% \mathrm{CW}=\left(\frac{\pi r{0}^{2}-\pi r{d}^{2}}{\pi r{0}^{2}}\right) \times 100
$$

Since $r{d}=6.4 \mathrm{~mm}(0.25 \mathrm{in}$.) and the percent cold work is $20 \% \mathrm{CW}$, we solve for $r{0}$ as follows:

$$
r{0}=\frac{r{d}}{\sqrt{1-\frac{\% \mathrm{CW}}{100}}}=\frac{6.4 \mathrm{~mm}}{\sqrt{1-\frac{20.0}{100}}}=7.2 \mathrm{~mm} \quad(0.280 \mathrm{in} .)
$$

question:
Experimentally, it has been observed for single crystals of a number of metals that the critical resolved shear stress $\tau{\text {crss }}$ is a function of the dislocation density $\rho{D}$ as

$$
\tau_{\text {crss }}=\tau{0}+A \sqrt{\rho{\mathrm{D}}}
$$

where $\tau{0}$ and $A$ are constants. For copper, the critical resolved shear stress is 0.69 MPa (100 psi) at a dislocation density of $10^{4} \mathrm{~mm}^{-2}$. If it is known that the value of $\tau{0}$ for copper is 0.069 MPa (10 psi), compute $\tau_{\text {crss }}$ at a dislocation density of $10^{6} \mathrm{~mm}^{-2}$.
solution:
We are asked in this problem to compute the critical resolved shear stress at a dislocation density of $10^{6}$ $\mathrm{mm}^{-2}$. It is first necessary to compute the value of the constant $A$ (in equation (\tau_{\mathrm{crss}}=\tau_0+A \sqrt{\rho_D})) from the one set of data as follows:

$$
A=\frac{\tau{\text {crss }}-\tau{0}}{\sqrt{\rho{D}}}=\frac{0.69 \mathrm{MPa}-0.069 \mathrm{MPa}}{\sqrt{10^{4} \mathrm{~mm}^{-2}}}=6.21 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{MPa}-\mathrm{mm}(0.90 \mathrm{psi}-\mathrm{mm})
$$

Now, since we know the values for both $A$ and $\tau{0}$, it is possible to compute the critical resolved shear stress at a dislocation density of $10^{6} \mathrm{~mm}^{-2}$ as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
\tau_{\text {crss }}=\tau{0}+A \sqrt{\rho{D}} \\
=(0.069 \mathrm{MPa})+\left(6.21 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{MPa}-\mathrm{mm}\right) \sqrt{10^{6} \mathrm{~mm}^{-2}}=6.28 \mathrm{MPa}(910 \mathrm{psi})
\end{gathered}
$$

question:
(a) From  figure 8.25 , compute the length of time required for the average grain diameter to increase from 0.03 to 0.3 mm at $600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for this brass material.
(b) Repeat the calculation, this time using $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

Give your answer as a tuple (length of time in (a), length of time in (b)).
solution:
(a) From  figure 8.25 , (and realizing that both axes are scaled logarithmically) at $600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the time necessary for the average grain diameter to grow to 0.03 is about 6 min ; and the total time to grow to 0.3 mm is approximately 3000 min . Therefore, the time to grow from 0.03 to 0.3 mm is $3000 \mathrm{~min}-6 \mathrm{~min}$, or approximately 3000 min .
(b) At $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ the time required for this same grain size increase is approximately 80 min .

question: Consider a hypothetical material that has a grain diameter of $2.1 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mm}$. After a heat treatment at $600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for 3 h , the grain diameter has increased to $7.2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mm}$. Compute the grain diameter when a specimen of this same original material (i.e., $d{0}=2.1 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mm}$ ) is heated for 1.7 h at $600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Assume the $n$ grain diameter exponent has a value of 2 .

A hypothetical metal alloy has a grain diameter of $1.7 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mm}$. After a heat treatment at $450^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for 250 min the grain diameter has increased to $4.5 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mm}$. Compute the time required for a specimen of this same material (i.e., $d{0}=1.7 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mm}$ ) to achieve a grain diameter of $8.7 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mm}$ while being heated at $450^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Assume the $n$ grain diameter exponent has a value of 2.1.

Assume parabolic grain growth so that d² – d₀² = K t. The average grain diameter for a brass material was measured as a function of time at $650^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, which is shown in the following table at two different times:

| Time (min) | Grain Diameter (mm) |
| :--: | :--: |
| 40 | $5.6 \times 10^{-2}$ |
| 100 | $8.0 \times 10^{-2}$ |

(a) What was the original grain diameter?
(b) What grain diameter would you predict after 200 min at $650^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$?

Give your answer as a tuple (the original grain diameter, grain diameter after 200 min).
solution:
(a) Using the data given and equation (d^2-d_0^2=K t) (for $n=2$ )-that is

$$
$d^{2}-d{0}^{2}=K t$
$$

we may set up two simultaneous equations with $d{0}$ and $K$ as unknowns, as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \left(5.6 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mm}\right)^{2}-d{0}^{2}=(40 \mathrm{~min}) K \\
& \left(8.0 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mm}\right)^{2}-d{0}^{2}=(100 \mathrm{~min}) K
\end{aligned}
$$

Solution of these expressions yields a value for $d{0}$, the original grain diameter, of

$$
d{0}=0.031 \mathrm{~mm}
$$

and a value for $K$ of

$$
K=5.44 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~mm}^{2} / \mathrm{min}
$$

(b) At 200 min , the diameter $d$ is computed using a rearranged form of equation (d^2-d_0^2=K t) (incorporating values of $d{0}$ and $K$ that were just determined) as follows:

$$
d=\sqrt{d{0}^{2}+K t}
$$

$$
=\sqrt{(0.031 \mathrm{~mm})^{2}+\left(5.44 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~mm}^{2} / \mathrm{min}\right)(200 \mathrm{~min})}
$$

$=0.109 \mathrm{~mm}$

question: A non-cold-worked brass specimen of average grain size d₀ = 0.010 mm has a yield strength of σy₀ = 150 MPa.  After annealing at 500 °C for 1 000 s the average grain size has grown to d = 0.016 mm.  If the friction stress σ₀ for this alloy is 25 MPa, estimate the new yield strength using the Hall–Petch equation.

The following yield strength, grain diameter, and heat treatment time (for grain growth) data were gathered for an iron specimen that was heat treated at $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Using these data, compute the yield strength of a specimen that was heated at $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for 3 h . Assume a value of 2 for $n$, the grain diameter exponent.

| Grain diameter $(\mathbf{m m})$ | Yield Strength $(\mathbf{M P a})$ | Heat Treating Time $(\boldsymbol{h})$ |
| :--: | :--: | :--: |
| 0.028 | 300 | 10 |
| 0.010 | 385 | 1 |

The tensile strength and number-average molecular weight for two poly(methyl methacrylate) materials are as follows:

| Tensile Strength (MPa) | Number-Average Molecular Weight (g/mol) |
| :--: | :--: |
| 50 | 30,000 |
| 150 | 50,000 |

Estimate the tensile strength at a number-average molecular weight of $40,000 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$.
solution:
This problem gives us the tensile strengths and associated number-average molecular weights for two poly(methyl methacrylate) materials and then asks that we estimate the tensile strength for $\bar{M}{n}=40,000 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$. equation (T S=T S_{\infty}-\frac{A}{\bar{M}_n}) cites the dependence of the tensile strength on $\bar{M}{n}$-that is

$$
T S=T S{\infty}-\frac{A}{\bar{M}{n}}
$$

Using data provided in the problem statement, we may set up two simultaneous equations from which it is possible to solve for the two constants $T S{\infty}$ and $A$. These equations are as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 50 \mathrm{MPa}=T S{\infty}-\frac{A}{30,000 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}} \\
& 150 \mathrm{MPa}=T S{\infty}-\frac{A}{50,000 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Values of the two constants are: $T S{\infty}=300 \mathrm{MPa}$ and $A=7.50 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{MPa}-\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{mol}$. Substituting these values into equation (T S=T S_{\infty}-\frac{A}{\bar{M}_n}) for $\bar{M}{n}=40,000 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$ leads to

$$
T S=T S{\infty}-\frac{A}{40,000 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}}
$$

$$
=300 \mathrm{MPa}-\frac{7.50 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{MPa}-\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{mol}}{40,000 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}}
$$

$=112.5 \mathrm{MPa}$

The tensile strength and number-average molecular weight for two polyethylene materials are as follows:

| Tensile Strength (MPa) | Number-Average Molecular Weight (\frac{g}{mol}) |
| :--: | :--: |
| 90 | 20,000 |
| 180 | 40,000 |

Estimate the number-average molecular weight that is required to give a tensile strength of 140 MPa .

question:
The tensile strength and number-average molecular weight for two polyethylene materials are as follows:

| Tensile Strength (MPa) | Number-Average Molecular Weight (g/mol) |
| :--: | :--: |
| 90 | 20,000 |
| 180 | 40,000 |

Estimate the number-average molecular weight that is required to give a tensile strength of 140 MPa .

Fifteen kilograms of polychloroprene is vulcanized with 5.2 kg of sulfur. What fraction of the possible crosslink sites is bonded to sulfur crosslinks, assuming that, on the average, 5.5 sulfur atoms participate in each crosslink?
solution:
This problem asks that we compute the fraction of possible crosslink sites in 15 kg of polychloroprene when 5.2 kg of S is added, assuming that, on the average, 5.5 sulfur atoms participate in each crosslink bond. Given the butadiene repeat unit in  table 4.5 , (which is composed of 4 carbon atoms, 5 hydrogen atoms, and 1 chlorine atom) we may calculate its molecular weight as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
A(\text { chloroprene })=4\left(A{\mathrm{C}}\right)+5\left(A{\mathrm{H}}\right)+A{\mathrm{Cl}} \\
=(4)(12.01 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol})+5(1.008 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol})+35.45 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}=88.53 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}
\end{gathered}
$$

Which means that for 15 kg of chloroprene there are the following number of moles of chloroprene, $n{\text {chloro }}$ :

$$
n{\text {chloro }}=\frac{15,000 \mathrm{~g}}{88.53 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}}=169.4 \mathrm{~mol}
$$

For the vulcanization of polychloroprene, there are two possible crosslink sites per repeat unit-one for each of the two carbon atoms that are doubly bonded. Furthermore, each of these crosslinks forms a bridge between two repeat units. Therefore, we can say that there is the equivalent of one crosslink per repeat unit. Let us now calculate the number of moles of sulfur ( $n{\text {sulfur }}$ ) that react with the chloroprene, by taking the mole ratio of sulfur to chloroprene, and then dividing this ratio by 5.5 atoms per crosslink; this yields the fraction of possible sites that are crosslinked. Now, the amount of sulfur added is $5.2 \mathrm{~kg}(5200 \mathrm{~g})$, which means that

$$
n{\text {sulfur }}=\frac{5200 \mathrm{~g}}{32.06 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}}=162.2 \mathrm{~mol}
$$

And, finally, we determine the fraction of sites crosslinked as follows:

$$
\text { fraction sites crosslinked }=\frac{\frac{n{\text {sulfur }}}{n{\text {chloro }}} \frac{162.2 \mathrm{~mol}}{169.4 \mathrm{~mol}}=0.174
$$

question: Compute the weight percent sulfur that must be added to completely crosslink an alternating acrylonitrilebutadiene copolymer, assuming that four sulfur atoms participate in each crosslink.
solution:
For an alternating acrylonitrile-butadiene copolymer, we are asked to compute the weight percent sulfur necessary for complete crosslinking, assuming that, on the average, four sulfur atoms participate in each crosslink. The acrylonitrile and butadiene repeat units are shown in  table 4.5 , from which it may be noted that there are two possible crosslink sites on each butadiene repeat unit (one site at each of the two carbon atoms that are doubly bonded), and no possible sites for acrylonitrile; also, because it is an alternating copolymer, the ratio of butadiene to acrylonitrile repeat units is $1: 1$. Thus, for each pair of combined butadiene-acrylonitrile repeat units which crosslink, eight sulfur atoms are required, or, for complete crosslinking, the sulfur-to-(acrylonitrile-butadiene) ratio is 4:1.

Now, let us consider as our basis, one mole of the combined acrylonitrile-butadiene repeat units. In order for complete crosslinking, four moles of sulfur are required. Thus, for us to convert this composition to weight percent, it is necessary to convert moles to mass. The acrylonitrile repeat unit consists of three carbon atoms, three hydrogen atoms, and one nitrogen atom; the butadiene repeat unit is composed of four carbon atoms and six hydrogen atoms. This gives a molecular weight for the combined repeat unit of

$$
\begin{aligned}
& m(\text { acrylonitrile-butadiene })=3\left(A{\mathrm{C}}\right)+3\left(A{\mathrm{H}}\right)+A{\mathrm{N}}+4\left(A{\mathrm{C}}\right)+6\left(A{\mathrm{H}}\right) \\
& =7(12.01 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol})+9(1.008 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol})+14.007 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}=107.15 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

Or, in one mole of this combined repeat unit, there are 107.15 g . Furthermore, for complete crosslinking 4.0 mol of sulfur is required, which amounts to $(4.0 \mathrm{~mol})(32.06 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol})=128.24 \mathrm{~g}$. Thus, the concentration of S in weight percent $C{\mathrm{S}}$ (using equation (C_1=\frac{m_1}{m_1+m_2} \times 100)) is just

$$
C{\mathrm{S}}=\frac{128.24 \mathrm{~g}}{128.24 \mathrm{~g}+107.15 \mathrm{~g}} \times 100=54.5 \mathrm{wt} \%
$$

question: If $45.3 \mathrm{wt} \%$ sulfur is combined with polyisoprene, how many crosslinks will be associated with each isoprene repeat unit if it is assumed that, on the average, five sulfur atoms participate in each crosslink?
solution:
This problem asks for us to determine how many crosslinks form per isoprene repeat unit when $45.3 \mathrm{wt} \%$ sulfur is added, assuming that, on the average, five sulfur atoms participate in each crosslink. If we arbitrarily consider 100 g of the vulcanized material, 45.3 g will be sulfur and 54.7 g will be polyisoprene. Next, let us find how many moles of sulfur and isoprene correspond to these masses. The atomic weight of sulfur is $32.06 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$, and thus,

$$
\text { \# moles } \mathrm{S}=\frac{45.3 \mathrm{~g}}{32.06 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}}=1.41 \mathrm{~mol}
$$

Now, in each isoprene repeat unit there are five carbon atoms and eight hydrogen atoms. Thus, the molecular weight of a mole of isoprene units is

$$
\begin{gathered}
A(\text { isoprene })=5 A{\mathrm{C}}+8 A{\mathrm{H}} \\
=(5)(12.01 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol})+(8)(1.008 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol})=68.11 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}
\end{gathered}
$$

Or, in 54.7 g of polyisoprene, the number of moles of isoprene is equal to

$$
\text { \# moles isoprene }=\frac{54.7 \mathrm{~g}}{68.11 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}}=0.803 \mathrm{~mol}
$$

Therefore, the ratio of moles of $S$ to the number of moles of polyisoprene is

$$
\frac{1.41 \mathrm{~mol}}{0.803 \mathrm{~mol}}: 1=1.76: 1
$$

When all possible sites are crosslinked, the ratio of the number of moles of sulfur to the number of moles of isoprene is $5: 1$; this is because there are two crosslink sites per repeat unit and each crosslink is shared between repeat units on adjacent chains, and there are 5 sulfur atoms per crosslink. Finally, to determine the fraction of sites that are crosslinked, we just divide the actual crosslinked \frac{sulfur}{isoprene} ratio by the completely crosslinked ratio. Or,

fraction of repeat unit sites crosslinked $=\frac{1.\frac{76}{1}}{\frac{5}{1}}=0.352$

question: For the vulcanization of polyisoprene, compute the weight percent of sulfur that must be added to ensure that $10 \%$ of possible sites will be crosslinked; assume that, on the average, 3.5 sulfur atoms are associated with each crosslink.
solution:
We are asked what weight percent of sulfur must be added to polyisoprene in order to ensure that $10 \%$ of possible sites are crosslinked, assuming that, on the average, 3.5 sulfur atoms are associated with each crosslink.  table 4.5  shows the chemical repeat unit for isoprene. For each of these units there are two possible crosslink sites; one site is associated with each of the two carbon atoms that are involved in the chain double bond. Since $10 \%$ of the possible sites are crosslinked, for each 100 isoprene repeat units 10 of them are crosslinked; actually there are two crosslink sites per repeat unit, but each crosslink is shared by two chains. Furthermore, on the average we assume that each crosslink is composed of 3.5 sulfur atoms; thus, there must be $3.5 \times 10$ or 35 sulfur atoms added for every 100 isoprene repeat units. In terms of moles, it is necessary to add 35 moles of sulfur to 100 moles of isoprene. The atomic weight of sulfur is $32.06 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$, while the molecular weight of an isoprene repeat unit is

$$
\begin{gathered}
A(\text { isoprene })=5\left(A{\mathrm{C}}\right)+8\left(A{\mathrm{H}}\right) \\
=(5)(12.01 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol})+(8)(1.008 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol})=68.11 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}
\end{gathered}
$$

The mass of sulfur added $\left(m{\mathrm{S}}\right)$ is equal to the number of moles of sulfur ( 35 mol ) times the atomic weight of sulfur $(32.06 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol})$ or

$$
m{\mathrm{S}}=(35 \mathrm{~mol})(32.06 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol})=1122 \mathrm{~g}
$$

While for isoprene the number of moles $\left(m{\text {ip }}\right)$ is equal to

$$
m{\mathrm{ip}}=(100 \mathrm{~mol})(68.11 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol})=6811 \mathrm{~g}
$$

Or, the concentration of sulfur that must be added, in weight percent (equation (C_1=\frac{m_1}{m_1+m_2} \times 100)), is just

$$
C{\mathrm{S}}=\frac{m{\mathrm{S}}}{m{\mathrm{S}}+m{\mathrm{ip}}} \times 100=\frac{1122 \mathrm{~g}}{1122 \mathrm{~g}+6811 \mathrm{~g}} \times 100=14.1 \mathrm{wt} \%
$$

question: What is the magnitude of the maximum stress that exists at the tip of an internal crack having a radius of curvature of $1.9 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mm}\left(7.5 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{in}\right.$.) and a crack length of $3.8 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mm}\left(1.5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{in}\right.$.) when a tensile stress of $140 \mathrm{MPa}(20,000 \mathrm{psi})$ is applied?
solution:
equation (\sigma_m=2 \sigma_0\left(\frac{a}{\rho_t}\right)^{1 / 2}) is employed to solve this problem-i.e.,

$$
\sigma{m}=2 \sigma{0}\left(\frac{a}{\rho{t}}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}
$$

Values for $\sigma{0}(140 \mathrm{MPa}), 2 a\left(3.8 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mm}\right)$, and $\rho{t}\left(1.9 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mm}\right)$ are provided in the problem statement. Therefore, we solve for $\sigma{m}$ as follows:

$$
\sigma{m}=(2)\left(140 \mathrm{MPa}\right)\left(\frac{\frac{3.8 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mm}}{2}}{1.9 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mm}}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}=2800 \mathrm{MPa} \quad(400,000 \mathrm{psi})
$$

question: Estimate the theoretical fracture strength of a brittle material if it is known that fracture occurs by the propagation of an elliptically shaped surface crack of length 0.5 mm ( 0.02 in .) and a tip radius of curvature of $5 \times$ $10^{-3} \mathrm{~mm}\left(2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{in}\right.$. $)$, when a stress of 1035 MPa ( 150,000 psi) is applied.
solution:
In order to estimate the theoretical fracture strength of this material it is necessary to calculate $\sigma{m}$ using equation (\sigma_m=2 \sigma_0\left(\frac{a}{\rho_t}\right)^{1 / 2}) given that $\sigma{0}=1035 \mathrm{MPa}, a=0.5 \mathrm{~mm}$, and $\rho{t}=5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mm}$. Thus,

$$
\begin{gathered}
\sigma{m}=2 \sigma{0}\left(\frac{a}{\rho{t}}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}} \\
=(2)(1035 \mathrm{MPa})\left(\frac{0.5 \mathrm{~mm}}{5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mm}}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}} \\
=2.07 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{MPa}=20.7 \mathrm{GPa}\left(3 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{psi}\right)
\end{gathered}
$$

question: A specimen of a 4340 steel alloy with a plane strain fracture toughness of $54.8 \mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{~m}}(50 \mathrm{ksi} \sqrt{\mathrm{in}}$. ) is exposed to a stress of 1030 MPa (150,000 psi). Will this specimen experience fracture if the largest surface crack is $0.5 \mathrm{~mm}(0.02 \mathrm{in}$.) long? Why or why not? Assume that the parameter Y has a value of 1.0.
solution:
This problem asks us to determine whether or not the 4340 steel alloy specimen will fracture when exposed to a stress of 1030 MPa . This requires that we solve for $\sigma{c}$ from equation (\sigma_c=\frac{K_{I c}}{Y \sqrt{\pi a}}), given the values of $K{I c}$ $(54.8 \mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{~m}})$, the largest value of $a(0.5 \mathrm{~mm})$, and $Y(1.0)$. Thus, the critical stress for fracture is equal to

$$
\begin{gathered}
\sigma{c}=\frac{K{I c}}{Y \sqrt{\pi a}} \\
=\frac{54.8 \mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{~m}}}{(1.0) \sqrt{(\pi)\left(0.5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\right)}} \\
=1380 \mathrm{MPa} \quad(200,000 \mathrm{psi})
\end{gathered}
$$

Therefore, fracture will not occur because this specimen will tolerate a stress of 1380 MPa (200,000 psi) before fracture, which is greater than the applied stress of $1030 \mathrm{MPa}(150,000 \mathrm{psi})$.

question: An aircraft component is fabricated from an aluminum alloy that has a plane strain fracture toughness of $40 \mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{m}}$ ( $36.4 \mathrm{ksi} \sqrt{\mathrm{in}}$.). It has been determined that fracture results at a stress of 300 MPa (43,500 psi) when the maximum (or critical) internal crack length is 4.0 mm ( 0.16 in .). For this same component and alloy, will fracture occur at a stress level of 260 MPa (38,000 psi) when the maximum internal crack length is 6.0 mm (0.24 in.)? Why or why not?
solution:
We are asked to determine if an aircraft component will fracture for a given fracture toughness (40 $\mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{m}}$ ), stress level ( 260 MPa ), and maximum internal crack length ( 6.0 mm ), given that fracture occurs for the same component using the same alloy for another stress level and internal crack length. (Note: Because the cracks are internal, their lengths are equal to $2 a$.) It first becomes necessary to solve for the parameter $Y$, using equation (K_{I c}=Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}) , for the conditions under which fracture occurred (i.e., $\sigma=300 \mathrm{MPa}$ and $2 a=4.0 \mathrm{~mm}$ ). Therefore,

$$
Y=\frac{K{I c}}{\sigma \sqrt{\pi a}}=\frac{40 \mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{~m}}}{(300 \mathrm{MPa}) \sqrt{(\pi)\left(\frac{4 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}}{2}\right)}}=1.68
$$

Now we will solve for the product $Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ for the other set of conditions, so as to ascertain whether or not this value is greater than the $K{I c}$ for the alloy. Thus,

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a} & =(1.68)(260 \mathrm{MPa}) \sqrt{(\pi)\left(\frac{6 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}}{2}\right)} \\
& =42.4 \mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{~m}} \quad(39 \mathrm{ksi} \sqrt{\mathrm{in}})
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, fracture will occur since this value $(42.4 \mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{m}})$ is greater than the $K{I c}$ of the material, $40 \mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{m}}$.

question: Suppose that a wing component on an aircraft is fabricated from an aluminum alloy that has a plane-strain fracture toughness of $26.0 \mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{m}}(23.7 \mathrm{ksi} \sqrt{\text { in. }}$ ). It has been determined that fracture results at a stress of 112 MPa (16,240 psi) when the maximum internal crack length is 8.6 mm ( 0.34 in .). For this same component and alloy, compute the stress level at which fracture will occur for a critical internal crack length of 6.0 mm ( 0.24 in.).
solution:
This problem asks us to determine the stress level at which an a wing component on an aircraft will fracture for a given fracture toughness $(26 \mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{m}})$ and maximum internal crack length $(6.0 \mathrm{~mm})$, given that fracture occurs for the same component using the same alloy at one stress level ( 112 MPa ) and another internal crack length ( 8.6 mm ). (Note: Because the cracks are internal, their lengths are equal to $2 a$.) It first becomes necessary to solve for the parameter $Y$ for the conditions under which fracture occurred using equation (K_{I c}=Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}). Therefore,

$$
Y=\frac{K{I c}}{\sigma \sqrt{\pi a}}=\frac{26 \mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{~m}}}{(112 \mathrm{MPa}) \sqrt{ }(\pi)\left(\frac{8.6 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}}{2}\right)}=2.0
$$

Now we will solve for $\sigma{c}$ (for a crack length of 6 mm ) using equation (\sigma_c=\frac{K_{I c}}{Y \sqrt{\pi a}}) as

$$
\sigma{c}=\frac{K{I c}}{Y \sqrt{\pi a}}=\frac{26 \mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{~m}}}{(2.0) \sqrt{ }(\pi)\left(\frac{6 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}}{2}\right)}=134 \mathrm{MPa} \quad(19,300 \mathrm{psi})
$$

question: A structural component is fabricated from an alloy that has a plane-strain fracture toughness of $62 \mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{m}}$. It has been determined that this component fails at a stress of 250 MPa when the maximum length of a surface crack is 1.6 mm . What is the maximum allowable surface crack length (in mm) without fracture for this same component exposed to a stress of 250 MPa and made from another alloy that has a plane strain fracture toughness of $51 \mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{m}}$ ?

A large plate is fabricated from a steel alloy that has a plane strain fracture toughness of $82.4 \mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{m}}$ ( $75.0 \mathrm{ksi} \sqrt{\mathrm{in}}$.). If the plate is exposed to a tensile stress of 345 MPa (50,000 psi) during service use, determine the minimum length of a surface crack that will lead to fracture. Assume a value of 1.0 for $Y$.
solution:
For this problem, we are given values of $K{I c}(82.4 \mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{m}}), \sigma(345 \mathrm{MPa})$, and $Y(1.0)$ for a large plate and are asked to determine the minimum length of a surface crack that will lead to fracture. All we need do is to solve for $a{c}$ using equation (a_c=\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\frac{K_{I c}}{\sigma Y}\right)^2); therefore

$$
\begin{gathered}
a{c}=\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\frac{K{I c}}{Y \sigma}\right)^{2} \\
=\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\frac{82.4 \mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{~m}}}{(1.0)(345 \mathrm{MPa})}\right]^{2} \\
=0.0182 \mathrm{~m}=18.2 \mathrm{~mm} \quad(0.72 \mathrm{in} .)
\end{gathered}
$$

solution:
This problem asks us to calculate the maximum internal crack length allowable for the Ti-6Al-4V titanium alloy in  table 9.1  given that it is loaded to a stress level equal to one-half of its yield strength. For this alloy, $K{I c}=55 \mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{m}}$; also, $\sigma=\sigma{\mathrm{y}} / 2=(910 \mathrm{MPa}) / 2=455 \mathrm{MPa}$. Now solving for $2 a{c}$ (since this crack is an internal one) using equation (a_c=\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\frac{K_{I c}}{\sigma Y}\right)^2) yields

$$
\begin{gathered}
2 a{c}=\frac{2}{\pi}\left(\frac{K{I c}}{\sigma Y}\right)^{2} \\
=\frac{2}{\pi}\left[\frac{55 \mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{~m}}}{(1.5)(455 \mathrm{MPa})}\right]^{2} \\
=0.0041 \mathrm{~m}=4.1 \mathrm{~mm} \quad(0.16 \mathrm{in} .)

$$

question: A structural component in the form of a wide plate is to be fabricated from a steel alloy that has a planestrain fracture toughness of $98.9 \mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{~m}}(90 \mathrm{ksi} \sqrt{\text { in. }})$ and a yield strength of 860 MPa (125,000 psi). The flaw size resolution limit of the flaw detection apparatus is 3.0 mm ( 0.12 in .). If the design stress is one-half the yield strength and the value of $Y$ is 1.0, determine whether a critical flaw for this plate is subject to detection.
solution:
This problem asks that we determine whether or not a critical flaw in a wide plate is subject to detection given the limit of the flaw detection apparatus ( 3.0 mm ), the value of $K{I c}(98.9 \mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{m}})$, the design stress $\left(\sigma{\mathrm{y}} / 2\right.$ in which $\sigma{y}=860 \mathrm{MPa}$ ), and $Y=1.0$. We first need to compute the value of $a{c}$ using equation (a_c=\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\frac{K_{I c}}{\sigma Y}\right)^2); thus

$$
\begin{gathered}
a{c}=\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\frac{K{I c}}{\sigma Y}\right)^{2} \\
=\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\frac{98.9 \mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{~m}}}{(1.0)\left(\frac{860 \mathrm{MPa}}{2}\right)}\right]^{2} \\
=0.0168 \mathrm{~m}=16.8 \mathrm{~mm} \quad(0.66 \mathrm{in} .)
\end{gathered}
$$

Therefore, the critical flaw is subject to detection since this value of $a{c}(16.8 \mathrm{~mm})$ is greater than the 3.0 mm resolution limit.

The fracture strength of glass may be increased by etching away a thin surface layer. It is believed that the etching may alter surface crack geometry (i.e., reduce the crack length and increase the tip radius). Compute the ratio of the etched and original crack-tip radii for a fourfold increase in fracture strength if half of the crack length is removed.
solution:
This problem asks that we compute the crack tip radius ratio before and after etching. Let

$$
\begin{gathered}
\rho{t}=\text { original crack tip radius, and } \\
\rho{t}^{\prime}=\text { etched crack tip radius }
\end{gathered}
$$

Also, as given in the problem statement

$$
\begin{gathered}
\sigma{f}^{\prime}=\sigma{f} \\
a^{\prime}=\frac{a}{2} \\
\sigma{0}^{\prime}=4 \sigma{0}
\end{gathered}
$$

When we incorporate the above relationships into two expressions of equation (\sigma_m=2 \sigma_0\left(\frac{a}{\rho_t}\right)^{1 / 2}) as follows:

$$
\sigma{f}=2 \sigma{0}\left(\frac{a}{\rho{t}}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}=\sigma{f}^{\prime}=2 \sigma{0}^{\prime}\left(\frac{a^{\prime}}{\rho{t}^{\prime}}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}
$$

We now solve for the $\frac{\rho{t}^{\prime}}{\rho{t}}$ ratio, which yields the following:

$$
\frac{\rho{t}^{\prime}}{\rho{t}}=\left(\frac{\sigma{0}^{\prime}}{\sigma{0}}\right)^{2}\left(\frac{a^{\prime}}{a}\right)=\left(\frac{4 \sigma{0}}{\sigma{0}}\right)^{2}\left(\frac{\frac{a}{2}}{a}\right)=8
$$

which is the solution requested in the problem statement.

question:
The following tabulated data were gathered from a series of Charpy impact tests on a tempered 4340 steel alloy.

| Temperature $\left({ }^{\circ} \mathbf{C}\right)$ | Impact Energy $(\boldsymbol{J})$ |
| :--: | :--: |

| -25 | 104 |
| -50 | 103 |

| -100 | 63 |
| -113 | 40 |
| -125 | 34 |
| -150 | 28 |
| -175 | 25 |
| -200 | 24 |

(a) Determine a ductile-to-brittle transition temperature as the temperature corresponding to the average of the maximum and minimum impact energies.
(b) Determine a ductile-to-brittle transition temperature as the temperature at which the impact energy is 50 J .

Give your answer as a tuple (answer to (a), answer to (b)).
solution:
(a) The average of the maximum and minimum impact energies from the data is

$$
\text { Average }=\frac{105 \mathrm{~J}+24 \mathrm{~J}}{2}=64.5 \mathrm{~J}
$$

As indicated on the plot by the one set of dashed lines, the ductile-to-brittle transition temperature according to this criterion is about $-100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
(b) Also, as noted on the plot by the other set of dashed lines, the ductile-to-brittle transition temperature for an impact energy of 50 J is about $-110^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

question: What is the maximum carbon content possible for a plain carbon steel that must have an impact energy of at least 200 J at $-50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ?
solution: From the curves in  figure 9.23 , for only the $0.11 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{C}$ and $0.01 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{C}$ steels are impact energies (and therefore, ductile-to-brittle temperatures) greater than 200 J . Therefore, $0.11 \mathrm{wt} \%$ is the maximum carbon concentration.

question:
A fatigue test was conducted in which the mean stress was 70 MPa (10,000 psi), and the stress amplitude was 210 MPa (30,000 psi).
(a) Compute the maximum and minimum stress levels.
(b) Compute the stress ratio.
(c) Compute the magnitude of the stress range.

Give your answer as a tuple (the maximum stress level, the minimum stress level, the stress ratio, the magnitude of the stress range).
solution:
(a) Given the values of $\sigma_{\mathrm{m}}(70 \mathrm{MPa})$ and $\sigma_{a}(210 \mathrm{MPa})$ we are asked to compute $\sigma_{\max }$ and $\sigma_{\min }$. From equation (\sigma_m=\frac{\sigma_{\max }+\sigma_{\min }}{2})

$$
\sigma_{m}=\frac{\sigma_{\max }+\sigma_{\min }}{2}=70 \mathrm{MPa}
$$

Or,

$$
\sigma_{\max }+\sigma_{\min }=140 \mathrm{MPa}
$$

Furthermore, utilization of equation (\sigma_a=\frac{\sigma_r}{2}=\frac{\sigma_{\max }-\sigma_{\min }}{2}) yields

$$
\sigma_{a}=\frac{\sigma_{\max }-\sigma_{\min }}{2}=210 \mathrm{MPa}
$$

Or,

$$
\sigma_{\max }-\sigma_{\min }=420 \mathrm{MPa}
$$

Simultaneously solving equation ((\sigma_{\max }+\sigma_{\min }=140 \mathrm{MPa}) and (\sigma_{\max }-\sigma_{\min }=420 \mathrm{MPa})) leads to

$$
\begin{gathered}
\sigma_{\max }=280 \mathrm{MPa}(40,000 \mathrm{psi}) \\
\sigma_{\min }=-140 \mathrm{MPa}(-20,000 \mathrm{psi})
\end{gathered}
$$

(b) Using equation (R=\frac{\sigma_{\min }}{\sigma_{\max }}) the stress ratio $R$ is determined as follows:

$$
R=\frac{\sigma_{\min }}{\sigma_{\max }}=\frac{-140 \mathrm{MPa}}{280 \mathrm{MPa}}=-0.50
$$

(c) The magnitude of the stress range $\sigma_{r}$ is determined using equation (\sigma_r=\sigma_{\max }-\sigma_{\min }) as

$$
\begin{gathered}
\sigma_{r}=\sigma_{\max }-\sigma_{\min }=280 \mathrm{MPa}-(-140 \mathrm{MPa}) \\
=420 \mathrm{MPa}(60,000 \mathrm{psi})
\end{gathered}
$$

question:
The following creep data were taken on an aluminum alloy at $480^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\left(900^{\circ} \mathrm{F}\right)$ and a constant stress of 2.75 MPa (400 psi). Determine the steady-state or minimum creep rate. Note: The initial and instantaneous strain is not included.

| Time $(\mathbf{m i n})$ | Strain | Time $(\mathbf{m i n})$ | Strain |
| :--: | :--: | :--: | :--: |
| 0 | 0.00 | 18 | 0.82 |
| 2 | 0.22 | 20 | 0.88 |
| 4 | 0.34 | 22 | 0.95 |
| 6 | 0.41 | 24 | 1.03 |
| 8 | 0.48 | 26 | 1.12 |
| 10 | 0.55 | 28 | 1.22 |
| 12 | 0.62 | 30 | 1.36 |
| 14 | 0.68 | 32 | 1.53 |
| 16 | 0.75 | 34 | 1.77 |
solution:
These creep data are plotted below


The steady-state creep rate $(\Delta \varepsilon / \Delta t)$ is the slope of the linear region (i.e., the straight line that has been superimposed on the curve). Taking strain values at 0 min and 30 min , leads to the following:

$$
\frac{\Delta \varepsilon}{\Delta t}=\frac{1.20-0.25}{30 \mathrm{~min}-0 \mathrm{~min}}=3.2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~min}^{-1}
$$

question: A specimen 975 mm (38.4 in.) long of an S-590 alloy ( figure 9.40 ) is to be exposed to a tensile stress of 300 MPa (43,500 psi) at $730^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\left(1350^{\circ} \mathrm{F}\right)$. Determine its elongation after 4.0 h . Assume that the total of both instantaneous and primary creep elongations is 2.5 mm ( 0.10 in.).
solution:
From the $730^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ line in  figure 9.40 , the steady state creep rate $\dot{\varepsilon}{s}$ is about $1.0 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~h}^{-1}$ at 300 MPa . The steady state creep strain, $\varepsilon{s}$, therefore, is just the product of $\dot{\varepsilon}{s}$ and time as

$$
\begin{gathered}
\varepsilon{s}=\dot{\varepsilon}{s} \times(\text { time }) \\
=\left(1.0 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~h}^{-1}\right)(4.0 \mathrm{~h})=0.040
\end{gathered}
$$

Strain and elongation are related as in equation (\varepsilon=\frac{l_i-l_0}{l_0}=\frac{\Delta l}{l_0})—viz.

$$
\varepsilon=\frac{\Delta l}{l{0}}
$$

Now, using the steady-state elongation, $l{0}$, provided in the problem statement ( 975 mm ) and the value of $\varepsilon{s}$, determined above ( 0.040 ), the steady-state elongation, $\Delta l{s}$, is determined as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
\Delta l{s}=l{0} \varepsilon{s} \\
=(975 \mathrm{~mm})(0.040)=39.0 \mathrm{~mm}(1.54 \mathrm{in} .)
\end{gathered}
$$

Finally, the total elongation is just the sum of this $\Delta l{s}$ and the total of both instantaneous and primary creep elongations [i.e., $2.5 \mathrm{~mm}(0.10 \mathrm{in}$.)]. Therefore, the total elongation is $39.0 \mathrm{~mm}+2.5 \mathrm{~mm}=41.5 \mathrm{~mm}$ (1.64 in.).

question: For a cylindrical S-590 alloy specimen ( figure 9.40 ) originally 14.5 mm ( 0.57 in.) in diameter and 400 mm (15.7 in.) long, what tensile load is necessary to produce a total elongation of 52.7 mm (2.07 in.) after 1150 h at $650^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\left(1200^{\circ} \mathrm{F}\right)$ ? Assume that the sum of instantaneous and primary creep elongations is 4.3 mm ( 0.17 in.).
solution:
It is first necessary to calculate the steady state creep rate so that we may utilize  figure 9.40  in order to determine the tensile stress. The steady state elongation, $\Delta t{s}$, is just the difference between the total elongation and the sum of the instantaneous and primary creep elongations; that is,

$$
\Delta t{s}=52.7 \mathrm{~mm}-4.3 \mathrm{~mm}=48.4 \mathrm{~mm} \quad(1.90 \mathrm{in} .)
$$

Now the steady state creep rate, $\dot{\varepsilon}{s}$, is just

$$
\begin{gathered}
\dot{\varepsilon}{s}=\frac{\Delta \varepsilon}{\Delta t} \\
=\frac{\frac{\Delta t{s}}{t{0}}}{\Delta t}=\frac{\frac{48.4 \mathrm{~mm}}{400 \mathrm{~mm}}}{1150 \mathrm{~h}} \\
=1.05 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~h}^{-1}
\end{gathered}
$$

Employing the $650^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ line in  figure 9.40 , a steady state creep rate of $1.05 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~h}^{-1}$ corresponds to a stress $\sigma$ of about 300 MPa (or $43,500 \mathrm{psi}$ ). From this we may compute the tensile load (for this cylindrical specimen) using equation (\sigma=\frac{F}{A_0}) as follows:

$$
F=\sigma A{0}=\sigma \pi\left(\frac{d{0}}{2}\right)^{2}
$$

Here $d{0}$ is the original cross-sectional diameter ( $14.5 \mathrm{~mm}=14.5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}$ ). Solving this equation for tensile load, incorporating the value of stress determined from  figure 9.40  above ( $300 \mathrm{MPa}=300 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$ ) leads to

$$
F=\sigma \pi\left(\frac{d{0}}{2}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
\begin{gathered}
=\left(300 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}\right)(\pi)\left(\frac{14.5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}}{2}\right)^{2} \\
=49,500 \mathrm{~N} \quad\left(11,100 \mathrm{lb}{\Gamma}\right)
\end{gathered}
$$
