\hline $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}+2 e^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Zn}$ & -0.763 \\
\hline $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}+3 e^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Al}$ & -1.662 \\
\hline $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}+2 e^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Mg}$ & -2.363 \\
\hline $\mathrm{Na}^{+}+e^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Na}$ & -2.714 \\
\hline $\mathrm{K}^{+}+e^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{K}$ & -2.924 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}"
solution:
This problem asks for us to calculate the temperature for a zinc-lead electrochemical cell when the potential between the Zn and Pb electrodes is +0.568 V . On the basis of their relative positions in the standard emf series ( table 16.1 ), assume that Zn is oxidized and Pb is reduced. Thus, the electrochemical reaction that occurs within this cell is as follows:

$$
\mathrm{Pb}^{2+}+\mathrm{Zn} \rightarrow \mathrm{Pb}+\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}
$$

Under these circumstances, equation (\Delta V=\left(V_2^0-V_1^0\right)-\frac{0.0592}{n} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{M}_1^{n+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{M}_2^{n+}\right]}) is written in the following form:

$$
\Delta V=\left(V{\mathrm{Pb}}^{0}-V{\mathrm{Zn}}^{0}\right)-\frac{R T}{n F} \ln \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Pb}^{2+}\right]}
$$

(Note: we denote the Faraday constant by an " $F$ " rather than with the symbol used in the textbook.)

Solving this expression for the temperature $T$ gives the following:

$$
T=-\frac{n F}{R}\left[\frac{\Delta V-\left(V{\mathrm{Pb}}^{0}-V{\mathrm{Zn}}^{0}\right)}{\ln \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Pb}^{2+}\right]}}\right]
$$

The standard potentials Zn and Pb from  table 16.1  are as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& V{\mathrm{Zn}}^{0}=-0.763 \mathrm{~V} \\
& V{\mathrm{Pb}}^{0}=-0.126 \mathrm{~V}
\end{aligned}
$$

Also $n$ in equation (\Delta V=\left(V_2^0-V_1^0\right)-\frac{0.0592}{n} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{M}_1^{n+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{M}_2^{n+}\right]}) has a value of 2 since twos electrons are involved in the above electrochemical reaction. Therefore, we solve for the temperature as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
T=-\frac{(2)(96,500 \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{mol})}{8.31 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol} \cdot \mathrm{~K}}\left[\frac{0.568 \mathrm{~V}-\{-0.126 \mathrm{~V}-(-0.763 \mathrm{~V})\}}{\ln \left(\frac{10^{-2} M}{10^{-4} M}\right)}\right] \\
=348 \mathrm{~K}=75^{\circ} \mathrm{C}
\end{gathered}
$$

question:
Compute the constant $K$ in $t=\frac{K W}{\rho A(\mathrm{CPR})}$  will for the CPR in units of mpy and $\mathrm{mm} / \mathrm{yr}$, respectively.

Give your answer as a tuple ($K$ in units of mpy, $K$ in units of $\mathrm{mm} / \mathrm{yr}$).
solution:
This problem is just an exercise in unit conversions. The parameter $K$ in equation (\mathrm{CPR}=\frac{K W}{\rho A t}) must convert the units of $W, \rho, A$, and $t$, into the unit scheme for the CPR.

For CPR in mpy (\frac{mil}{yr})

$$
\begin{gathered}
K=\frac{W(\mathrm{mg})(1 \mathrm{~g} / 1000 \mathrm{mg})}{\rho\left(\frac{\mathrm{g}}{\mathrm{~cm}^{3}}\right)\left(\frac{2.54 \mathrm{~cm}}{\mathrm{in}}\right)^{3}\left[A\left(\mathrm{in}^{2}\right)\right]\left(\frac{1 \mathrm{in} .}{1000 \mathrm{mil}}\right)[t(\mathrm{~h})]\left(\frac{1 \mathrm{day}}{24 \mathrm{~h}}\right)\left(\frac{1 \mathrm{yr}}{365 \text { days }}\right)} \\
$=534.6$
\end{gathered}
$$

For CPR in $\mathrm{mm} / \mathrm{yr}$

$$
\begin{gathered}
K=\frac{W(\mathrm{mg})(1 \mathrm{~g} / 1000 \mathrm{mg})}{\rho\left(\frac{\mathrm{g}}{\mathrm{~cm}^{3}}\right)\left(\frac{1 \mathrm{~cm}}{10 \mathrm{~mm}}\right)^{3}\left[A\left(\mathrm{~cm}^{2}\right)\right]\left(\frac{10 \mathrm{~mm}}{\mathrm{~cm}}\right)^{2}[t(\mathrm{~h})]\left(\frac{1 \mathrm{day}}{24 \mathrm{~h}}\right)\left(\frac{1 \mathrm{yr}}{365 \text { days }}\right)} \\
$=87.6$
\end{gathered}
$$

question: A piece of corroded metal alloy plate was found in a submerged ocean vessel. It was estimated that the original area of the plate was $800 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ and that approximately 7.6 kg had corroded away during the submersion. Assuming a corrosion penetration rate of $4 \mathrm{~mm} / \mathrm{yr}$ for this alloy in seawater, estimate the time of submersion in years. The density of the alloy is $4.5 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}$.

A thick steel sheet of area 100 in. ${ }^{2}$ is exposed to air near the ocean. After a one-year period it was found to experience a weight loss of 485 g due to corrosion. To what rate of corrosion, in both mpy and $\mathrm{mm} / \mathrm{yr}$, does this correspond? The density of steel is $7.9 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^3$.

Give your answer as a tuple (the rate of corrosion in mpy, the rate of corrosion in mm/yr).

(a) Demonstrate that the CPR is related to the corrosion current density $i\left(A / \mathrm{cm}^{2}\right)$ through the expression

$$
\mathrm{CPR}=\frac{K A i}{n \rho}
$$

where $K$ is a constant, $A$ is the atomic weight of the metal experiencing corrosion, $n$ is the number of electrons associated with the ionization of each metal atom, and $\rho$ is the density of the metal.
(b) Calculate the value of the constant $K$ for the CPR in mpy and $i$ in $\mu A / \mathrm{cm}^{2}\left(10^{-6} \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{cm}^{2}\right)$.
solution:
Possibly the best way to conduct this demonstration is by using a unit dimensional analysis. The corrosion rate, $r$, in equation (r=\frac{i}{n \mathscr{F}}) has the units (SI) of

$$
r=\frac{i}{n F}=\frac{\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m}^{2}-\mathrm{s}}{(\text { unitless })(\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{mol})}=\frac{\mathrm{mol}}{\mathrm{~m}^{2}-\mathrm{s}}
$$

(Note: we denote the Faraday constant by an " $F$ " rather than with the symbol used in the textbook.)

The units of CPR in equation (\mathrm{CPR}=\frac{K W}{\rho A t}) are \frac{length}{time}, or in the SI scheme, $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$. In order to convert the above expression to the units of $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$ it is necessary to multiply $r$ by the atomic weight $A$ and divide by the density $\rho$ as follows:

$$
\frac{r A}{\rho}=\frac{\left(\mathrm{mol} / \mathrm{m}^{2}-\mathrm{s}\right)(\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{mol})}{\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{m}^{3}}=\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}
$$

From this dimensional analysis, and since we can convert the units corrosion rate $r$ to units of corrosion penetration rate CPR using equation (\frac{r A}{\rho}=\frac{\left(\mathrm{mol} / \mathrm{m}^2-\mathrm{s}\right)(\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{mol})}{\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{m}^3}=\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}), then it is the case that the CPR is proportional to $r$ according to the following expression:

$$
\mathrm{CPR}=K{1}\left(\frac{r A}{\rho}\right)
$$

Here $K{1}$ is a constant, and the units for the CPR are correct. If we now substitute the expression for $r$ in equation (r=\frac{i}{n \mathscr{F}}) into equation (\mathrm{CPR}=K_1\left(\frac{r A}{\rho}\right)), the following expression results:

$$
\mathrm{CPR}=K{1}\left(\frac{i}{n F}\right)\left(\frac{A}{\rho}\right)=\frac{K{1} i A}{n F \rho}
$$

Inasmuch as both $K{1}$ and $F$ in equation (\mathrm{CPR}=K_1\left(\frac{i}{n F}\right)\left(\frac{A}{\rho}\right)=\frac{K_1 i A}{n F \rho}) are constants, then their ratio is also a constant, and if we designate that constant $K$, then

$$
K=\frac{K{1}}{F}
$$

and equation (\mathrm{CPR}=K_1\left(\frac{i}{n F}\right)\left(\frac{A}{\rho}\right)=\frac{K_1 i A}{n F \rho}) becomes

$$
\mathrm{CPR}=\frac{K A i}{n \rho}
$$

Which is equation (\mathrm{CPR}=\frac{K A i}{n \rho}) .
(b) Now we will calculate the value of $K$ in order to give the CPR in mpy for $i$ in $\mu \mathrm{A} / \mathrm{cm}^{2}\left(10^{-6} \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{cm}^{2}\right)$. It should be noted that the units of A (in $\mu \mathrm{A} / \mathrm{cm}^{2}$ ) are amperes or $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{s}$. Substitution of the units normally used in equation (\mathrm{CPR}=K_1\left(\frac{i}{n F}\right)\left(\frac{A}{\rho}\right)=\frac{K_1 i A}{n F \rho})—viz.,

$$
\mathrm{CPR}=K{1} \frac{A i}{n F \rho}
$$

leads to the following:

$$
=K{1} \frac{(\mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol})\left(\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{s}-\mathrm{cm}^{2}\right)}{(\text { unitless })(\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{mol})\left(\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}\right)}=\mathrm{cm} / \mathrm{s}
$$

Since we want the CPR in mpy, that $i$ is given in $\mu \mathrm{A} / \mathrm{cm}^{2}$, and realizing that $K=K{1} / F$, leads to the following:

$$
K=\left(\frac{1}{96,500 \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{mol}}\right)\left(\frac{10^{-6} \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{cm}^{2}}{\mu \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{cm}^{2}}\right)\left(\frac{1 \mathrm{in} .}{2.54 \mathrm{~cm}}\right)\left(\frac{10^{3} \mathrm{mil}}{\mathrm{in} .}\right)\left(\frac{3.1536 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{~s}}{\mathrm{yr}}\right)
$$

question:
Nickel experiences corrosion in an acid solution according to the reaction

$$
\mathrm{Ni}+2 \mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}+\mathrm{H}{2}
$$

The rates of both oxidation and reduction half-reactions are controlled by activation polarization.
(a) Compute the rate of oxidation of Ni (in $\mathrm{mol} / \mathrm{cm}^{2}-\mathrm{s}$ ), given the following activation polarization data:

For Nickel

$$
\begin{aligned}
V{\left(\mathrm{Ni} / \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}\right)}=-0.25 \mathrm{~V} & V{\left(\mathrm{H}^{+} / \mathrm{H}{2}\right)}=0 \mathrm{~V} \\
i{0}=10^{-8} \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{cm}^{2} & i{0}=6 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{cm}^{2} \\
\beta=+0.12 & \beta=-0.10
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute the rate of oxidation and value of the corrosion potential.

Give your answer as a tuple (the rate of oxidation, the corrosion potential).
solution:
(a) This portion of the problem asks that we compute the rate of oxidation for Ni given that both the oxidation and reduction reactions are controlled by activation polarization, and also given the polarization data for both nickel oxidation and hydrogen reduction. The first thing necessary is to establish relationships of the form of equation (\eta_a= \pm \beta \log \frac{i}{i_0}) for the potentials of both oxidation and reduction reactions. Next we will set these expressions equal to one another, and then solve for the value of $i$ which is really the corrosion current density, $i{c}$. Finally, the corrosion rate may be calculated using equation (r=\frac{i}{n \mathscr{F}}). The two potential expressions are as follows:

For hydrogen reduction

$$
V{\mathrm{H}}=V{\left(\mathrm{H}^{+} / \mathrm{H}{2}\right)}+\beta{\mathrm{H}} \log \left(\frac{i}{i{0{\mathrm{H}}}}\right)
$$

And for Ni oxidation

$$
V{\mathrm{Ni}}=V{\left(\mathrm{Ni} / \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}\right)}+\beta{\mathrm{Ni}} \log \left(\frac{i}{i{0{\mathrm{Ni}}}}\right)
$$

Setting $V{\mathrm{H}}=V{\mathrm{Ni}}$ and solving for $\log i$ (i.e., $\log i{c}$ ) leads to the following expression:

$$
\log i{c}=\left(\frac{1}{\beta{\mathrm{Ni}}-\beta{\mathrm{H}}}\right)\left[V{\left(\mathrm{H}^{+} / \mathrm{H}{2}\right)}-V{\left(\mathrm{Ni} / \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}\right)}-\beta{\mathrm{H}} \log i{0{\mathrm{H}}}+\beta{\mathrm{Ni}} \log i{0{\mathrm{Ni}}}\right]
$$

And, incorporating values for the various parameters provided in the problem statement leads to

$$
\begin{gathered}
\log i{c}=\left[\frac{1}{0.12-(-0.10)}\right]\left[0-(-0.25)-(-0.10)\left\{\log \left(6 \times 10^{-7}\right)\right\}+(0.12)\left\{\log \left(10^{-8}\right)\right\}\right] \\
$=-6.055$
\end{gathered}
$$

From which $i{c}$ may be determined as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
i{c}=10^{-6.055}=8.81 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{cm}^{2} \\
=8.81 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{s}-\mathrm{cm}^{2}
\end{gathered}
$$

Now, from equation (r=\frac{i}{n \mathscr{F}})

$$
r=\frac{i{c}}{n F}
$$

we compute the oxidation rate as follows:

$$
r=\frac{8.81 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{s}-\mathrm{cm}^{2}}{(2)(96,500 \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{mol})}=4.56 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{cm}^{2}-\mathrm{s}
$$

(Note: in the above discussion we denote the Faraday constant by an " $F$ " rather than with the symbol used in the textbook.)
(b) Now we are asked to compute the value of the corrosion potential, $V{c}$. This is possible by using either of the above equations for $V{\mathrm{H}}$ or $V{\mathrm{Ni}}$ and substituting for $i$ the value determined above for $i{c}$. This is done as follows (using the expression for hydrogen reduction):

$$
\begin{gathered}
V{c}=V{\left(\mathrm{H}^{+} / \mathrm{H}{2}\right)}+\beta{\mathrm{H}} \log \left(\frac{i{c}}{i{0{\mathrm{H}}}}\right) \\
=0+(-0.10 \mathrm{~V}) \log \left(\frac{8.81 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{cm}^{2}}{6 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{cm}^{2}}\right)=-0.0167 \mathrm{~V}
\end{gathered}
$$

question:
For each of the metals listed in the following table, compute the Pilling-Bedworth ratio. Also, on the basis of this value, specify whether you would expect the oxide scale that forms on the surface to be protective, and then justify your decision. Density data for both the metal and its oxide are also tabulated. Give your answer as a tuple: (Pilling-Bedworth ratios for Mg,Pilling-Bedworth ratios for V,Pilling-Bedworth ratios for Zn). | Metal | Metal Density
$\left(\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}\right)$ | Metal Oxide | Oxide Density
$\left(\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}\right)$ | | :--: | :--: | :--: | :--: | | $M g$ | 1.74 | $M g O$ | 3.58 | | $V$ | 6.11 | $V{2} O{3}$ | 3.36 | | Zn | 7.13 | ZnO | 5.61 |
solution:
For this problem we are given, for three metals, their densities, oxide chemical formulas, and oxide densities, and are asked to compute the Pilling-Bedworth ratios, and then to specify whether or not the oxide scales that form will be protective. The form of the equation used to calculate this ratio for some metal is either equation (\mathrm{P}-\mathrm{B} \text { ratio }=\frac{A_{\mathrm{O}} \rho_{\mathrm{M}}}{A_{\mathrm{M}} \rho_{\mathrm{O}}}) or equation (\mathrm{P}-\mathrm{B} \text { ratio }=\frac{A_{\mathrm{O}} \rho_{\mathrm{M}}}{a A_{\mathrm{M}} \rho_{\mathrm{O}}}). For magnesium, oxidation occurs by the following reaction:

$$
\mathrm{Mg}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{MgO}
$$

Therefore, we use equation (\mathrm{P}-\mathrm{B} \text { ratio }=\frac{A_{\mathrm{O}} \rho_{\mathrm{M}}}{A_{\mathrm{M}} \rho_{\mathrm{O}}}) to make the calculation as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
\mathrm{P}-\mathrm{B} \text { ratio }=\frac{A{\mathrm{MgO}} \rho{\mathrm{Mg}}}{A{\mathrm{Mg}} \rho{\mathrm{MgO}}} \\
=\frac{(40.31 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol})\left(1.74 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}\right)}{(24.31 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol})\left(3.58 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}\right)}=0.81
\end{gathered}
$$

(In this computation we used $24.31 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$ and $40.31 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$, respectively for the atomic weights of Mg and MgO .)

Thus, this would probably be a nonprotective oxide film since the P-B ratio is less than unity; to be protective, this ratio should be between one and two.

The oxidation reaction for V is given by the following reaction:

$$
2 \mathrm{~V}+\frac{5}{2} \mathrm{O}{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{~V}{2} \mathrm{O}{5}
$$

We calculate the P-B ratio using equation (\mathrm{P}-\mathrm{B} \text { ratio }=\frac{A_{\mathrm{O}} \rho_{\mathrm{M}}}{a A_{\mathrm{M}} \rho_{\mathrm{O}}}) as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
\mathrm{P}-\mathrm{B} \text { ratio }=\frac{A{\mathrm{V}{2} \mathrm{O}{5}} \rho{\mathrm{V}}}{(2) A{\mathrm{V}} \rho{\mathrm{V}{2} \mathrm{O}{5}}} \\
=\frac{\left(181.88 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}\right)\left(6.11 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}\right)}{(2)\left(50.94 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}\right)\left(3.36 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}\right)}=3.25
\end{gathered}
$$

Hence, the film would be nonprotective since the ratio does not lie between one and two.

Now for Zn , the reaction for its oxidation is as follows:

$$
\mathrm{Zn}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{ZnO}
$$

Therefore, the Pilling-Bedworth ratio is determined as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
\mathrm{P}-\mathrm{B} \text { ratio }=\frac{A{\mathrm{ZnO}} \rho{\mathrm{Zn}}}{A{\mathrm{Zn}} \rho{\mathrm{ZnO}}} \\
=\frac{\left(81.41 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}\right)\left(7.13 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}\right)}{(65.41 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol})\left(5.61 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}\right)}=1.58
\end{gathered}
$$

Thus, the ZnO film would probably be protective since the ratio is between one and two.

Estimate the energy required to raise the temperature of $5 \mathrm{~kg}\left(11.0 \mathrm{lb}{m}\right)$ of the following materials from $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $150^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\left(68^{\circ} \mathrm{F}\right.$ to $\left.300^{\circ} \mathrm{F}\right)$ : aluminum, brass, aluminum oxide (alumina), and polypropylene.

Give your answer as a tuple (aluminum, brass, aluminum oxide (alumina), polypropylene).

solution:
The energy, $E$, required to raise the temperature of a given mass of material, $m$, is the product of the specific heat, the mass of material, and the temperature change, $\Delta T$, as

$$
E=c{p} m \Delta T
$$

The $\Delta T$ in this problem is equal to $150^{\circ} \mathrm{C}-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=130^{\circ} \mathrm{C}(=130 \mathrm{~K})$, while the mass is 5 kg , and the specific heats are presented in  table 17.1 , as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& c{p}(\text { aluminum })=900 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg}-\mathrm{K} \\
& c{p}(\text { brass })=375 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg}-\mathrm{K} \\
& c{p}(\text { alumina })=775 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg}-\mathrm{K} \\
& c{p}(\text { polypropylene })=1925 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg}-\mathrm{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, energy values for these four materials are determined as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
E(\text { aluminum })=(900 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg}-\mathrm{K})(5 \mathrm{~kg})(130 \mathrm{~K})=5.85 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~J} \\
E(\text { brass })=(375 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg}-\mathrm{K})(5 \mathrm{~kg})(130 \mathrm{~K})=2.44 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~J} \\
E(\text { alumina })=(775 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg}-\mathrm{K})(5 \mathrm{~kg})(130 \mathrm{~K})=5.04 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~J} \\
E(\text { polypropylene })=(1925 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg}-\mathrm{K})(5 \mathrm{~kg})(130 \mathrm{~K})=1.25 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~J}
\end{gathered}
$$

question: The constant $A$ in equation (C_v=A T^3) is $12 \pi^{4} R / 5 \theta{D}^{3}$, where $R$ is the gas constant and $\theta{D}$ is the Debye temperature (K). Estimate $\theta{D}$ for aluminum, given that the specific heat is $4.60 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg}-\mathrm{K}$ at 15 K . $A^{\prime}=26.98 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$.
solution:
For aluminum, we want to compute the Debye temperature, $\theta{D}$, given the expression for $A$ in equation (C_v=A T^3) and the heat capacity at 15 K . First of all, let us determine the magnitude of $A$ using the following rearranged form of equation (C_v=A T^3):

$$
A=\frac{C{v}}{T^{3}}
$$

Inasmuch as the specific heat is given in the problem statement, and heat capacity is required in the above expression, it is necessary to convert $c{v}$ to $C{v}$, which is possible using the following equation:

$$
C{v}=c{v} A^{\prime}\left(\frac{1 \mathrm{~kg}}{1000 \mathrm{~g}}\right)
$$

Here we use $A^{\prime}$ to represent the atomic weight (so as not to confuse this parameter with the $A$ in equation (C_v=A T^3)). Making the above substitution into equation (A=\frac{C_v}{T^3}) above yields the following expression for $A$ :

$$
A=\left[\frac{c{v} A^{\prime}\left(\frac{1 \mathrm{~kg}}{1000 \mathrm{~g}}\right)}{T^{3}}\right]
$$

We now compute the value of $A$ using the following values for parameters in this equation:
$T=15 \mathrm{~K}$ (given in problem statement)
$c{v}=4.60 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg}-\mathrm{K}$ (given in problem statement)
$A^{\prime}=26.98 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$
Therefore, the $A$ is determined as follows:

$$
A=\left[\frac{c{v} A^{\prime}\left(\frac{1 \mathrm{~kg}}{1000 \mathrm{~g}}\right)}{T^{3}}\right]
$$

$$
\begin{gathered}
=\left[\frac{(4.60 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg}-\mathrm{K})(26.98 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol})\left(\frac{1 \mathrm{~kg}}{1000 \mathrm{~g}}\right)}{(15 \mathrm{~K})^{3}}\right] \\
=3.68 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}-\mathrm{K}^{4}
\end{gathered}
$$

The problem statement cites the following expression for $A$ :

$$
A=\frac{12 \pi^{4} R}{5 \theta{\mathrm{D}}^{3}}
$$

From this equation we solve for the Debye temperature, $\theta{\mathrm{D}}$, as requested as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
\theta{\mathrm{D}}=\left(\frac{12 \pi^{4} R}{5 A}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}} \\
=\left[\frac{(12)(\pi)^{4}(8.31 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}-\mathrm{K})}{(5)\left(3.68 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}-\mathrm{K}^{4}\right)}\right]^{\frac{1}{3}}=375 \mathrm{~K}
\end{gathered}
$$

question: A 0.4-m (15.7-in.) rod of a metal elongates 0.48 mm ( 0.019 in.) on heating from $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\left(68^{\circ} \mathrm{F}\right.$ to $212^{\circ} \mathrm{F}$ ). Determine the value of the linear coefficient of thermal expansion for this material.
solution:
The linear coefficient of thermal expansion for this material may be determined using a rearranged form of equation (\frac{\Delta l}{l_0}=\alpha_l \Delta T) as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
\alpha{l}=\frac{\Delta l}{l{0} \Delta T}=\frac{\Delta l}{l{0}\left(T{f}-T{0}\right)} \\
=\frac{0.48 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}}{(0.4 \mathrm{~m})\left(100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)} \\
=15.0 \times 10^{-6}\left({ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)^{-1} \quad\left[8.40 \times 10^{-6}\left({ }^{\circ} \mathrm{F}\right)^{-1}\right]
\end{gathered}
$$

question:
Compute the density for iron at $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, given that its room-temperature density is $7.870 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}$. Assume that the volume coefficient of thermal expansion, $a{v}$, is equal to $3 a{I}$.

solution:
In this problem we are asked to determine the density of iron at $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Let us use as the basis for this determination $1 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$ of iron at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ that has a mass of 7.870 g . If it is assumed that the mass of iron $(7.870 \mathrm{~g})$ remains constant upon heating, then the density at $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is just this mass divided by the $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ volume; this volume will be greater than $1 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$, as a result of thermal expansion. Let us compute the volume of this specimen of iron when it is heated to $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. A volume expansion expression is given in equation (\frac{\Delta V}{V_0}=\alpha_v \Delta T)-viz.,

$$
\frac{\Delta V}{V{0}}=\alpha{v} \Delta T
$$

or, rearranging leads to

$$
\Delta V=V{0} \alpha{v} \Delta T
$$

But since

$$
\Delta V=V{f}-V{0}
$$

in which $V{f}$ and $V{0}$ represent the final and initial volumes, respectively, we can rewrite equation (\Delta V=V_0 \alpha_v \Delta T) as follows:

$$
V{f}-V{0}=V{0} \alpha{v} \Delta T
$$

From this expression we want to solve for the final volume $V{f}$ at $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$; therefore we may rewrite the above equation as follows:

$$
V{f}=V{0} \alpha{v} \Delta T+V{0}=V{0}\left(1+\alpha{v} \Delta T\right)
$$

Also, $\alpha{v}=3 \alpha{I}$, as noted in the problem statement, which, when substituted into the above equation leads to the following:

$$
V{f}=V{0}\left(1+3 \alpha{I} \Delta T\right)
$$

The value of $\alpha{l}$ for iron given in  table 17.1  is $11.8 \times 10^{-6}\left({ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)^{-1}$. Therefore, the volume of this specimen of Fe at $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\left(V{f}\right)$, using equation (\frac{\Delta V}{V_0}=\alpha_v \Delta T) b is the following:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V{f} & =V{0}\left(1+3 \alpha{l} \Delta T\right) \\
& =\left(1 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}\right)\left\{1+(3)\left[11.8 \times 10^{-6}\left({ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)^{-1}\right]\left(700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)\right\} \\
& =1.02471 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Note: In this computation we have taken room temperature to be $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.) Thus, the $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ density is just the 7.870 g mass divided by this new volume-i.e.,

$$
\rho=\frac{7.870 \mathrm{~g}}{1.02471 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}}=7.680 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}
$$

solution:
This problem asks for us to determine the temperature to which a cylindrical rod of tungsten 15.025 mm in diameter must be heated in order for it of just fit into a 15.000 mm diameter circular hole in a plate of 1025 steel (which, of course, is also heated), assuming that the initial temperature is $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. This requires the use of equation (\frac{l_f-l_0}{l_0}=\alpha_l\left(T_f-T_0\right)), which is applied to the diameters of both the rod and hole. That is

$$
\frac{d{f}-d{0}}{d{0}}=\alpha{l}\left(T{f}-T{0}\right)
$$

Solving this expression for the final diameter $d{f}$ yields the following

$$
d{f}=d{0}\left[1+\alpha{l}\left(T{f}-T{0}\right)\right]
$$

Now all we need do is to establish expressions for $d{f}($ steel $)$ and $d{f}(\mathrm{~W})$, set them equal to one another, and solve for $T{f}$. According to  table 17.1 , $\alpha{l}($ steel $)=12.0 \times 10^{-6}\left({ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)^{-1}$ and $\alpha{l}(\mathrm{~W})=4.5 \times 10^{-6}\left({ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)^{-1}$. Thus

$$
\begin{gathered}
d{f}(\text { steel })=d{f}(\mathrm{~W}) \\
(15.000 \mathrm{~mm})\left[1+\left\{12.0 \times 10^{-6}\left({ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)^{-1}\right\}\left(T{f}-25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)\right] \\
=(15.025 \mathrm{~mm})\left[1+\left\{4.5 \times 10^{-6}\left({ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)^{-1}\right\}\left(T{f}-25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)\right]
\end{gathered}
$$

Now solving for $T{f}$ gives $T{f}=247.4^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

solution:
(a) The steady-state heat flux through the plate may be computed using equation (q=-k \frac{d T}{d x}); the thermal conductivity for brass, found in  table 17.1 , is $120 \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{m}-\mathrm{K}$. Therefore,

$$
\begin{gathered}
q=-k \frac{\Delta T}{\Delta x} \\
=-(120 \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{m}-\mathrm{K})\left[\frac{(50+273 \mathrm{~K})-(150+273 \mathrm{~K})}{7.5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}}\right] \\
=1.60 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{m}^{2}
\end{gathered}
$$

(b) Let $d \frac{Q}{d} t$ represent the total heat loss such that

$$
\frac{d Q}{d t}=q A t
$$

where $A$ and $t$ are the cross-sectional area and time, respectively. Thus,

$$
\begin{gathered}
\frac{d Q}{d t}=\left(1.60 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{s}-\mathrm{m}^{2}\right)\left(0.5 \mathrm{~m}^{2}\right)(60 \mathrm{~s} / \mathrm{min})(60 \mathrm{~min} / \mathrm{h}) \\
=2.88 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{h} \quad\left(2.73 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{Btu} / \mathrm{h}\right)
\end{gathered}
$$

(c) If soda-lime glass is used $(k=1.7 \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{m}-\mathrm{K}$,  table 17.1 ),

$$
\begin{gathered}
\frac{d Q}{d t}=-k A t \frac{\Delta T}{\Delta x} \\
=-\left(1.7 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{s}-\mathrm{m}-\mathrm{K}\right)\left(0.5 \mathrm{~m}^{2}\right)(3600 \mathrm{~s} / \mathrm{h})\left(\frac{-100 \mathrm{~K}}{7.5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}}\right) \\
=4.08 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{h} \quad\left(3.9 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{Btu} / \mathrm{h}\right)
\end{gathered}
$$

(d) If the thickness of the brass is increased to 15 mm , then

$$
\begin{gathered}
\frac{d Q}{d t}=-k A t \frac{\Delta T}{\Delta x} \\
=-\left(120 \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{m}-\mathrm{K}\right)\left(0.5 \mathrm{~m}^{2}\right)(3600 \mathrm{~s} / \mathrm{h})\left(\frac{-100 \mathrm{~K}}{15 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}}\right) \\
=1.44 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{h} \quad\left(1.36 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{Btu} / \mathrm{h}\right)
\end{gathered}
$$

solution:
(a) We are asked to compute the magnitude of the stress within a brass rod that is heated while its ends are maintained rigid. To solve this part of the problem we employ equation (\sigma=E \alpha_l\left(T_0-T_f\right)=E \alpha_l \Delta T), using a value of 97 GPa for the modulus of elasticity of brass ( table 7.1 ), and a value of $20.0 \times 10^{-6}\left({ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)^{-1}$ for $\alpha{l}$ ( table 17.1 ). Therefore, we calculate the magnitude of the stress as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
\sigma=E \alpha{l}\left(T{0}-T{f}\right) \\
=\left(97 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{MPa}\right)\left[20.0 \times 10^{-6}\left({ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)^{-1}\right]\left(15^{\circ} \mathrm{C}-85^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right) \\
=-136 \mathrm{MPa} \quad(-19,600 \mathrm{psi})
\end{gathered}
$$

The stress will be compressive since its sign is negative.

(b) Upon cooling the indicated amount, the stress becomes

$$
\begin{gathered}
\sigma=E \alpha{l}\left(T{0}-T{f}\right) \\
=\left(97 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{MPa}\right)\left[20.0 \times 10^{-6}\left({ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)^{-1}\right]\left[\left(15^{\circ} \mathrm{C}-\left(-15^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)\right]\right. \\
=+58 \mathrm{MPa}(+8620 \mathrm{psi})
\end{gathered}
$$

This stress will be tensile since its sign is positive.

question: A steel wire is stretched so that it carries a tensile stress of 70 MPa at 20 °C. If the length of the wire is then held fixed and the temperature is raised, what temperature must be reached to reduce the tensile stress to 17 MPa?  Use E = 207 GPa and α = 12.0×10⁻⁶ °C⁻¹ for the steel.
solution:
We want to heat the steel wire in order to reduce the stress level from 70 MPa to 17 MPa ; in doing so, we reduce the stress in the wire by $70 \mathrm{MPa}-17 \mathrm{MPa}=53 \mathrm{MPa}$, which stress will be a compressive one (i.e., $\sigma=-53$ MPa ). Solving for $T{f}$ from equation (\sigma=E \alpha_l\left(T_0-T_f\right)=E \alpha_l \Delta T) [and using values for $E=207 \mathrm{GPa}$ and $\alpha{l}=12.0 \times 10^{-6}\left({ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)^{-1}$, respectively] yields the following:

$$
\begin{gathered}
T{f}=T{0}-\frac{\sigma}{E \alpha{l}} \\
=20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}-\frac{-53 \mathrm{MPa}}{\left(207 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{MPa}\right)\left[12.0 \times 10^{-6}\left({ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)^{-1}\right]} \\
=20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}+21.3^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=41.3^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\left(105^{\circ} \mathrm{F}\right)
\end{gathered}
$$

question: If a cylindrical rod of brass 150.00 mm long and 10.000 mm in diameter is heated from $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $160^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ while its ends are maintained rigid, determine its change in diameter. Hint: You may want to consult  table 7.1 . For brass, $v=0.34$; the value of $\alpha_l$ for brass is $20.0 \times 10^{-6}\left({ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)^{-1}$.
solution:
This problem asks for us to determine the change in diameter of a cylindrical brass rod 150.00 mm long and 10.000 mm in diameter when it is heated from $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $160^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ while its ends are maintained rigid. There will be two contributions to the diameter increase of the rod; the first is due to thermal expansion (which will be denoted as $\Delta d{1}$ ), while the second is from Poisson's lateral expansion as a result of elastic deformation from stresses that are established from the inability of the rod to elongate as it is heated (denoted as $\Delta d{2}$ ). The magnitude of $\Delta d{1}$ may be computed using a modified form of equation (\begin{aligned}
&\frac{l_f-l_0}{l_0}=\alpha_l\left(T_f-T_0\right)\\
&\frac{\Delta l}{l_0}=\alpha_l \Delta T
\end{aligned}) as follows:

$$
\Delta d{1}=d{0} \alpha{l}\left(T{f}-T{0}\right)
$$

The value of $\alpha{l}$ for brass is $20.0 \times 10^{-6}\left({ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)^{-1}$. Thus,

$$
\begin{gathered}
\Delta d{1}=(10.000 \mathrm{~mm})\left[20.0 \times 10^{-6}\left({ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)^{-1}\right]\left(160^{\circ} \mathrm{C}-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right) \\
=0.0280 \mathrm{~mm}
\end{gathered}
$$

Now, $\Delta d{2}$ is related to the transverse strain $\left(\varepsilon{x}\right)$ according to a modified form of equation (\varepsilon=\frac{l_i-l_0}{l_0}=\frac{\Delta l}{l_0}) as follows:

$$
\frac{\Delta d{2}}{d{0}}=\varepsilon{x}
$$

Also, transverse strain and longitudinal strain $\left(\varepsilon{z}\right)$ are related according to equation (\nu=-\frac{\varepsilon_x}{\varepsilon_z}=-\frac{\varepsilon_y}{\varepsilon_z})-that is,

$$
\varepsilon{x}=-v \varepsilon{z}
$$

where $v$ is Poisson's ratio. Substitution of this expression for $\varepsilon{x}$ into the first equation above leads to

$$
\frac{\Delta d{2}}{d{0}}=-v \varepsilon{z}
$$

Furthermore, the longitudinal strain is related to the modulus of elasticity through equation (\sigma=E \varepsilon)-i.e.,

$$
\varepsilon{z}=\frac{\sigma}{E}
$$

And, therefore, making these substitutions leads to the following expression:

$$
\frac{\Delta d{2}}{d{0}}=-v \frac{\sigma}{E}
$$

Now, from equation (\sigma=E \alpha_l\left(T_0-T_f\right)=E \alpha_l \Delta T) stress is equal to

$$
\sigma=E \alpha{l}\left(T{0}-T{f}\right)
$$

which, when substituted into the preceding equation leads to

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Delta d{2}}{d{0}} & =-\frac{v E \alpha{l}\left(T{0}-T{f}\right)}{E} \\
& =-v \alpha{l}\left(T{0}-T{f}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $\Delta d{2}$ and realizing that, for brass, $v=0.34$ yields

$$
\begin{gathered}
\Delta d{2}=-d{0} v \alpha{l}\left(T{0}-T{f}\right) \\
=-\left(10.000 \mathrm{~mm}\right)(0.34)\left[20.0 \times 10^{-6}\left({ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)^{-1}\right]\left(20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}-160^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right) \\
=0.0095 \mathrm{~mm}
\end{gathered}
$$

Finally, the total $\Delta d$ is just $\Delta d{1}+\Delta d{2}=0.0280 \mathrm{~mm}+0.0095 \mathrm{~mm}=0.0375 \mathrm{~mm}$.

question: The two ends of a cylindrical rod of nickel 120.00 mm long and 12.000 mm in diameter are maintained rigid. If the rod is initially at $70^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, to what temperature must it be cooled in order to have a $0.023-\mathrm{mm}$ reduction in diameter? The values of $v$ and $\alpha{l}$ for nickel are 0.31 and $13.3 \times 10^{-6}\left({ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)^{-1}$, respectively.
solution:
This problem asks for us to determine to what temperature a cylindrical rod of nickel 120.00 mm long and 12.000 mm in diameter must be cooled from $70^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in order to have a $0.023-\mathrm{mm}$ reduction in diameter if the rod ends are maintained rigid. There will be two contributions to the diameter decrease of the rod; the first is due to thermal contraction (which will be denoted as $\Delta d{1}$ ), while the second is from Poisson's lateral contraction as a result of elastic deformation from stresses that are established from the inability of the rod to contract as it is cooled (denoted as $\Delta d{2}$ ). The magnitude of $\Delta d{1}$ may be computed using a modified form of equation (\frac{\Delta l}{l_0}=\alpha_l \Delta T) as follows:

$$
\Delta d{1}=d{0} \alpha{l}\left(T{f}-T{0}\right)
$$

Now, $\Delta d{2}$ is related to the transverse strain $\left(\varepsilon{x}\right)$ according to a modified form of equation (\varepsilon=\frac{l_i-l_0}{l_0}=\frac{\Delta l}{l_0}) as

$$
\frac{\Delta d{2}}{d{0}}=\varepsilon{x}
$$

Also, transverse strain and longitudinal strain $\left(\varepsilon{z}\right)$ are related according to equation (\nu=-\frac{\varepsilon_x}{\varepsilon_z}=-\frac{\varepsilon_y}{\varepsilon_z})-that is

$$
\varepsilon{x}=-v \varepsilon{z}
$$

where $v$ is Poisson's ratio. Substitution of this expression for $\varepsilon{x}$ into the first equation above leads to

$$
\frac{\Delta d{2}}{d{0}}=-v \varepsilon{z}
$$

Furthermore, the longitudinal strain is related to the modulus of elasticity through equation (\sigma=E \varepsilon)-i.e.,

$$
\varepsilon{z}=\frac{\sigma}{E}
$$

And, therefore, we may write the following:

$$
\frac{\Delta d{2}}{d{0}}=-v \frac{\sigma}{E}
$$

Now, from equation (\sigma=E \alpha_l\left(T_0-T_f\right)=E \alpha_l \Delta T) stress is equal to

$$
\sigma=E \alpha{l}\left(T{0}-T{f}\right)
$$

which, when substituted into the preceding equation, leads to

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Delta d{2}}{d{0}} & =-\frac{v E \alpha{l}\left(T{0}-T{f}\right)}{E} \\
& =-v \alpha{l}\left(T{0}-T{f}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

And, solving for $\Delta d{2}$ from this expression

$$
\Delta d{2}=-d{0} v \alpha{l}\left(T{0}-T{f}\right)
$$

Now, the total $\Delta d$ is just $\Delta d=\Delta d{1}+\Delta d{2}$, as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
\Delta d=d{0} \alpha{l}\left(T{f}-T{0}\right)+d{0} v \alpha{l}\left(T{f}-T{0}\right) \\
=d{0} \alpha{l}\left(T{f}-T{0}\right)(1+v)
\end{gathered}
$$

The values of $v$ and $\alpha{l}$ for nickel are 0.31 and $13.3 \times 10^{-6}\left({ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)^{-1}$, respectively. Incorporating, into the above equation, these values, as well as those for $\Delta d, d{0}$, and $T{0}$ cited in the problem statement gives

$$
-(0.023 \mathrm{~mm})=(12.000 \mathrm{~mm})\left[13.3 \times 10^{-6}\left({ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)^{-1}\right]\left(T{f}-70^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)(1+0.31)
$$

And, finally, solving the above expression for $T{f}$ yields $T{f}=-40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

question:
A coil of wire 0.25 m long and having 400 turns carries a current of 15 A. χm = 3.13×10^–4.
(a) What is the magnitude of the magnetic field strength $H$ ?
(b) Compute the flux density $B$ if the coil is in a vacuum.
(c) Compute the flux density inside a bar of chromium that is positioned within the coil. The susceptibility for chromium is given in  table 18.2 .
(d) Compute the magnitude of the magnetization $M$.

Give your answer as a tuple (answer to (a), answer to (b), answer to (c), answer to (d)).

Reference:
"Table 18.2
Room-Temperature Magnetic Susceptibilities for Diamagnetic and Paramagnetic Materials
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline \multicolumn{2}{|c|}{Diamagnetics} & \multicolumn{2}{|c|}{Paramagnetics} \\
\hline Material & Susceptibility $\chi_m$ (volume) (SI units) & Material & Susceptibility $\chi_m$ (volume) (SI units) \\
\hline Aluminum oxide & $-1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ & Aluminum & $2.07 \times 10^{-5}$ \\
\hline Copper & $-0.96 \times 10^{-5}$ & Chromium & $3.13 \times 10^{-4}$ \\
\hline Gold & $-3.44 \times 10^{-5}$ & Chromium chloride & $1.51 \times 10^{-3}$ \\
\hline Mercury & $-2.85 \times 10^{-5}$ & Manganese sulfate & $3.70 \times 10^{-3}$ \\
\hline Silicon & $-0.41 \times 10^{-5}$ & Molybdenum & $1.19 \times 10^{-4}$ \\
\hline Silver & $-2.38 \times 10^{-5}$ & Sodium & $8.48 \times 10^{-6}$ \\
\hline Sodium chloride & $-1.41 \times 10^{-5}$ & Titanium & $1.81 \times 10^{-4}$ \\
\hline Zinc & $-1.56 \times 10^{-5}$ & Zirconium & $1.09 \times 10^{-4}$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}"

It is possible to express the magnetic susceptibility $\chi{m}$ in several different units. For the discussion of this chapter, $\chi{m}$ was used to designate the volume susceptibility in SI units-that is, the quantity that gives the magnetization per unit volume $\left(m^{3}\right)$ of material when multiplied by $H$. The mass susceptibility $\chi{m}(\mathrm{~kg})$ yields the magnetic moment (or magnetization) per kilogram of material when multiplied by $H$; similarly, the atomic susceptibility $\chi{m}(a)$ gives the magnetization per kilogram-mole. The last two quantities are related to $\chi{m}$ through the following relationships:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \chi{m}=\chi{m}(k g) \times \text { mass density }\left(\text { in } \mathrm{kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}\right) \\
& \chi{m}(a)=\chi{m}(k g) \times \text { atomic weight (in } \mathrm{kg})
\end{aligned}
$$

When using the cgs-emu system, comparable parameters exist, which may be designated by $\chi{m}^{\prime}, \chi{m}^{\prime}(g)$, and $\chi{m}^{\prime}(a)$; the $\chi{m}$ and $\chi{m}^{\prime}$ are related in accordance with  table 18.1 . From  table 18.2 , $\chi{m}$ for copper is $-0.96 \times 10^{-5}$; convert this value into the other five susceptibilities. Give your answer as a tuple: ($\chi_m(\mathrm{~kg})$, $\chi_m(a), \chi_m^{\prime}, \chi_m^{\prime}(\mathrm{g})$, $\chi_m^{\prime}(\mathrm{a})$)

Reference:
"Table 18.1 Magnetic Units and Conversion Factors for the SI and cgs-emu Systems
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline \multirow[b]{2}{*}{Quantity} & \multirow[b]{2}{*}{Symbol} & \multicolumn{2}{|c|}{SI Units} & \multirow[b]{2}{*}{cgs-emu Unit} & \multirow[b]{2}{*}{Conversion} \\
\hline & & Derived & Primary & & \\
\hline Magnetic induction (flux density) & B & Tesla ( $\left.\mathrm{Wb} / \mathrm{m}^2\right)^a$ & kg/s•C & Gauss & $1 \mathrm{~Wb} / \mathrm{m}^2=10^4$ gauss \\
\hline Magnetic field strength & H & Amp-turn/m & $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m} \cdot \mathrm{s}$ & Oersted & 1 amp-turn/m = $4 \pi \times 10^{-3}$ oersted \\
\hline Magnetization & M (SI) $I$ (cgs-emu) & Amp-turn/m & $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m} \cdot \mathrm{s}$ & Maxwell/cm ${ }^2$ & 1 amp -turn $/ \mathrm{m}=$ $10^{-3}$ maxwell $/ \mathrm{cm}^2$ \\
\hline Permeability of a vacuum & $\mu_0$ & Henry/m ${ }^b$ & $\mathrm{kg} \cdot \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{C}^2$ & Unitless (emu) & $4 \pi \times 10^{-7}$ henry $/ \mathrm{m}=$ 1 emu \\
\hline Relative permeability & $\mu_r$ (SI) $\mu^{\prime}$ (cgs-emu) & Unitless & Unitless & Unitless & $\mu_r=\mu^{\prime}$ \\
\hline Susceptibility & $\chi_m$ (SI) $\chi_m^{\prime}$ (cgs-emu) & Unitless & Unitless & Unitless & $\chi_m=4 \pi \chi_m^{\prime}$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
${ }^a$ Units of the weber (Wb) are volt-seconds.
${ }^b$ Units of the henry are webers per ampere."


"Table 18.2
Room-Temperature Magnetic Susceptibilities for Diamagnetic and Paramagnetic Materials
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline \multicolumn{2}{|c|}{Diamagnetics} & \multicolumn{2}{|c|}{Paramagnetics} \\
\hline Material & Susceptibility $\chi_m$ (volume) (SI units) & Material & Susceptibility $\chi_m$ (volume) (SI units) \\
\hline Aluminum oxide & $-1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ & Aluminum & $2.07 \times 10^{-5}$ \\
\hline Copper & $-0.96 \times 10^{-5}$ & Chromium & $3.13 \times 10^{-4}$ \\
\hline Gold & $-3.44 \times 10^{-5}$ & Chromium chloride & $1.51 \times 10^{-3}$ \\
\hline Mercury & $-2.85 \times 10^{-5}$ & Manganese sulfate & $3.70 \times 10^{-3}$ \\
\hline Silicon & $-0.41 \times 10^{-5}$ & Molybdenum & $1.19 \times 10^{-4}$ \\
\hline Silver & $-2.38 \times 10^{-5}$ & Sodium & $8.48 \times 10^{-6}$ \\
\hline Sodium chloride & $-1.41 \times 10^{-5}$ & Titanium & $1.81 \times 10^{-4}$ \\
\hline Zinc & $-1.56 \times 10^{-5}$ & Zirconium & $1.09 \times 10^{-4}$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}"
solution:
For this problem, we want to convert the volume susceptibility of copper (i.e., $-0.96 \times 10^{-5}$ ) into other systems of units.

From the expression for the mass susceptibility given in the problem statement we may write

$$
\chi{m}(\mathrm{~kg})=\frac{\chi{m}}{\rho\left(\mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}\right)}
$$

The density of copper is $8.94 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}$, we convert into units of $\mathrm{kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$ as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
\rho=\left(8.94 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}\right)\left(\frac{1 \mathrm{~kg}}{1000 \mathrm{~g}}\right)\left(\frac{100 \mathrm{~cm}}{\mathrm{~m}}\right)^{3} \\
=8.94 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}
\end{gathered}
$$

We now compute the mass density susceptibility as follows:

$$
\chi{m}(\mathrm{~kg})=\frac{\chi{m}}{\rho\left(\mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}\right)}
$$

$$
=\frac{-0.96 \times 10^{-5}}{8.94 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}}=-1.07 \times 10^{-9}
$$

The atomic susceptibility is defined above as follows:

$$
\chi{m}(\mathrm{a})=\chi{m}(\mathrm{~kg}) \times[\text { atomic weight }(\text { in } \mathrm{kg})]
$$

The atomic weight of copper (in $\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{mol}$ ) is $63.55 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$, which converts into $\mathrm{kg} / \mathrm{mol}$ as follows:

$$
\text { atomic weight }(\text { in } \mathrm{kg})=(63.55 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol})\left(\frac{1 \mathrm{~kg}}{1000 \mathrm{~g}}\right)=6.355 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{mol}
$$

Therefore we compute the atomic susceptibility

$$
\begin{gathered}
\chi{m}(\mathrm{a})=\chi{m}(\mathrm{~kg}) \times[\text { atomic weight }(\text { in } \mathrm{kg})] \\
=\left(-1.07 \times 10^{-9}\right)\left(6.355 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{mol}\right)=-6.80 \times 10^{-11}
\end{gathered}
$$

From  table 18.1  the cgs-emu volume susceptibility is equal to the following:

$$
\chi{m}^{\prime}=\frac{\chi{m}}{4 \pi}
$$

Here $\chi{m}$ is the SI volume susceptibility, which was provided in the problem statement. Therefore,

$$
\begin{gathered}
\chi{m}^{\prime}=\frac{\chi{m}}{4 \pi} \\
=\frac{-0.96 \times 10^{-5}}{4 \pi}=-7.64 \times 10^{-7}
\end{gathered}
$$

The next susceptibility we want to determine is $\chi{m}^{\prime}(\mathrm{g})$, which in, cgs-emu units, is equal to

$$
\chi{m}^{\prime}(\mathrm{g})=\frac{\chi{m}^{\prime}}{\text { mass density }\left(\text { in } \mathrm{g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}\right)}
$$

Therefore,

$$
\begin{gathered}
\chi{m}^{\prime}(\mathrm{g})=\frac{-7.64 \times 10^{-7}}{8.94 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}} \\
=-8.55 \times 10^{-8}
\end{gathered}
$$

And finally, the cgs-emu atomic susceptibility $\chi{m}^{\prime}(\mathrm{a})$ is defined as follows:

$$
\chi{m}^{\prime}(\mathrm{a})=\chi{m}^{\prime}(\mathrm{g}) \times[\text { atomic weight }(\text { in } \mathrm{g})]
$$

which for copper has a value of

$$
\chi{m}^{\prime}(\mathrm{a})=\left(-8.55 \times 10^{-8}\right)(63.55 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol})=-5.43 \times 10^{-6}
$$

question:
The magnetic flux density within a bar of some material is 0.630 tesla at an $H$ field of $5 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}$. Compute the following for this material: (a) the magnetic permeability and (b) the magnetic susceptibility.

Give your answer as a tuple (the magnetic permeability, the magnetic susceptibility).

The magnetization within a bar of some metal alloy is $1.2 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}$ at an H field of $200 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}$. Compute the following: (a) the magnetic susceptibility, (b) the permeability, and (c) the magnetic flux density within this material. 

Give your answer as a tuple (the magnetic susceptibility, the permeability, the magnetic flux density).
solution:
(a) This portion of the problem calls for us to compute the magnetic susceptibility within a bar of some metal alloy when $M=1.2 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}$ and $H=200 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}$. This requires that we solve for $\chi{m}$ from equation (M=\chi_m H) as follows:

$$
\chi{m}=\frac{M}{H}=\frac{1.2 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}}{200 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}}=6000
$$

(b) In order to calculate the permeability we must employ a combined form of equation ((\mu_r=\frac{\mu}{\mu_0}) and (\chi_m=\mu_r-1)) as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
\mu=\mu{r} \mu{0} \\
=\left(\chi{m}+1\right) \mu{0} \\
=(6000+1)\left(1.257 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{H} / \mathrm{m}\right)=7.54 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{H} / \mathrm{m}
\end{gathered}
$$

(c) The magnetic flux density may be determined using equation (B=\mu H) as

$$
B=\mu H=\left(7.54 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{H} / \mathrm{m}\right)(200 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m})=1.51 \text { tesla }
$$

question: Confirm that there are 1.72 Bohr magnetons associated with each cobalt atom, given that the saturation magnetization is $1.45 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}$, that cobalt has an HCP crystal structure with an atomic radius of 0.1253 nm and a c/a ratio of 1.623 .

Assume there exists some hypothetical metal that exhibits ferromagnetic behavior and that has (1) a simple cubic crystal structure ( figure 3.3 ), (2) an atomic radius of 0.125 nm , and (3) a saturation flux density of 0.85 tesla. Determine the number of Bohr magnetons per atom for this material.

The chemical formula for copper ferrite may be written as $\left(\mathrm{CuFe}{2} \mathrm{O}{4}\right){8}$ because there are eight formula units per unit cell. If this material has a saturation magnetization of $1.35 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}$ and a density of $5.40 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}$. estimate the number of Bohr magnetons associated with each $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ ion.
solution:
We want to compute the number of Bohr magnetons per $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ ion in $\left(\mathrm{CuFe}{2} \mathrm{O}{4}\right){8}$. Let $n{\mathrm{B}}$ represent the number of Bohr magnetons per $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ ion; then, using equation (M_s=0.60 \mu_{\mathrm{B}} N), we write

$$
M{s}=n{\mathrm{B}} \mu{\mathrm{B}} N
$$

in which $N$ is the number of $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ ions per cubic meter of material. But, from equation (N=\frac{\rho N_{\mathrm{A}}}{A_{\mathrm{Ni}}})

$$
N=\frac{\rho N{\mathrm{A}}}{A}
$$

Here $A$ is the molecular weight of $\mathrm{CuFe}{2} \mathrm{O}{4}(239.25 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol})$. Thus, combining the previous two equations leads to the following:

$$
M{s}=\frac{n{\mathrm{B}} \mu{\mathrm{B}} \rho N{\mathrm{A}}}{A}
$$

or, upon rearrangement (and expressing the density in units of grams per meter cubed), the number of Bohr magnetons per $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ ion $\left(n{\mathrm{B}}\right)$ is computed as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
n{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{M{s} A}{\mu{\mathrm{B}} \rho N{\mathrm{A}}} \\
=\frac{\left(1.35 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}\right)(239.25 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol})}{\left(9.27 \times 10^{-24} \mathrm{~A}-\mathrm{m}^{2} / \mathrm{BM}\right)\left(5.40 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{m}^{3}\right)\left(6.022 \times 10^{23} \mathrm{ions} / \mathrm{mol}\right)} \\
=1.07 \text { Bohr magnetons } / \mathrm{Cu}^{2+} \text { ion }
\end{gathered}
$$

question: “Samarium iron garnet has the formula Sm₃Fe₅O₁₂ which can be written Sm₃ᶜ Fe₂ᵃ Fe₃ᵈ O₁₂, where the subscripts ᵃ, ᶜ, and ᵈ denote the crystallographic a, c, and d sites.  The 2 Fe³⁺ ions on the a‐sites have their spins aligned parallel to those of the 3 Sm³⁺ ions on the c‐sites, and these are antiparallel to the 3 Fe³⁺ ions on the d‐sites.  Given that (1) each cubic unit cell (edge 1.2529 nm) contains eight Sm₃Fe₅O₁₂ formula units, (2) the saturation magnetization Mₛ=1.35×10⁵ A/m, and (3) each Fe³⁺ carries 5 μ_B, compute the number of Bohr magnetons μ_Sm carried by each Sm³⁺ ion.”
solution:
In this problem we are to determine the number of Bohr magnetons associated with each $\mathrm{Sm}^{3+}$ ion given that each unit cell consists of eight formula units, the unit cell is cubic with an edge length of 1.2529 nm , the saturation magnetization for the material is $1.35 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}$, and that there are 5 Bohr magnetons for each $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ ion.

The first thing to do is to calculate the number of Bohr magnetons per unit cell, which we will denote $n{\mathrm{B}}$. Solving for $n{\mathrm{B}}$ in equation (M_s=\frac{n_{\mathrm{B}} \mu_{\mathrm{B}}}{a^3}), we get

$$
\begin{gathered}
n{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{M{s} a^{3}}{\mu{\mathrm{B}}} \\
=\frac{\left(1.35 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}\right)\left(1.2529 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}\right)^{3}}{9.27 \times 10^{-24} \mathrm{~A}-\mathrm{m}^{2} / \mathrm{BM}} \\
=28.64 \text { Bohr \frac{magnetons}{unit} cell }
\end{gathered}
$$

Now, there are 8 formula units per unit cell or $\frac{28.64}{8}=3.58$ Bohr magnetons per formula unit. Furthermore, for each formula unit there are two $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ ions on $a$ sites and three $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ on $d$ sites which magnetic moments are aligned antiparallel. Since there are 5 Bohr magnetons associated with each $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ ion, the net magnetic moment contribution per formula unit from the $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ ions is 5 Bohr magnetons. This contribution is antiparallel to the contribution from the $\mathrm{Sm}^{3+}$ ions, and since there are three $\mathrm{Sm}^{3+}$ ions per formula unit, then

No. of Bohr magnetons $/ \mathrm{Sm}^{3+}=\frac{3.58 \mathrm{BM}+5 \mathrm{BM}}{3}=2.86 \mathrm{BM}$

An iron bar magnet having a coercivity of $7000 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}$ is to be demagnetized. If the bar is inserted within a cylindrical wire coil 0.25 m long and having 150 turns, what electric current is required to generate the necessary magnetic field?

A bar of an iron-silicon alloy having the $B-H$ behavior shown in  figure 18.29  is inserted within a coil of wire 0.40 m long and having 50 turns through which passes a current of 0.1 A .
(a) What is the B field within this bar?
(b) At this magnetic field,
(i) What is the permeability?
(ii) What is the relative permeability?
(iii) What is the susceptibility?
(iv) What is the magnetization?

Give your answer as a tuple (B, the permeability,  the relative permeability, the susceptibility, the magnetization).
solution:
(a) We want to determine the magnitude of the $B$ field within an iron-silicon alloy, the $B-H$ behavior for which is shown in  figure 18.29 , when $l=0.40 \mathrm{~m}, N=50$ turns, and $I=0.1 \mathrm{~A}$. Applying equation (H=\frac{N I}{l}) we calculate the value of $H$ as follows:

$$
H=\frac{N I}{l}=\frac{(50 \text { turns })(0.1 \mathrm{~A})}{0.40 \mathrm{~m}}=12.5 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}
$$

Below is shown the $B$-versus- $H$ plot for this material. The $B$ value from the curve corresponding to $H=12.5 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}$ is about 1.07 tesla.


(b)
(i) The permeability at this field is just $\Delta B / \Delta H$ of the tangent of the $B-H$ curve at $H=12.5 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}$. The slope of this line as drawn in the above figure is

$$
\mu=\frac{\Delta B}{\Delta H}=\frac{(1.50-0.66) \text { tesla }}{(25-0) \mathrm{A} / \mathrm{m}}=3.36 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{H} / \mathrm{m}
$$

(ii) From equation (\mu_r=\frac{\mu}{\mu_0}), the relative permeability is computed a follows:

$$
\mu{r}=\frac{\mu}{\mu{0}}=\frac{3.36 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{H} / \mathrm{m}}{1.257 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{H} / \mathrm{m}}=26,730
$$

(iii) Using equation (\chi_m=\mu_r-1), the susceptibility is

$$
\begin{gathered}
\chi{m}=\mu{r}-1 \\
$=26,730-1=26,729$
\end{gathered}
$$

(iv) The magnetization is determined from equation (M=\chi_m H) as follows:

$$
M=\chi{m} H=(26,729)(12.5 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m})=3.34 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}
$$

Neglect any orientation polarization effects. Give your answer as a tuple: (fraction for fused silica, fraction for polystyrene).

\end{tabular}
${ }^a$ One mil $=0.001 \mathrm{in}$. These values of dielectric strength are average ones, the magnitude being dependent on specimen thickness and geometry, as well as the rate of application and duration of the applied electric field."
solution:
The frequencies of visible radiation are on the order of $10^{15} \mathrm{~Hz}$. At these frequencies only electronic polarization is operable. Thus, $\varepsilon{r}$ from equation (n \cong \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}) is the electronic contribution to $\varepsilon{r}$; let us designate it as $\varepsilon{r}^{\prime}$. In other words

$$
\varepsilon{r}^{\prime}=n^{2}
$$

For fused silica, $\varepsilon{r}^{\prime}$ is equal to

$$
\varepsilon{r}^{\prime}(\text { silica })=(1.458)^{2}=2.13
$$

And, for polystyrene (PS)

$$
\varepsilon{r}^{\prime}(\mathrm{PS})=(1.60)^{2}=2.56
$$

The fraction of the electronic contribution is just the ratio of $\varepsilon{r}^{\prime}$ and $\varepsilon{r}$, where $\varepsilon{r}$ values are taken from  table 12.5  (4.0 and 2.6 for fused silica and polystyrene, respectively, at a frequency of 60 Hz ). Thus, for fused silica

$$
\frac{\varepsilon{r}^{\prime}(\text { silica })}{\varepsilon{r}(60 \mathrm{~Hz})}=\frac{2.13}{4.0}=0.53
$$

and for polystyrene we have

$$
\frac{\varepsilon{r}^{\prime}(\mathrm{PS})}{\varepsilon{r}(60 \mathrm{~Hz})}=\frac{2.56}{2.60}=0.98
$$

question: The index of refraction of quartz is anisotropic. Suppose that visible light is passing from one grain to another of different crystallographic orientation and at normal incidence to the grain boundary. Calculate the reflectivity at the boundary if the indices of refraction for the two grains are 1.544 and 1.553 in the direction of light propagation.

Zinc selenide has a band gap of 2.58 eV . Over what range of wavelengths of visible light is it transparent?

Give your answer as a tuple (the lower bound, the upper bound).

The fraction of nonreflected radiation that is transmitted through a 5-mm thickness of a transparent material is 0.95 . If the thickness is increased to 12 mm , what fraction of light will be transmitted?
solution:
In this problem we are asked to calculate the fraction of nonreflected light transmitted through a $12-\mathrm{mm}$ thickness of transparent material, given that the fraction transmitted through a $5-\mathrm{mm}$ thickness is 0.95 . From equation (I_T^{\prime}=I_0^{\prime} e^{-\beta x}), the fraction of nonreflected light transmitted is just $\frac{I{T}^{\prime}}{I{0}^{\prime}}$. Thus, we may rewrite equation (I_T^{\prime}=I_0^{\prime} e^{-\beta x}) as follows:

$$
\frac{I{T}^{\prime}}{I{0}^{\prime}}=e^{-\beta x}
$$

Using this expression and the first set of data in the problem statement, we must first determine the value of $\beta$. This is possible by algebraic manipulation of equation (\frac{I_T^{\prime}}{I_0^{\prime}}=e^{-\beta x}). Taking natural logarithms of both sides of equation (\frac{I_T^{\prime}}{I_0^{\prime}}=e^{-\beta x}) leads to the following expression:

$$
\ln \left(\frac{I{T}^{\prime}}{I{0}^{\prime}}\right)=-\beta x
$$

Now solving for $\beta$ in this expression gives the following:

$$
\beta=-\frac{1}{x} \ln \left(\frac{I{T}^{\prime}}{I{0}^{\prime}}\right)
$$

The value of $\beta$ is determined using the following data given in the problem statement:

$$
\begin{aligned}
