(a) We can calculate the size of the interstitial site in BCC iron at the $(0,1 / 2,1 / 4)$ location with the help of Figure 4-2(a). The radius $R_{\mathrm{BCC}}$ of the iron atom is

$$
R_{\mathrm{BCC}}=\frac{\sqrt{3} a_0}{4}=\frac{(\sqrt{3})(0.2866)}{4}=0.1241 \mathrm{~nm}
$$


From Figure 4-2(a), we find that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{1}{2} a_0\right)^2+\left(\frac{1}{4} a_0\right)^2 & =\left(r_{\text {interstitial }}+R_{\mathrm{BCC}}\right)^2 \\
\left(r_{\text {interstitial }}+R_{\mathrm{BCC}}\right)^2 & =0.3125 a_0^2=(0.3125)(0.2866 \mathrm{~nm})^2=0.02567 \\
r_{\text {interstitial }} & =\sqrt{0.02567}-0.1241=0.0361 \mathrm{~nm}
\end{aligned}
$$


For FCC iron, the interstitial site such as the $(0,0,1 / 2)$ lies along $\langle 001\rangle$ directions. Thus, the radius of the iron atom and the radius of the interstitial site are [Figure 4-2(b)]:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\mathrm{FCC}} & =\frac{\sqrt{2} a_0}{4}=\frac{(\sqrt{2})(0.3571)}{4}=0.1263 \mathrm{~nm} \\
2 r_{\text {interstitial }}+2 R_{\mathrm{FCC}} & =a_0 \\
r_{\text {interstitial }} & =\frac{0.3571-(2)(0.1263)}{2}=0.0523 \mathrm{~nm}
\end{aligned}
$$


The interstitial site in BCC iron is smaller than the interstitial site in FCC iron. Although both are smaller than the carbon atom, carbon distorts the BCC crystal structure more than the FCC structure. As a result, fewer carbon atoms are expected to enter interstitial positions in BCC iron than in FCC iron.
(b) In BCC iron, two iron atoms are expected in each unit cell. We can find a total of 24 interstitial sites of the type $(1 / 4,1 / 2,0)$; however, since each site is located at a face of the unit cell, only half of each site belongs uniquely to a single cell. Thus, there are

$$
(24 \text { sites })\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=12 \text { interstitial sites per unit cell }
$$


If all of the interstitial sites were filled, the atomic percentage of carbon contained in the iron would be

$$
\text { at } \% \mathrm{C}=\frac{12 \mathrm{C} \text { atoms }}{12 \mathrm{C} \text { atoms }+2 \mathrm{Fe} \text { atoms }} \times 100=86 \%
$$


In FCC iron, four iron atoms are expected in each unit cell, and the number of octahedral interstitial sites is

$$
\left.(12 \text { edges })\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)+1 \text { center }=4 \text { interstitial sites per unit cell [Figure } 4-2(\mathrm{c})\right]
$$


Again, if all the octahedral interstitial sites were filled, the atomic percentage of carbon in the FCC iron would be

$$
\text { at } \% \mathrm{C}=\frac{4 \mathrm{C} \text { atoms }}{4 \mathrm{C} \text { atoms }+4 \mathrm{Fe} \text { atoms }} \times 100=50 \%
$$

question: Write the appropriate defect reactions for (a) incorporation of magnesium oxide (MgO) in nickel oxide $(\mathrm{NiO})$ and (b) formation of a Schottky defect in alumina $(\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3})$ using the Kröger-Vink notation. Ensure that the reactions maintain charge, mass, and site balance. Give your answer as a tuple (defect_reaction_a, defect_reaction_b).
solution:
part_a: MgO is the guest and NiO is the host material. We assume that $\mathrm{Mg}^{+2}$ ions will occupy $\mathrm{Ni}^{+2}$ sites and oxygen anions from MgO will occupy $\mathrm{O}^{-2}$ sites of NiO. The defect reaction is:

$$
\mathrm{MgO} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{NiO}} \mathrm{Mg}_{\mathrm{Ni}}^{2}+\mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{O}}^{x}
$$

This equation ensures charge, mass, and site balance. On both sides, we have one Mg, one oxygen, and no net charge. The site balance is maintained as one $\mathrm{Mg}^{+2}$ occupies one $\mathrm{Ni}^{+2}$ site, and one $\mathrm{O}^{-2}$ site is used for the oxygen from MgO.
part_b: A Schottky defect in alumina involves two aluminum ions and three oxygen ions. The vacancies are represented as $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{Al}}^{m}$ for aluminum and $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{O}}^{\prime \prime}$ for oxygen. The defect reaction is:

$$
\text{null} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}} 2 \mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{Al}}^{m}+3 \mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{O}}^{\prime}
$$

This maintains site balance (two aluminum sites and three oxygen sites used) and mass balance (zero mass on the right-hand side).

question: Write the appropriate defect reactions for the incorporation of calcium oxide $(\mathrm{CaO})$ in zirconia $(\mathrm{ZrO}_{2})$ using the Kröger-Vink notation.
solution:
We assume that $\mathrm{Ca}^{+2}$ will occupy $\mathrm{Zr}^{+4}$ sites. Incorporating one $\mathrm{Ca}^{+2}$ into a $\mathrm{Zr}^{+4}$ site results in an effective charge of -2 at the site. This process uses one $\mathrm{Zr}^{+4}$ site and, for site balance, requires two oxygen sites. One $\mathrm{O}^{-2}$ from $\mathrm{CaO}$ occupies one of the $\mathrm{O}^{-2}$ sites in $\mathrm{ZrO}_{2}$, and the other oxygen site remains vacant to maintain mass balance, creating an oxygen ion vacancy. The defect reaction is represented as:

$$
\mathrm{CaO} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{ZrO}_{2}} \mathrm{Ca}_{\mathrm{Zr}}^{\prime\prime} + \mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{O}}^{x} + \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{O}}^{\cdot\cdot}
$$

The concentration of oxygen vacancies in $\mathrm{ZrO}_{2}$ (i.e., $[\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{O}}^{\cdot\cdot}]$) increases with increasing $\mathrm{CaO}$ concentration. These oxygen ion vacancies enable $\mathrm{CaO}$-stabilized $\mathrm{ZrO}_{2}$ to function as an ionic conductor, suitable for use in oxygen sensors and solid oxide fuel cells.

question: Calculate the length of the Burgers vector in copper. The lattice parameter of copper $(\mathrm{Cu})$ is 0.36151 nm . The close-packed directions.
solution:
Copper has an FCC crystal structure. The lattice parameter of copper $(\mathrm{Cu})$ is 0.36151 nm . The close-packed directions, or the directions of the Burgers vector, are of the form $\langle 110\rangle$. The repeat distance along the $\langle 110\rangle$ directions is one-half the face diagonal, since lattice points are located at corners and centers of faces.

$$
\text { Face diagonal }=\sqrt{2} a_0=(\sqrt{2})(0.36151)=0.51125 \mathrm{~nm}
$$


The length of the Burgers vector, or the repeat distance, is

$$
\mathbf{b}=\frac{1}{2}(0.51125) \mathrm{nm}=0.25563 \mathrm{~nm}
$$

question: A sketch of a dislocation in magnesium oxide $(\mathrm{MgO})$, which has the sodium chloride crystal structure and a lattice parameter of 0.396 nm , is shown in Figure. Determine the length of the Burgers vector.
solution:
In Figure, a clockwise loop around the dislocation begins at point $x$ and moves equal atom spacings in the two horizontal directions and equal atom spacings in the two vertical directions to finish at point $y$. The vector $\mathbf{b}$ is the Burgers vector. Because $\mathbf{b}$ is parallel to a [110] direction, it must be perpendicular to (110) planes. The length of $\mathbf{b}$ is the distance between two adjacent (110) planes. From Equation($d_{h k l}=\frac{a_0}{\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}}$):

$$
d_{110}=\frac{a_{0}}{\sqrt{h^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2}}}=\frac{0.396}{\sqrt{1^{2}+1^{2}+0^{2}}}=0.280 \mathrm{~nm}
$$

The Burgers vector is a (110) direction that is 0.280 nm in length. Note that two extra half planes of atoms make up the dislocation—one composed of oxygen ions and one of magnesium ions (Figure). This formula for calculating the magnitude of the Burgers vector will not work for non-cubic systems. It is better to consider the magnitude of the Burgers vector as equal to the repeat distance in the slip direction.

question: The planar density of the (112) plane in BCC iron is $9.94 \times 10^{14}$ atoms $/ \mathrm{cm}^{2}$. Calculate (a) the planar density of the (110) plane and (b) the interplanar spacings for both the (112) and (110) planes. The lattice parameter of BCC iron is 0.2866 nm or $2.866 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~cm}$. Give your answer as a tuple (planar density of (110) plane, interplanar spacing of (112) plane, interplanar spacing of (110) plane).

We wish to produce a rod composed of a single crystal of pure aluminum, which has a critical resolved shear stress of 148 psi. We would like to orient the rod in such a manner that, when an axial stress of 500 psi is applied, the rod deforms by slip in a $45^{\circ}$ direction to the axis of the rod and actuates a sensor that detects the overload. Design the rod and a method by which it might be produced. Give your answer as a tuple: (λ_deg, φ_deg).

The yield strength of mild steel with an average grain size of 0.05 mm is $20,000 \mathrm{psi}$. The yield stress of the same steel with a grain size of 0.007 mm is $40,000 \mathrm{psi}$. What will be the average grain size of the same steel with a yield stress of $30,000 \mathrm{psi}$? Assume the Hall-Petch equation is valid and that changes in the observed yield stress are due to changes in grain size.
solution:
Using the Hall-Petch equation:

$$\sigma_{y}=\sigma_{0}+K d^{-1 / 2}$$

For a grain size of 0.05 mm, the yield stress is:

$$20,000 \mathrm{psi} \times (6.895 \mathrm{MPa}) / (1000 \mathrm{psi}) = 137.9 \mathrm{MPa}$$

Substituting into the Hall-Petch equation:

$$137.9 = \sigma_{0} + \frac{K}{\sqrt{0.05}}$$

For a grain size of 0.007 mm, the yield stress is:

$$40,000 \mathrm{psi} \times (6.895 \mathrm{MPa}) / (1000 \mathrm{psi}) = 275.8 \mathrm{MPa}$$

Substituting into the Hall-Petch equation:

$$275.8 = \sigma_{0} + \frac{K}{\sqrt{0.007}}$$

Solving these two equations gives:

$$K = 18.44 \mathrm{MPa} \cdot \mathrm{mm}^{1 / 2}, \sigma_{0} = 55.5 \mathrm{MPa}$$

Now, the Hall-Petch equation is:

$$\sigma_{y} = 55.5 + 18.44 d^{-1 / 2}$$

For a yield stress of $30,000 \mathrm{psi}$ or 206.9 MPa, solving for $d$ gives:

$$206.9 = 55.5 + 18.44 d^{-1 / 2}$$

$$151.4 = 18.44 d^{-1 / 2}$$

$$d^{-1 / 2} = 8.21$$

$$d = (8.21)^{-2} = 0.0148 \mathrm{mm}$$

Thus, the average grain size should be 0.0148 mm.

question: Suppose we count 16 grains per square inch in a photomicrograph taken at a magnification of 250. What is the ASTM grain size number?
solution:
Consider one square inch from the photomicrograph taken at a magnification of 250. At a magnification of 100, the one square inch region from the 250 magnification image would appear as 

$$1 \mathrm{in}^{2}\left(\frac{100}{250}\right)^{2}=0.16 \mathrm{in}^{2}$$

and we would see 

$$\frac{16 \text { grains }}{0.16 \mathrm{in}^{2}}=100 \text { grains } / \mathrm{in}^{2}$$

Substituting in the ASTM grain size equation, 

$$\begin{aligned}
N & =100 \text { grains } / \mathrm{in}^{2}=2^{n-1} \\
\log 100 & =(n-1) \log 2 \\
2 & =(n-1)(0.301) \\
n & =7.64
\end{aligned}$$

question: Suppose that interstitial atoms are found to move from one site to another at the rates of $5 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{jumps} / \mathrm{s}$ at $500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $8 \times 10^{10} \mathrm{jumps} / \mathrm{s}$ at $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Calculate the activation energy $Q$ for the process.
solution:
Figure represents the data on a $\ln (\mathrm{rate})$ versus $1 / T$ plot; the slope of this line, as calculated in the figure, gives $Q / R=14,032 \mathrm{~K}$, or $Q=27,880 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{mol}$. Alternately, we could write two simultaneous equations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Rate}\left(\frac{\text { jumps }}{\mathrm{s}}\right) & =c_0 \exp \left(\frac{-Q}{R T}\right) \\
5 \times 10^8\left(\frac{\text { jumps }}{\mathrm{s}}\right) & =c_0\left(\frac{\text { jumps }}{\mathrm{s}}\right) \exp \left[\frac{-Q\left(\frac{\mathrm{cal}}{\mathrm{~mol}}\right)}{\left[1.987\left(\frac{\mathrm{cal}}{\mathrm{~mol} \cdot \mathrm{~K}}\right)\right][(500+273)(\mathrm{K})]}\right] \\
& =c_0 \exp (-0.000651 Q)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
8 \times 10^{10}\left(\frac{\text { jumps }}{\mathrm{s}}\right) & =c_0\left(\frac{\text { jumps }}{\mathrm{s}}\right) \exp \left[\frac{-Q\left(\frac{\mathrm{cal}}{\mathrm{~mol}}\right)}{\left[1.987\left(\frac{\mathrm{cal}}{\mathrm{~mol} \cdot \mathrm{~K}}\right)\right][(800+273)(\mathrm{K})]}\right] \\
& =c_0 \exp (-0.000469 Q)
\end{aligned}
$$


Note the temperatures were converted into K. Since

$$
c_0=\frac{5 \times 10^8}{\exp (-0.000651 Q)}\left(\frac{\text { jumps }}{\mathrm{s}}\right)
$$

then

$$
\begin{aligned}
8 \times 10^{10} & =\frac{\left(5 \times 10^8\right) \exp (-0.000469 Q)}{\exp (-0.000651 Q)} \\
160 & =\exp [(0.000651-0.000469) Q]=\exp (0.000182 Q) \\
\ln (160) & =5.075=0.000182 Q \\
Q & =\frac{5.075}{0.000182}=27,880 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

question: One step in manufacturing transistors, which function as electronic switches in integrated circuits, involves diffusing impurity atoms into a semiconductor material such as silicon (Si). Consider a diffusion couple between pure tungsten and a tungsten alloy containing 1 at $\%$ thorium. After several minutes of exposure at $2000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, a transition zone of 0.01 cm thickness is established. What is the flux of thorium atoms at this time if diffusion is due to (a) volume diffusion, (b) grain boundary diffusion, and (c) surface diffusion? Refer to Table for the diffusion coefficients and activation energies for thorium in tungsten. The lattice parameter of BCC tungsten is $3.165 \AA$. Give your answer as a tuple (volume diffusion flux, grain boundary diffusion flux, surface diffusion flux).

Table:
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline \multicolumn{5}{|l|}{TABLE 5-2 $\quad$ The effect of the type of diffusion for thorium in tungsten and for self-diffusion in silver} \\
\hline \multirow[b]{2}{*}{Diffusion Type} & \multicolumn{4}{|c|}{Diffusion Coefficient ( $D$ )} \\
\hline & \multicolumn{2}{|c|}{Thorium in Tungsten} & \multicolumn{2}{|c|}{Silver in Silver} \\
\hline & $D_0\left(\mathrm{~cm}^2 / \mathrm{s}\right)$ & $Q$ ( $\mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{mol}$ ) & $D_0\left(\mathrm{~cm}^2 / \mathrm{s}\right)$ & $Q(\mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{mol})$ \\
\hline Surface & 0.47 & 66,400 & 0.068 & 8,900 \\
\hline Grain boundary & 0.74 & 90,000 & 0.24 & 22,750 \\
\hline Volume & 1.00 & 120,000 & 0.99 & 45,700 \\
\hline \multicolumn{5}{|l|}{*Given by parameters for Equation 5-4.} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
solution:
The lattice parameter of BCC tungsten is $3.165 \AA$. Thus, the number of tungsten atoms $/ \mathrm{cm}^3$ is

$$
\frac{\text { W atoms }}{\mathrm{cm}^3}=\frac{2 \text { atoms } / \text { cell }}{\left(3.165 \times 10^{-8}\right)^3 \mathrm{~cm}^3 / \text { cell }}=6.3 \times 10^{22}
$$


In the tungsten -1 at $\%$ thorium alloy, the number of thorium atoms is

$$
c_{\mathrm{Th}}=(0.01)\left(6.3 \times 10^{22}\right)=6.3 \times 10^{20} \mathrm{Th} \frac{\text { atoms }}{\mathrm{cm}^3}
$$
In the pure tungsten, the number of thorium atoms is zero. Thus, the concentration gradient is

$$
\frac{\Delta c}{\Delta x}=\frac{0-6.3 \times 10^{20} \frac{\text { atoms }}{\mathrm{cm}^2}}{0.01 \mathrm{~cm}}=-6.3 \times 10^{22} \mathrm{Th} \frac{\text { atoms }}{\mathrm{cm}^3 \cdot \mathrm{~cm}}
$$

a. Volume diffusion

$$
\begin{aligned}
D & =1.0 \frac{\mathrm{~cm}^2}{\mathrm{~s}} \exp \left(\frac{-120,000 \frac{\mathrm{cal}}{\mathrm{~mol}}}{\left(1.987 \frac{\mathrm{cal}}{\mathrm{~mol} \cdot \mathrm{~K}}\right)(2273 \mathrm{~K})}\right)=2.89 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~cm}^2 / \mathrm{s} \\
J & =-D \frac{\Delta c}{\Delta x}=-\left(2.89 \times 10^{-12 \frac{\mathrm{~cm}^2}{\mathrm{~s}}}\right)\left(-6.3 \times 10^{22} \frac{\mathrm{atoms}}{\mathrm{~cm}^3 \cdot \mathrm{~cm}}\right) \\
& =18.2 \times 10^{\left.10 \frac{\mathrm{Th} \text { atoms }}{\mathrm{cm}}\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

b. Grain boundary diffusion

$$
\left.\begin{array}{rl}
D & =0.74 \frac{\mathrm{~cm}^2}{\mathrm{~s}} \exp \left(\frac{-90,000 \frac{\mathrm{cal}}{\mathrm{~mol}}}{\left(1.987 \frac{\mathrm{cal}}{\mathrm{~mol} \cdot \mathrm{~K}}\right)(2273 \mathrm{~K})}\right)=1.64 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~cm}^2 / \mathrm{s} \\
J & =-\left(1.64 \times 10^{-9} \frac{\mathrm{~cm}^2}{\mathrm{~s}}\right.
\end{array}\right)\left(-6.3 \times 10^{22} \frac{\mathrm{atoms}}{\mathrm{~cm}^3 \cdot \mathrm{~cm}}\right)=10.3 \times 10^{13 \frac{\mathrm{Th} \mathrm{atoms}}{\mathrm{~cm}^2 \cdot \mathrm{~s}}}
$$

c. Surface diffusion

$$
\left.\begin{array}{rl}
D & =0.47 \frac{\mathrm{~cm}^2}{\mathrm{~s}} \exp \left(\frac{-66,400 \frac{\mathrm{cal}}{\mathrm{~mol}}}{\left(1.987 \frac{\mathrm{cal}}{\mathrm{~mol} \cdot \mathrm{~K}}\right)(2273 \mathrm{~K})}\right)=1.94 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~cm}^2 / \mathrm{s} \\
J & =-\left(1.94 \times 10^{-7} \frac{\mathrm{~cm}^2}{\mathrm{~s}}\right.
\end{array}\right)\left(-6.3 \times 10^{22} \frac{\mathrm{atoms}}{\mathrm{~cm}^3 \cdot \mathrm{~cm}}\right)=12.2 \times 10^{15 \frac{\mathrm{Th} \text { atoms }}{\mathrm{cm}^2 \cdot \mathrm{~s}}}
$$

question:
The surface of a $0.1 \% \mathrm{C}$ steel gear is to be hardened by carburizing. In gas carburizing, the steel gears are placed in an atmosphere that provides $1.2 \% \mathrm{C}$ at the surface of the steel at a high temperature. Carbon then diffuses from the surface into the steel. For optimum properties, the steel must contain $0.45 \% \mathrm{C}$ at a depth of 0.2 cm below the surface. Assume that the temperature is high enough (at least $900^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) so that the iron has the FCC structure.

It is given that for these concentration conditions ( $C_s=1.2 \% \mathrm{C}, C_0=0.1 \% \mathrm{C}, C_x=0.45 \% \mathrm{C}$ ) and depth ( $x=0.2 \mathrm{~cm}$ ), the relationship $\frac{C_s-C_x}{C_s-C_0} \approx 0.68$ is satisfied. Furthermore, by consulting an error function table, this corresponds to the argument of the error function $\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}$ being approximately 0.71 .

Using this information and the diffusion data for C in FCC iron from given data ( $D_0=0.23 \mathrm{~cm}^2 / \mathrm{s}$, $Q=32,900 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{mol}$, and the gas constant $R=1.987 \mathrm{cal} /(\mathrm{mol} \cdot \mathrm{K})$ ), derive an expression for the carburizing time $t$ (in seconds) as a function of absolute temperature $T$ (in Kelvin).

\hline \multicolumn{2}{|l|}{TABLE 5-3 Error function values for Fick's second law} \\
\hline Argument of the Error Function $\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}$ & Value of the Error Function erf $\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}$ \\
\hline 0 & 0 \\
\hline 0.10 & 0.1125 \\

\hline Au in Ag & 45,500 & 0.26 \\
\hline Ag in Au & 40,200 & 0.072 \\
\hline Al in Cu & 39,500 & 0.045 \\
\hline Al in $\mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ & 114,000 & 28.0 \\
\hline O in $\mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ & 152,000 & 1900.0 \\
\hline Mg in MgO & 79,000 & 0.249 \\
\hline O in MgO & 82,100 & 0.000043 \\

solution:
Since the boundary conditions for which Equation ($\frac{c_s-c_x}{c_s-c_0}=\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}\right)$) was derived are assumed to be valid, we can use this equation:

$$
\frac{c_s-c_x}{c_s-c_0}=\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}\right)
$$


We know that $c_s=1.2 \% \mathrm{C}, c_0=0.1 \% \mathrm{C}, c_x=0.45 \% \mathrm{C}$, and $x=0.2 \mathrm{~cm}$. From Fick's second law:

$$
\frac{c_s-c_x}{c_s-c_0}=\frac{1.2 \% \mathrm{C}-0.45 \% \mathrm{C}}{1.2 \% \mathrm{C}-0.1 \% \mathrm{C}}=0.68=\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{0.2 \mathrm{~cm}}{2 \sqrt{D t}}\right)=\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{0.1 \mathrm{~cm}}{\sqrt{D t}}\right)
$$


From Table 5-3, we find that

$$
\frac{0.1 \mathrm{~cm}}{\sqrt{D t}}=0.71 \text { or } D t=\left(\frac{0.1}{0.71}\right)^2=0.0198 \mathrm{~cm}^2
$$


Any combination of $D$ and $t$ with a product of $0.0198 \mathrm{~cm}^2$ will work. For carbon diffusing in FCC iron, the diffusion coefficient is related to temperature by Equation:

$$
D=D_0 \exp \left(\frac{-Q}{R T}\right)
$$


From Table 5-1:

$$
D=0.23 \exp \left(\frac{-32,900 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{mol}}{1.987 \frac{\mathrm{cal}}{\mathrm{~mol} \cdot \mathrm{~K}} T(\mathrm{~K})}\right)=0.23 \exp \left(\frac{-16,558}{T}\right)
$$


Therefore, the temperature and time of the heat treatment are related by

$$
t=\frac{0.0198 \mathrm{~cm}^2}{D \frac{\mathrm{~cm}^2}{\mathrm{~s}}}=\frac{0.0198 \mathrm{~cm}^2}{0.23 \exp (-16,558 / T) \frac{\mathrm{cm}^2}{\mathrm{~s}}}=\frac{0.0861}{\exp (-16,558 / T)}
$$


Some typical combinations of temperatures and times are
If $T=900^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=1173 \mathrm{~K}$, then $t=116,273 \mathrm{~s}=32.3 \mathrm{~h}$
If $T=1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=1273 \mathrm{~K}$, then $t=38,362 \mathrm{~s}=10.7 \mathrm{~h}$
If $T=1100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=1373 \mathrm{~K}$, then $t=14,876 \mathrm{~s}=4.13 \mathrm{~h}$
If $T=1200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=1473 \mathrm{~K}$, then $t=6,560 \mathrm{~s}=1.82 \mathrm{~h}$

question:
Design of a More Economical Heat TreatmentnnWe find that 10 hours are required to successfully carburize a batch of 500 steel gears at $900^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, where the iron has the FCC structure. It costs $\$1000$ per hour to operate the carburizing furnace at $900^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $\$1500$ per hour to operate the furnace at $1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. For carbon diffusing in FCC iron, the activation energy is $32,900 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{mol}$. Is it economical to increase the carburizing temperature to $1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$?

 **Give your answer as a tuple: (required_time_at_1000C, cost_per_part_at_900C, cost_per_part_at_1000C).*
solution:
We again assume that we can use the solution to Fick's second law given by Equation:

$$
\frac{c_s-c_x}{c_s-c_0}=\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D t}}\right)
$$


Note that since we are dealing with only changes in heat treatment time and temperature, the term $D t$ must be constant.

The temperatures of interest are $900^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=1173 \mathrm{~K}$ and $1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=1273 \mathrm{~K}$. To achieve the same carburizing treatment at $1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ as at $900^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ :

$$
D_{1273} t_{1273}=D_{1173} t_{1173}
$$


For carbon diffusing in FCC iron, the activation energy is $32,900 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{mol}$. Since we are dealing with the ratios of times, it does not matter whether we substitute for the time in hours or seconds. It is, however, always a good idea to use units that balance out. Therefore, we will show time in seconds. Note that temperatures must be converted into Kelvin.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{1273} t_{1273} & =D_{1173} t_{1173} \\
D= & D_0 \exp (-Q / R T) \\
t_{1273}= & \frac{D_{1173} t_{1173}}{D_{1273}} \\
= & \frac{D_0 \exp \left(-\frac{32,900 \frac{\mathrm{cal}}{\mathrm{~mol}}}{1.987 \frac{\mathrm{cal}}{\mathrm{~mol} \cdot \mathrm{~K}} 1173 \mathrm{~K}}\right)(10 \mathrm{hours})(3600 \mathrm{sec} / \mathrm{hour})}{D_0 \exp \left(-\frac{32,900 \frac{\mathrm{cal}}{\mathrm{~mol}}}{1.987 \frac{\mathrm{cal}}{\mathrm{~mol} \cdot \mathrm{~K}} 1273 \mathrm{~K}}\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{1273} & =\frac{\exp (-14.1156)(10)(3600)}{\exp (-13.0068)} \\
& =(10)(0.3299)(3600) \mathrm{s} \\
t_{1273} & =3.299 \mathrm{~h}=3 \mathrm{~h} \text { and } 18 \mathrm{~min}
\end{aligned}
$$


Notice, we did not need the value of the pre-exponential term $D_0$, since it canceled out.

At $900^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the cost per part is $(\$ 1000 / \mathrm{h})(10 \mathrm{~h}) / 500$ parts $=\$ 20 /$ part. At $1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the cost per part is $(\$ 1500 / \mathrm{h})(3.299 \mathrm{~h}) / 500$ parts $=\$ 9.90 /$ part.

solution:

question:
An aluminum rod is to withstand an applied force of 45,000 pounds. The engineering stress-strain curve for the aluminum alloy to be used is shown in Figure. To ensure safety, the maximum allowable stress on the rod is limited to $25,000 \mathrm{psi}$, which is below the yield strength of the aluminum. The rod must be at least 150 in. long but must deform elastically no more than 0.25 in. when the force is applied. Design an appropriate rod.

Reference Context: The engineering stress-strain curve (see Figure) is used to record the results of a tensile test. The curve helps in determining the elastic and plastic deformation characteristics of the material under load.

*Give your answer as the value of minimum_diameter_in.
solution:
From the definition of engineering strain,

$$e=\frac{\Delta l}{l_{0}}$$

For a rod that is 150 in. long, the strain that corresponds to an extension of 0.25 in. is

$$e=\frac{0.25 \mathrm{in.}}{150 \mathrm{in.}}=0.00167$$

According to Figure, this strain is purely elastic, and the corresponding stress value is approximately $17,000 \mathrm{psi}$, which is below the $25,000 \mathrm{psi}$ limit. We use the definition of engineering stress to calculate the required cross-sectional area of the rod:

$$S=\frac{F}{A_{0}}$$

Note that the stress must not exceed $17,000 \mathrm{psi}$, or consequently, the deflection will be greater than 0.25 in. Rearranging,

$$A_{0}=\frac{F}{S}=\frac{45,000 \mathrm{lb}}{17,000 \mathrm{psi}}=2.65 \mathrm{in.}^{2}$$

The rod can be produced in various shapes, provided that the cross-sectional area is 2.65 in.${ }^{2}$ For a round cross section, the minimum diameter to ensure that the stress is not too high is

$$A_{0}=\frac{\pi d^{2}}{4}=2.65 \mathrm{in.}^{2} \quad \text { or } \quad d=1.84 \mathrm{in}$$

Thus, one possible design that meets all of the specified criteria is a suspension rod that is 150 in. long with a diameter of 1.84 in.

The solution utilizes the engineering stress-strain curve (see Figure) to ensure the design meets the specified criteria for stress and deformation.

question:
Calculate the modulus of elasticity of the aluminum alloy for which the engineering stress-strain curve is shown in Figure. Calculate the length of a bar of initial length 50 in. when a tensile stress of $30,000 \mathrm{psi}$ is applied. The modulus of elasticity is closely related to the binding energies of the atoms, indicating that high forces are required to separate the atoms and cause the material to stretch elastically. Thus, the material has a high modulus of elasticity. For metallic materials, the modulus of elasticity is considered a microstructure insensitive property since the value is dominated by the stiffness of atomic bonds.

**Give your answer as a tuple: (modulus_of_elasticity, final_length).*
solution:
When a stress of $34,948 \mathrm{psi}$ is applied, a strain of $0.0035 \mathrm{in.} / \mathrm{in.}$ is produced. Thus, the modulus of elasticity is calculated as:

$$\text{Modulus of elasticity} = E = \frac{S}{e} = \frac{34,948 \mathrm{psi}}{0.0035} = 10 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{psi}$$

Note that any combination of stress and strain in the elastic region will produce this result. From Hooke's Law,

$$e = \frac{S}{E} = \frac{30,000 \mathrm{psi}}{10 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{psi}} = 0.003 \mathrm{in.} / \mathrm{in.}$$

From the definition of engineering strain,

$$e = \frac{\Delta l}{l_{0}}$$

Thus,

$$\Delta l = e(l_{0}) = 0.003 \mathrm{in.} / \mathrm{in.}(50 \mathrm{in.}) = 0.15 \mathrm{in.}$$

When the bar is subjected to a stress of $30,000 \mathrm{psi}$, the total length is given by

$$l = \Delta l + l_{0} = 0.15 \mathrm{in.} + 50 \mathrm{in.} = 50.15 \mathrm{in.}$$

The stiffness of a component is proportional to its Young's modulus. A component with a high modulus of elasticity will show much smaller changes in dimensions if the applied stress causes only elastic deformation when compared to a component with a lower elastic modulus.

question:
The aluminum alloy in has a final length after failure of 2.195 in. and a final diameter of 0.398 in at the fractured surface. Original gauge length l₀ = 2.000 in. and original diameter d₀ = 0.505 in. Calculate the ductility of this alloy. The ductility can be measured by the percent elongation and the percent reduction in area, as defined by the equations:

$$\% \text { Elongation }=\frac{l_{f}-l_{0}}{l_{0}} \times 100$$

$$\% \text { Reduction in area }=\frac{A_{0}-A_{f}}{A_{0}} \times 100$$

where $l_{f}$ is the final length after failure, $l_{0}$ is the original length, $A_{f}$ is the final cross-sectional area at the fracture surface, and $A_{0}$ is the original cross-sectional area. Give your answer as a tuple (percent elongation, percent reduction in area).
solution:
The ductility of the aluminum alloy is calculated using the percent elongation and the percent reduction in area:

$$\begin{aligned}
\% \text { Elongation } & =\frac{l_{f}-l_{0}}{l_{0}} \times 100=\frac{2.195-2.000}{2.000} \times 100=9.75 \% \\
\% \text { Reduction in area } & =\frac{A_{0}-A_{f}}{A_{0}} \times 100 \\
& =\frac{(\pi / 4)(0.505)^{2}-(\pi / 4)(0.398)^{2}}{(\pi / 4)(0.505)^{2}} \times 100 \\
& =37.9 \%
\end{aligned}$$

question: The flexural strength of a composite material reinforced with glass fibers is $45,000 \mathrm{psi}$, and the flexural modulus is $18 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{psi}$. A sample, which is 0.5 in. wide, 0.375 in. high, and 8 in. long, is supported between two rods 5 in. apart. Determine the force required to fracture the material and the deflection of the sample at fracture, assuming that no plastic deformation occurs. Give your answer as a tuple (force, deflection).

Figure shows the results of an indentation into single crystal (001) MgO using a diamond Berkovich indenter. The unloading stiffness at a maximum indentation depth of $1.45 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ is $1.8 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$. A calibration on fused silica indicates that the projected contact area at the corresponding contact depth is $41 \mu \mathrm{~m}^2$. The Poisson's ratio of MgO is 0.17 . Calculate the elastic modulus and hardness of MgO . Give your answer as a tuple (elastic modulus, hardness).
solution:
The projected contact area $A_c=41 \mu \mathrm{~m}^2 \times \frac{(1 \mathrm{~m})^2}{\left(10^6 \mu \mathrm{~m}\right)^2}=41 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~m}^2$.
The reduced modulus is given by

$$
E_r=\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2 \beta} \frac{S}{\sqrt{A_c}}=\frac{\sqrt{\pi}\left(1.8 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}\right)}{2(1.034) \sqrt{\left(41 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~m}^2\right)}}=241 \times 10^9 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2=241 \mathrm{GPa}
$$


Substituting for the Poisson's ratio of MgO and the elastic constants of diamond and solving for $E$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{E_r} & =\frac{1-0.17^2}{E}+\frac{1-0.07^2}{1.141 \times 10^{12} \mathrm{~Pa}}=\frac{1}{241 \times 10^9 \mathrm{~Pa}} \\
\frac{0.9711}{E} & =\frac{1}{241 \times 10^9 \mathrm{~Pa}}-\frac{1-0.07^2}{1.141 \times 10^{12} \mathrm{~Pa}} \\
E & =296 \mathrm{GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$


From Figure, the load at the indentation depth of $1.45 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ is 380 mN $\left(380 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~N}\right)$. Thus, the hardness is

$$
H=\frac{P_{\max }}{A_c}=\frac{380 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~N}}{41 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~m}^2}=9.3 \times 10^9 \mathrm{~Pa}=9.3 \mathrm{GPa}
$$

question: Assume that an advanced ceramic sialon (acronym for SiAION or silicon aluminum oxynitride), has a tensile strength of $60,000 \mathrm{psi}$. Let us assume that this value is for a flaw-free ceramic. (In practice, it is almost impossible to produce flaw-free ceramics.) A thin crack 0.01 in. deep is observed before a sialon part is tested. The part unexpectedly fails at a stress of 500 psi by propagation of the crack. Estimate the radius of the crack tip.
solution:
The failure occurred because the 500 psi applied stress, magnified by the stress concentration at the tip of the crack, produced an actual stress equal to the ultimate tensile strength. Using the equation $\sigma_{\text{actual}} = 2 \sigma \sqrt{a / r}$, we can solve for $r$:

$$
\begin{gathered}
60,000 \mathrm{psi} = (2)(500 \mathrm{psi}) \sqrt{0.01 \mathrm{in.} / r} \\
\sqrt{0.01 / r} = 60 \text{ or } 0.01 / r = 3600 \\
r = 2.8 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{in.} = 7.1 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~cm} = 710 \AA
\end{gathered}
$$

The likelihood of our being able to measure a radius of curvature of this size by any method of nondestructive testing is virtually zero. Therefore, although the equation may help illustrate the factors that influence how a crack propagates in a brittle material, it does not help in predicting the strength of actual ceramic parts.

question: Determine the minimum allowable thickness for a 3-in.-wide plate made of sialon that has a fracture toughness of $9,000 \mathrm{psi} \sqrt{\mathrm{in}}$. The plate must withstand a tensile load of $40,000 \mathrm{lb}$. The part will be nondestructively tested to ensure that no flaws are present that might cause failure. The minimum allowable thickness of the part will depend on the minimum flaw size that can be determined by the available testing technique. Assume that three nondestructive testing techniques are available. X-ray radiography can detect flaws larger than 0.02 in.; gamma-ray radiography can detect flaws larger than 0.008 in.; and ultrasonic inspection can detect flaws larger than 0.005 in . Assume that the geometry factor $f=1.0$ for all flaws. **Give your answer as a tuple: (minimum_thickness_X_ray, minimum_thickness_gamma_ray, minimum_thickness_ultrasonic).**
solution:
For the given flaw sizes, we must calculate the minimum thickness of the plate that will ensure that these flaw sizes will not propagate. From Equation ($\sigma=\frac{K}{f \sqrt{\pi a}}$),

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\min } & =\frac{F \sqrt{\pi a}}{K_{I c}}=\frac{(40,000)(\sqrt{\pi})(\sqrt{a})}{9,000} \\
A_{\min } & =7.88 \sqrt{a} \text { in. }^{2} \text { and thickness }=\left(7.88 \text { in. }^{2} / 3 \text { in. }\right) \sqrt{a}=2.63 \sqrt{a}
\end{aligned}$$

The following table summarizes the results for each nondestructive testing method:

\begin{tabular}{lcccc}
\hline \begin{tabular}{l} 
Nondestructive \\
Testing \\
Method
\end{tabular} & \begin{tabular}{c} 
Smallest \\
Detectable \\
Crack (in.)
\end{tabular} & \begin{tabular}{c}

(in. ${ }^2$ )
\end{tabular} & \begin{tabular}{c} 
Minimum \\

(in.)
\end{tabular} & \begin{tabular}{c}

\hline X-ray radiography & 0.020 & 1.11 & 0.37 & 36,000 \\
$\gamma$-ray radiography & 0.008 & 0.70 & 0.23 & 57,000 \\
Ultrasonic inspection & 0.005 & 0.56 & 0.19 & 71,000 \\
\hline

Our ability to detect flaws, coupled with our ability to produce a ceramic with flaws smaller than our detection limit, significantly affects the maximum stress than can be tolerated and, hence, the size of the part. In this example, the part can be smaller if ultrasonic inspection is available.

The fracture toughness is also important. Had we used $\mathrm{Si}_{3} \mathrm{~N}_{4}$, with a fracture toughness of $3,000 \mathrm{psi} \sqrt{\text { in. }}$. instead of the sialon, we could repeat the calculations and show that, for ultrasonic testing, the minimum thickness is 0.56 in . and the maximum stress is only $24,000 \mathrm{psi}$.

question: An advanced engineered ceramic has a Weibull modulus $m=9$. The flexural strength is 250 MPa at a probability of failure $F=0.4$. What is the level of flexural strength if the probability of failure has to be 0.1 ?
solution:
We assume all samples tested had the same volume thus the size of the sample will not be a factor in this case. We can use the symbol $V$ for sample volume instead of $V_0$. We are dealing with a brittle material so we begin with Equation:

$$
F(V)=1-P(V)=1-\exp \left[-\left(\frac{\sigma}{\sigma_0}\right)^m\right]
$$

or

$$
1-F(V)=\exp \left[-\left(\frac{\sigma}{\sigma_0}\right)^m\right]
$$


Take the logarithm of both sides:

$$
\ln [1-F(V)]=-\left(\frac{\sigma}{\sigma_0}\right)^m
$$


Take logarithms of both sides again,

$$
\ln \{-\ln [1-F(V)]\}=m\left(\ln \sigma-\ln \sigma_0\right)
$$


We can eliminate the minus sign on the left-hand side of Equation ($\ln \{-\ln [1-F(V)]\}=m\left(\ln \sigma-\ln \sigma_0\right)$) by rewriting it as

$$
\ln \left\{\ln \left[\frac{1}{1-F(V)}\right]\right\}=m\left(\ln \sigma-\ln \sigma_0\right)
$$
For $F=0.4, \sigma=250 \mathrm{MPa}$, and $m=9$ in Equation ($\ln \left\{\ln \left[\frac{1}{1-F(V)}\right]\right\}=m\left(\ln \sigma-\ln \sigma_0\right)$), we have

$$
\ln \left[\ln \left(\frac{1}{1-0.4}\right)\right]=9\left(\ln 250-\ln \sigma_0\right)
$$


Therefore,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln [\ln (1 / 0.6)] & =\ln [\ln (1.66667)]=\ln (0.510826)=-0.67173 \\
& =9\left(5.52146-\ln \sigma_0\right) .
\end{aligned}
$$


Therefore, $\ln \sigma_0=5.52146+0.07464=5.5961$. This gives us a value of $\sigma_0=269.4 \mathrm{MPa}$. This is the characteristic strength of the ceramic. For a stress level of 269.4 MPa , the probability of survival is 0.37 (or the probability of failure is 0.63 ). As the required probability of failure $(F)$ goes down, the stress level to which the ceramic can be subjected $(\sigma)$ also goes down. Note that if Equation ($\ln \left[\ln \left(\frac{1}{1-0.4}\right)\right]=9\left(\ln 250-\ln \sigma_0\right)$) is solved exactly for $\sigma_0$, a slightly different value is obtained.

Now, we want to determine the value of $\sigma$ for $F=0.1$. We know that $m=9$ and $\sigma_0=269.4 \mathrm{MPa}$, so we need to get the value of $\sigma$. We substitute these values into Equation ($\ln \left\{\ln \left[\frac{1}{1-F(V)}\right]\right\}=m\left(\ln \sigma-\ln \sigma_0\right)$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln \left[\ln \left(\frac{1}{1-0.1}\right)\right] & =9(\ln \sigma-\ln 269.4) \\
\ln \left[\ln \left(\frac{1}{0.9}\right)\right] & =9(\ln \sigma-\ln 269.4) \\
\ln (\ln 1.11111)=\ln (0.105361) & =-2.25037=9(\ln \sigma-5.5962) \\
\therefore-0.25004 & =\ln \sigma-5.5962, \text { or } \\
\ln \sigma & =5.3462
\end{aligned}
$$

or $\sigma=209.8 \mathrm{MPa}$. As expected, as we lowered the probability of failure to 0.1 , we also decreased the level of stress that can be supported.

question:
A high-strength steel plate, which has a plane strain fracture toughness of $80 \mathrm{MPa} \sqrt{\mathrm{m}}$ is alternately loaded in tension to 500 MPa and in compression to 60 MPa. The plate is to survive for 10 years with the stress being applied at a frequency of once every 5 minutes. Design a manufacturing and testing procedure that ensures that the component will serve as intended. Assume a geometry factor $f=1.0$ for all flaws. The crack growth rate versus the stress intensity factor range for this steel is given by $\frac{d a}{d N}=C(\Delta K)^{n}$, where $C=1.62 \times 10^{-12}$ and $n=3.2$ for the units shown in the figure. 

Give your answer as a tuple: (critical crack size, initial surface flaw size, number of cycles).

A 1-cm-thick copper plate is cold-reduced to 0.50 cm and later further reduced to 0.16 cm. Determine the total percent cold work and the tensile strength of the 0.16 cm plate. (Refer to the figures for mechanical properties of copper after cold working.) 

Give your answer as a tuple: (total_percent_cold_work, tensile_strength).
solution:
The total percent cold work (%CW) can be calculated using the formula for percent reduction in thickness, since the width of the plate does not change during rolling. The formula is given by:

$$\% \mathrm{CW} = \left[\frac{t_{0} - t_{f}}{t_{0}}\right] \times 100$$

where $t_{0}$ is the original thickness and $t_{f}$ is the final thickness. Substituting the given values:

$$\% \mathrm{CW} = \left[\frac{1 \mathrm{~cm} - 0.16 \mathrm{~cm}}{1 \mathrm{~cm}}\right] \times 100 = 84\%$$

From Figure 8-7, the tensile strength corresponding to 84% cold work is approximately $85,000 \mathrm{psi}$.

$r^{*}$ is given by 

$$ 
r^{*}=\frac{2 \sigma_{s l} T_{m}}{\Delta H_{f} \Delta T}=\frac{(2)\left(177 \times 10^{-7}\right)(1358)}{(1628)(236)}=12.51 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~cm} 
$$ 

Note that a temperature difference of $1^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is equal to a temperature change of 1 K , or $\Delta T=236^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=236 \mathrm{~K}$. 

The lattice parameter for FCC copper is $a_{0}=0.3615 \mathrm{~nm}=3.615 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~cm}$. Thus, the unit cell volume is given by 

$$ 
V_{\text {unit cell }}=\left(a_{0}\right)^{3}=\left(3.615 \times 10^{-8}\right)^{3}=47.24 \times 10^{-24} \mathrm{~cm}^{3} 
$$ 

The volume of the critical radius is given by 

$$ 
V_{r^{*}}=\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}=\left(\frac{4}{3} \pi\right)\left(12.51 \times 10^{-8}\right)^{3}=8200 \times 10^{-24} \mathrm{~cm}^{3} 
$$ 

The number of unit cells in the critical nucleus is 

$$ 
\frac{V_{\text {unit cell }}}{V_{r^{*}}}=\frac{8200 \times 10^{-24}}{47.24 \times 10^{-24}}=174 \text { unit cells } 
$$ 

Since there are four atoms in each FCC unit cell, the number of atoms in the critical nucleus must be 

$$ 
(4 \text { atoms } / \text { cell })(174 \text { cells } / \text { nucleus })=696 \text { atoms } / \text { nucleus } 
$$ 

In these types of calculations, we assume that a nucleus that is made from only a few hundred atoms still exhibits properties similar to those of bulk materials. This is not strictly correct and as such is a weakness of the classical theory of nucleation.

question: Determine the constants in the equation that describe the relationship between secondary dendrite arm spacing and solidification time for aluminum alloys (see Figure). Give the answer as a tuple (m, k).
solution:
We could obtain the value of SDAS at two times from the graph and calculate k and $m$ using simultaneous equations; however, if the scales on the ordinate and abscissa are equal for powers of ten (as in Figure), we can obtain the slope m from the log-log plot by directly measuring the slope of the graph. In Figure, we can mark five equal units on the vertical scale and 12 equal units on the horizontal scale. The slope is

$$
\mathrm{m}=\frac{5}{12}=0.42
$$


The constant k is the value of SDAS when $t_s=1 \mathrm{~s}$, since

$$
\log \mathrm{SDAS}=\log \mathrm{k}+\mathrm{m} \log t_s
$$


If $t_s=1 \mathrm{~s}, \mathrm{~m} \log t_s=0$, and $\mathrm{SDAS}=\mathrm{k}$, from Figure:

$$
\mathrm{k}=7 \times 10^{-4} \frac{\mathrm{~cm}}{\mathrm{~s}}
$$

question: A 4-in.-diameter aluminum bar solidifies to a depth of 0.5 in. beneath the surface in 5 minutes. After 20 minutes, the bar has solidified to a depth of 1.5 in. How much time is required for the bar to solidify completely?

Design the thickness of an aluminum alloy casting with a length of 12 in., a width of 8 in., and a tensile strength of $40,000 \mathrm{psi}$. The mold constant in Chvorinov's rule for aluminum alloys cast in a sand mold is $45 \mathrm{~min} / \mathrm{in}^{2}$. Assume that data shown in Figures 9-6 and 9-7 can be used.
solution:
In order to obtain a tensile strength of $40,000 \mathrm{psi}$, a secondary dendrite arm spacing of about 0.007 cm is required (see Figure 9-7). From Figure 9-6 we can determine that the solidification time required to obtain this spacing is about 300 s or 5 minutes. From Chvorinov's rule

$$
t_{s}=\mathrm{B}\left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{2}
$$

where $\mathrm{B}=45 \mathrm{~min} / \mathrm{in} .{ }^{2}$ and $x$ is the thickness of the casting. Since the length is 12 in. and the width is 8 in.,

$$
\begin{gathered}
V=(8)(12)(x)=96 x \\
A=(2)(8)(12)+(2)(x)(8)+(2)(x)(12)=40 x+192 \\
5 \min =\left(45 \mathrm{~min} / \mathrm{in} .^{2}\right)\left(\frac{96 x}{40 x+192}\right)^{2} \\
\frac{96 x}{40 x+192}=\sqrt{(5 / 45})=0.333 \\
96 x=13.33 x+64 \\
x=0.77 \mathrm{in} .
\end{gathered}
$$

question: Using Chvorinov’s rule (t = B·(V/A)²), design a cylindrical riser of height H = 2 D on a 2 cm × 8 cm × 16 cm casting so that its solidification time is 25 % longer than that of the casting.  Determine the required riser diameter D.
solution:
We know that the riser must freeze after the casting. To be conservative, we typically require that the riser take $25 \%$ longer to solidify than the casting. Therefore,

$$
t_{\mathrm{r}}=1.25 t_{\mathrm{c}} \text { or } \mathrm{B}\left(\frac{V}{A}\right)_{\mathrm{r}}^2=1.25 \mathrm{~B}\left(\frac{V}{A}\right)_{\mathrm{c}}^2
$$


The subscripts $r$ and $c$ stand for riser and casting, respectively. The mold constant B is the same for both casting and riser, so

$$
\left(\frac{V}{A}\right)_{\mathrm{r}}=\sqrt{1.25}\left(\frac{V}{A}\right)_{\mathrm{c}}
$$


The volume of the casting is

$$
V_{\mathrm{c}}=(2 \mathrm{~cm})(8 \mathrm{~cm})(16 \mathrm{~cm})=256 \mathrm{~cm}^3
$$


The area of the riser adjoined to the casting must be subtracted from the total surface area of the casting in order to calculate the surface area of the casting in contact with the mold:

$$
A_{\mathrm{c}}=(2)(2 \mathrm{~cm})(8 \mathrm{~cm})+(2)(2 \mathrm{~cm})(16 \mathrm{~cm})+(2)(8 \mathrm{~cm})(16 \mathrm{~cm})-\frac{\pi D^2}{4}=352 \mathrm{~cm}^2-\frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

where $D$ is the diameter of the cylindrical riser. We can write equations for the volume and area of the cylindrical riser, noting that the cylinder height $H=2 D$ :

$$
\begin{aligned}
& V_{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{\pi D^2}{4} H=\frac{\pi D^2}{4}(2 D)=\frac{\pi D^3}{2} \\
& A_{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{\pi D^2}{4}+\pi D H=\frac{\pi D^2}{4}+\pi D(2 D)=\frac{9}{4} \pi D^2
\end{aligned}
$$

where again we have not included the area of the riser adjoined to the casting in the area calculation. The volume to area ratio of the riser is given by

$$
\left(\frac{V}{A}\right)_{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{\left(\pi D^3 / 2\right)}{\left(9 \pi D^2 / 4\right)}=\frac{2}{9} D
$$

and must be greater than that of the casting according to

$$
\left(\frac{V}{A}\right)_{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{2}{9} D>\sqrt{1.25}\left(\frac{V}{A}\right)_{\mathrm{c}}
$$


Substituting,

$$
\frac{2}{9} D>\sqrt{1.25}\left(\frac{256 \mathrm{~cm}^3}{352 \mathrm{~cm}^2-\pi D^2 / 4}\right)
$$


Solving for the smallest diameter for the riser:

$$
D=3.78 \mathrm{~cm}
$$


Although the volume of the riser is less than that of the casting, the riser solidifies more slowly because of its compact shape.

question:
From the phase diagram for the NiO-MgO binary system [see Figure], describe a composition that can melt at $2600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ but will not melt when placed into service at $2300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. 

Give your answer as a tuple: (min MgO mol percent, max MgO mol percent).
solution: The material must have a liquidus temperature below $2600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, but a solidus temperature above $2300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The $\mathrm{NiO}-\mathrm{MgO}$ phase diagram [see Figure] permits us to choose an appropriate composition. To identify a composition with a liquidus temperature below $2600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, there must be less than $60 \mathrm{~mol} \% \mathrm{MgO}$ in the refractory. To identify a composition with a solidus temperature above $2300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, there must be at least $50 \mathrm{~mol} \% \mathrm{MgO}$ present. Consequently, we can use any composition between $50 \mathrm{~mol} \% \mathrm{MgO}$ and $60 \mathrm{~mol} \% \mathrm{MgO}$.

question: Determine the degrees of freedom in a $\mathrm{Cu}-40 \% \mathrm{Ni}$ alloy at (a) $1300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, (b) $1250^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, and (c) $1200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Use Figure. Give your answer as a tuple (degrees of freedom at 1300°C, degrees of freedom at 1250°C, degrees of freedom at 1200°C).
solution:
This is a binary system $(C=2)$. The two components are Cu and Ni. We will assume constant pressure. Therefore, Equation $(1+C=F+P)$ can be used as follows.
(a) At $1300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, P=1$, since only one phase (liquid) is present; $C=2$, since both copper and nickel atoms are present. Thus,

$$
1+C=F+P \quad \therefore 1+2=F+1 \text { or } F=2
$$

We must fix both the temperature and the composition of the liquid phase to completely describe the state of the copper-nickel alloy in the liquid region.
(b) At $1250^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, P=2$, since both liquid and solid are present; $C=2$, since copper and nickel atoms are present. Now,

$$
1+C=F+P \quad \therefore 1+2=F+2 \text { or } F=1
$$

If we fix the temperature in the two-phase region, the compositions of the two phases are also fixed. Alternately, if the composition of one phase is fixed, the temperature and composition of the second phase are automatically fixed.
(c) At $1200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, P=1$, since only one phase (solid) is present; $C=2$, since both copper and nickel atoms are present. Again,

$$
1+C=F+P \quad \therefore 1+2=F+1 \text { or } F=2
$$

and we must fix both temperature and composition to completely describe the state of the solid.

question:
Determine the composition of each phase in a $\mathrm{Cu}-40 \% \mathrm{Ni}$ alloy at $1300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 1270^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, $1250^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, and $1200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (See Figure.)

**Give your answer as a tuple: (liquid_Ni_1300C, liquid_Ni_1270C, solid_Ni_1270C, liquid_Ni_1250C, solid_Ni_1250C, solid_Ni_1200C).**
solution:
The vertical line at $40 \%$ Ni represents the overall composition of the alloy.
- $\mathbf{1 3 0 0}{ }^{\circ} \mathbf{C}$ : Only liquid is present. The liquid must contain $40 \% \mathrm{Ni}$, the overall composition of the alloy.
- $\mathbf{1 2 7 0}{ }^{\circ} \mathbf{C}$ : Two phases are present. A horizontal line within the $\alpha+L$ field is drawn. The endpoint at the liquidus, which is in contact with the liquid region, is at $37 \%$ Ni . The endpoint at the solidus, which is in contact with the $\alpha$ region, is at $50 \% \mathrm{Ni}$. Therefore, the liquid contains $37 \% \mathrm{Ni}$, and the solid contains $50 \% \mathrm{Ni}$.
- $\mathbf{1 2 5 0}{ }^{\boldsymbol{\circ}} \mathbf{C}$ : Again two phases are present. The tie line drawn at this temperature shows that the liquid contains $32 \% \mathrm{Ni}$, and the solid contains $45 \% \mathrm{Ni}$.
- $\mathbf{1 2 0 0}{ }^{\circ} \mathbf{C}$ : Only solid $\alpha$ is present, so the solid must contain $40 \% \mathrm{Ni}$.

question: Calculate the amounts of $\alpha$ and $L$ at $1250^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in the $\mathrm{Cu}-40 \% \mathrm{Ni}$ alloy shown in Figure. Give your answer as a tuple (alpha_mass_percent, liquid_mass_percent).
solution:
Let's say that $x=$ mass fraction of the alloy that is solid $\alpha$. Since we have only two phases, the balance of the alloy must be in the liquid phase $(L)$. Thus, the mass fraction of liquid will be $1-x$. Consider 100 grams of the alloy. This alloy will consist of 40 grams of nickel at all temperatures. At $1250^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, let us write an equation that will represent the mass balance for nickel. At $1250^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, we have $100 x$ grams of the $\alpha$ phase. We have $100(1-x)$ grams of liquid.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Total mass of nickel in } 100 \text { grams of the alloy }=\begin{array}{l}
\text { mass of nickel in liquid } \\
+ \text { mass of nickel in } \alpha
\end{array} \\
& \therefore 100 \times(\% \text { Ni in alloy })=[(100)(1-x)](\% \text { Ni in } L)+(100)(x)(\% \text { Ni in } \alpha) \\
& \therefore(\% \text { Ni in alloy })=(\% \text { Ni in } L)(1-x)+(\% \text { Ni in } \alpha)(x)
\end{aligned}
$$

By multiplying and rearranging,

$$
x=\frac{(\% \text { Ni in alloy })-(\% \text { Ni in } L)}{(\% \text { Ni in } \alpha)-(\% \text { Ni in } L)}
$$

From the phase diagram at $1250^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$:

$$
x=\frac{40-32}{45-32}=\frac{8}{13}=0.62
$$

If we convert from mass fraction to mass percent, the alloy at $1250^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ contains $62 \%$ $\alpha$ and $38 \% L$. Note that the concentration of nickel in the $\alpha$ phase (at $1250^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$) is $45 \%$, and the concentration of nickel in the liquid phase (at $1250^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$) is $32 \%$.

question:
Determine the amount of each phase in the $\mathrm{Cu}-40 \% \mathrm{Ni}$ alloy shown in Figure at $1300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 1270^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 1250^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, and $1200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

**Give your answer as a tuple: (percent_liquid_1300C, percent_liquid_1270C, percent_alpha_1270C, percent_liquid_1250C, percent_alpha_1250C, percent_alpha_1200C).**
solution:
- $1300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ : There is only one phase, so $100 \% L$.
- $1270^{\circ} \mathrm{C}: \% L=\frac{50-40}{50-37} \times 100=77 \%$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \alpha & =\frac{40-37}{50-37} \times 100=23 \% \\
\text { - } 1250^{\circ} \mathrm{C}: \% L & =\frac{45-40}{45-32} \times 100=38 \% \\
\% \alpha & =\frac{40-32}{45-32} \times 100=62 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

- $1200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ : There is only one phase, so $100 \% \alpha$.

Note that at each temperature, we can determine the composition of the phases in equilibrium from the ends of the tie line drawn at that temperature. The lever rule is used to calculate the relative fraction or $\%$ of a phase in a two-phase mixture. The end points of the tie line we use give us the composition (i.e., the chemical concentration of different components) of each phase.

question: Consider the binary phase diagram in Figure. Identify the three-phase reactions that occur. Give your answer as a tuple (reaction1, reaction2, reaction3, reaction4, reaction5).
solution:
We find horizontal lines at $1150^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 920^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 750^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 450^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, and $300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. For $1150^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$: This reaction occurs at $15 \% \mathrm{~B}$ (i.e., the in-between point is at $15 \% \mathrm{~B}$). $\delta+L$ are present above the point, and $\gamma$ is present below. The reaction is

$$
\delta+L \rightarrow \gamma \quad \text { a peritectic }
$$

$920^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$: This reaction occurs at $40 \% \mathrm{~B}$:

$$
L_{1} \rightarrow \gamma+L_{2}, \quad \text { a monotectic }
$$

$750^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$: This reaction occurs at $70 \% \mathrm{~B}$:

$$
L \rightarrow \gamma+\beta, \quad \text { a eutectic }
$$

$450^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$: This reaction occurs at $20 \% \mathrm{~B}$:

$$
\gamma \rightarrow \alpha+\beta, \quad \text { a eutectoid }
$$

$300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$: This reaction occurs at $50 \% \mathrm{~B}$:

$$
\alpha+\beta \rightarrow \mu, \quad \text { a peritectoid }
$$
answer: (δ + L → γ, L₁ → γ + L₂, L → γ + β, γ → α + β, α + β → μ)

question: Determine the amounts and compositions of each microconstituent in a $\mathrm{Pb}-30 \%$ Sn alloy immediately after the eutectic reaction has been completed. Give your answer as a tuple: (amount_primary_alpha, amount_eutectic)

The intent is to use a hypereutectic Al–Si alloy cast by centrifugation.  
The alloy composition C₀ = 17 wt% Si.  
The eutectic composition C_E = 12.6 wt% Si (and/or the eutectic temperature).  
Using the lever rule, calculate the weight fraction of primary‐β (primary Si) that forms in the bulk alloy.
solution:
Many wear resistant parts are produced from steels, which have a relatively high density, but the hypereutectic Al-Si alloys containing primary $\beta$ may provide the wear resistance that we wish at one-third the weight of the steel.

Since the part to be produced is cylindrical in shape, centrifugal casting (Figure 11-22) might be a unique method for producing it. In centrifugal casting,liquid metal is poured into a rotating mold and the centrifugal force produces a hollow shape. In addition, material that has a high density is spun to the outside wall of the casting, while material that has a lower density than the liquid migrates to the inner wall.

When we centrifugally cast a hypereutectic Al-Si alloy, primary $\beta$ nucleates and grows. The density of the $\beta$ phase (if we assume it to be same as that of pure Si ) is, according to Appendix A, $2.33 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^3$, compared with a density near $2.7 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^3$ for aluminum. As the primary $\beta$ particles precipitate from the liquid, they are spun to the inner surface. The result is a casting that is composed of eutectic microconstituent (with reasonable ductility) at the outer wall and a hypereutectic composition, containing large amounts of primary $\beta$, at the inner wall.

A typical alloy used to produce aluminum engine components is $\mathrm{Al}-17 \% \mathrm{Si}$. From Figure 11-19, the total amount of primary $\beta$ that can form is calculated at $578^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, just above the eutectic temperature:

$$
\% \text { Primary } \beta=\frac{17-12.6}{99.83-12.6} \times 100=5.0 \%
$$


Although only $5.0 \%$ primary $\beta$ is expected to form, the centrifugal action can double or triple the amount of $\beta$ at the inner wall of the casting.

question: Determine the activation energy for the recrystallization of copper from the sigmoidal curves in Figure. Give your answer as a tuple: (activation_energy_cal_per_mol, pre_exponential_factor_min_inverse).
solution:
The rate of transformation is the reciprocal of the time $\tau$ required for half of the transformation to occur. From Figure, the times required for $50 \%$ transformation at several different temperatures can be calculated:
\begin{tabular}{rccc}
\hline$\left.T{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ & $T(\mathrm{~K})$ & $\tau(\mathrm{min})$ & Rate $\left(\mathrm{min}^{-1}\right)$ \\
\hline 135 & 408 & 9 & 0.111 \\
119 & 392 & 22 & 0.045 \\
102 & 375 & 80 & 0.0125 \\
88 & 361 & 250 & 0.0040 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

The rate of transformation is an Arrhenius equation, so a plot of $\ln$ (rate) versus $1 / T$ allows us to calculate the constants in the equation. Taking the natural $\log$ of both sides of Equation (Growth rate $=\mathrm{A} \exp \left(\frac{-Q}{R T}\right)$):

$$
\ln (\text { Growth rate })=\ln A-\frac{Q}{R T}
$$
Thus, if we plot $\ln$ (Growth rate) as a function of $1 / T$, we expect a straight line with a slope of $-Q / R$. A linear regression to the data  which indicates that the slope $-Q / R=-10,494 \mathrm{~K}$. Therefore,

$$
Q=10,494 \mathrm{~K} \times 1.987 \mathrm{cal} /(\mathrm{mol} \cdot \mathrm{~K})=20,852 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{mol}
$$

and the constant A is calculated as

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln \mathrm{A} & =23.59 \\
\mathrm{~A} & =\exp (23.59)=1.76 \times 10^{10} \mathrm{~min}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$


In this particular example, the rate at which the reaction occurs increases as the temperature increases, indicating that the reaction may be dominated by diffusion.

question: Compare the composition of the $\alpha$ solid solution in the $\mathrm{Al}-4 \% \mathrm{Cu}$ alloy at room temperature when the alloy cools under equilibrium conditions with that when the alloy is quenched, based on the aluminum-copper phase diagram (see Figure). Give your answer as a tuple (equilibrium composition, quenched composition).
solution: From the aluminum-copper phase diagram (see Figure), a tie line can be drawn at room temperature under equilibrium conditions. The composition of the $\alpha$ phase determined from the tie line is about $0.02 \% \mathrm{Cu}$. However, when the alloy is quenched, the composition of the $\alpha$ phase remains at $4 \% \mathrm{Cu}$, indicating that the $\alpha$ phase is supersaturated with copper under non-equilibrium conditions.

question:
Given that at the eutectoid temperature the ferrite phase contains 0.0218 %C and the cementite phase (Fe₃C) contains 6.67 %C,  use the lever rule to calculate the weight fractions of ferrite and cementite in pearlite, which has an overall composition of 0.77 %C. 

Give your answer as (ferrite_fraction, cementite_fraction).
solution:
Using the lever rule with the given composition of pearlite ($0.77 \% \mathrm{C}$), the amounts of ferrite ($\alpha$) and cementite ($\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{C}$) can be calculated as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \alpha & =\frac{6.67-0.77}{6.67-0.0218} \times 100=88.7 \% \\
\% \mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{C} & =\frac{0.77-0.0218}{6.67-0.0218} \times 100=11.3 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

This calculation shows that pearlite is composed of approximately 88.7% ferrite and 11.3% cementite. The ferrite phase is relatively soft and ductile, while the cementite phase is hard and brittle, contributing to the dispersion strengthening effect in steels.

question: Calculate the amounts and compositions of phases and microconstituents in a $\mathrm{Fe}-0.60 \% \mathrm{C}$ alloy at $726^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Give your answer as a tuple (amount of proeutectoid alpha, amount of pearlite, composition of proeutectoid alpha, composition of pearlite).
solution:
The phases present in the $\mathrm{Fe}-0.60 \% \mathrm{C}$ alloy at $726^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ are ferrite ($\alpha$) and cementite ($\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{C}$). Using the lever rule at $726^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the amounts of these phases can be calculated as follows:

$$
\begin{gathered}
\alpha(0.0218 \% \mathrm{C}) \quad \% \alpha=\left[\frac{6.67-0.60}{6.67-0.0218}\right] \times 100=91.3 \% \\
\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{C}(6.67 \% \mathrm{C}) \% \mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{C}=\left[\frac{0.60-0.0218}{6.67-0.0218}\right] \times 100=8.7 \%
\end{gathered}
$$

The microconstituents are primary ferrite and pearlite. Constructing a tie line just above $727^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ allows for the calculation of the amounts and compositions of ferrite and austenite just before the eutectoid reaction starts. All of the austenite at that temperature will have the eutectoid composition (i.e., it will contain $0.77 \% \mathrm{C}$) and will transform to pearlite; all of the proeutectoid ferrite will remain as primary ferrite.

$$
\begin{gathered}
\text { Primary } \alpha(0.0218 \% \mathrm{C}) \% \text { Primary } \alpha=\left[\frac{0.77-0.60}{0.77-0.0218}\right] \times 100=22.7 \% \\
\text { Austenite just above } 727^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=\text { Pearlite }: 0.77 \% \mathrm{C} \\
\% \text { Pearlite }=\left[\frac{0.60-0.0218}{0.77-0.0218}\right] \times 100=77.3 \%
\end{gathered}
$$

question:
Unusual combinations of properties can be obtained by producing a steel with a microstructure containing $50 \%$ ferrite and $50 \%$ martensite. The martensite provides strength, and the ferrite provides ductility and toughness. Design a heat treatment to produce a dual phase steel in which the composition of the martensite is $0.60 \% \mathrm{C}$.

**Give your answer as a tuple: (steel_carbon_content, heat_treatment_temperature).**
solution:
To obtain a mixture of ferrite and martensite, we need to heat treat a hypoeutectoid steel into the $\alpha+\gamma$ region of the phase diagram. The steel is then quenched, permitting the $\gamma$ portion of the structure to transform to martensite.

The heat treatment temperature is fixed by the requirement that the martensite contain $0.60 \%$ C. From the solubility line between the $\gamma$ and the $\alpha+\gamma$ regions, we find that $0.60 \% \mathrm{C}$ is obtained in austenite when the temperature is about $750^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. To produce $50 \%$ martensite, we need to select a steel that gives $50 \%$ austenite when the steel is held at $750^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If the carbon content of the steel is $x$, then

$$
\% \gamma=\left[\frac{(x-0.02)}{(0.60-0.02)}\right] \times 100=50 \text { or } x=0.31 \% \mathrm{C}
$$


Our final design is
1. Select a hypoeutectoid steel containing $0.31 \% \mathrm{C}$.
2. Heat the steel to $750^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and hold (perhaps for 1 h , depending on the thickness of the part) to produce a structure containing $50 \%$ ferrite and $50 \%$ austenite, with $0.60 \% \mathrm{C}$ in the austenite.
3. Quench the steel to room temperature. The austenite transforms to martensite, also containing $0.60 \% \mathrm{C}$.

question: An unalloyed steel tool used for machining aluminum automobile wheels has been found to work well, but the purchase records have been lost and you do not know the steel's composition. The microstructure of the steel is tempered martensite. Assume that you cannot estimate the composition of the steel from the structure. Design a treatment that may help determine the steel's carbon content. Give your answer as a tuple (carbon content lower bound, carbon content upper bound).
solution:
Assume that there is no access to equipment that would permit you to analyze the chemical composition directly. Since the entire structure of the steel is a very fine tempered martensite, we can do a simple heat treatment to produce a structure that can be analyzed more easily. This can be done in two different ways.

The first way is to heat the steel to a temperature just below the $A_{1}$ temperature and hold for a long time. The steel overtempers and large $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{C}$ spheres form in a ferrite matrix. We then estimate the amount of ferrite and cementite and calculate the carbon content using the lever law. If we measure $16 \% \mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{C}$ using this method, the carbon content is

$$
\% \mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{C}=\left[\frac{(x-0.0218)}{(6.67-0.0218)}\right] \times 100=16 \text { or } x=1.086 \% \mathrm{C}
$$

A better approach, however, is to heat the steel above the $A_{\mathrm{cm}}$ to produce all austenite. If the steel then cools slowly, it transforms to pearlite and a primary microconstituent. If, when we do this, we estimate that the structure contains $95 \%$ pearlite and $5 \%$ primary $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{C}$, then

$$
\% \text { Pearlite }=\left[\frac{(6.67-x)}{(6.67-0.77)}\right] \times 100=95 \text { or } x=1.065 \% \mathrm{C}
$$

The carbon content is on the order of 1.065 to $1.086 \%$, consistent with a 10110 steel. In this procedure, we assumed that the weight and volume percentages of the microconstituents are the same, which is nearly the case in steels.

question:
Design of a Heat Treatment for an Axle. A heat treatment is needed to produce a uniform microstructure and hardness of HRC 23 in a 1050 steel axle.

Give your answer as a tuple: (austenitizing temperature min, austenitizing temperature max, quenching temperature, primary ferrite percentage, pearlite percentage).
solution:
We might attempt this task in several ways. We could austenitize the steel, then cool at an appropriate rate by annealing or normalizing to obtain the correct hardness. By doing this, however, we find that the structure and hardness vary from the surface to the center of the axle.

A better approach is to use an isothermal heat treatment. From Figure 13-7, we find that a hardness of HRC 23 is obtained by transforming austenite to a mixture of ferrite and pearlite at $600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. From Figure 13-1, we find that the $A_3$ temperature is $770^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Therefore, our heat treatment is
1. Austenitize the steel at $770+(30$ to 55$)=800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $825^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, holding for 1 h and obtaining $100 \% \gamma$.
2. Quench the steel to $600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and hold for a minimum of 10 s . Primary ferrite begins to precipitate from the unstable austenite after about 1.0 s . After 1.5 s , pearlite begins to grow, and the austenite is completely transformed to ferrite and pearlite after about 10 s . After this treatment, the microconstituents present are

$$
\begin{gathered}
\text { Primary } \alpha=\left[\frac{(0.77-0.5)}{(0.77-0.218)}\right] \times 100=36 \% \\
\text { Pearlite }=\left[\frac{(0.5-0.0218)}{(0.77-0.0218)}\right] \times 100=64 \%
\end{gathered}
$$

3. Cool in air to room temperature, preserving the equilibrium amounts of primary ferrite and pearlite. The microstructure and hardness are uniform because of the isothermal anneal.

question: Design of a Quench and Temper Treatment. A rotating shaft that delivers power from an electric motor is made from a 1050 steel. Its yield strength should be at least $145,000 \mathrm{psi}$, yet it should also have at least $15 \%$ elongation in order to provide toughness. Design a heat treatment to produce this part. **Give your answer as a tuple: (austenitizing_temperature_C, tempering_temperature_C).**
solution:
We are not able to obtain this combination of properties by annealing or normalizing (Figure 13-4); however, a quench and temper heat treatment produces a microstructure that can provide both strength and toughness. Figure 13-9 shows that the yield strength exceeds 145,000 psi if the steel is tempered below 460°C, whereas the elongation exceeds 15% if tempering is done above 425°C. The A3 temperature for the steel is 770°C. A possible heat treatment is

1. Austenitize above the A3 temperature of 770°C for 1 h. An appropriate temperature may be 770+55=825°C.
2. Quench rapidly to room temperature. Since the Mf is about 250°C, martensite will form.
3. Temper by heating the steel to 440°C. Normally, 1 h will be sufficient if the steel is not too thick.
4. Cool to room temperature.

question:
A steel cable 0.5 in. in diameter has a yield strength of 70,000 psi. The density of steel is about $7.87 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}$. Based on the data in Table, determine (a) the maximum load that the steel cable can support without yielding, (b) the diameter of a cold-worked aluminum-manganese alloy (3004-H18) required to support the same load as the steel, and (c) the weight per foot of the steel cable versus the aluminum alloy cable. The yield strength of the aluminum alloy is $36,000 \mathrm{psi}$ and its density is $2.70 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}$. Give your answer as a tuple (maximum load without yielding in lb, diameter of aluminum alloy in in, weight per foot of steel cable in lb, weight per foot of aluminum alloy cable in lb).

Table:

\hline \multicolumn{6}{|l|}{\begin{tabular}{l}

\hline 3004-H18 & & 41,000 & 36,000 & 9 & architectural uses, \\
\hline 4043-0 & 5.2\% Si & 21,000 & 10,000 & 22 & filler metal for welding, \\

\hline 5182-0 & 4.5\% Mg & 42,000 & 19,000 & 25 & marine components \\
\hline 5182-H19 & & 61,000 & 57,000 & 4 & \\
